# Module 1: Fluid Pressure — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define fluid pressure. State Pascal's law. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Fluid Pressure:** Fluid pressure is the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a surface in contact with it. It is given by $P = F/A$ and is measured in **Pascals (Pa)**.

**Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the container.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of Fluid Pressure:**
> 1. It is a **scalar quantity**.
> 2. It always acts **normal** to the contact surface.
> 3. It is **isotropic** — same in all directions at a point.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between absolute pressure and gauge pressure. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$) | Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Reference** | Measured with respect to **perfect vacuum (zero)** | Measured with respect to **local atmospheric pressure** |
| **Value** | Always **positive** (or zero) | Can be **positive, zero, or negative** |
| **Relation** | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| **Example** | Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa (absolute) | Tyre pressure gauge reads ~30 psi (gauge) |
| **Sensor** | Barometer | Bourdon gauge, manometer |

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for hydrostatic pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a static incompressible fluid of density $\rho$. List the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A U-tube manometer containing mercury is connected to a gas pipeline. The difference in mercury levels in the two limbs is 30 cm. Calculate the gauge pressure and absolute pressure of the gas. Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³ and atmospheric pressure = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation:**

Consider a small fluid element in the form of a thin horizontal slab of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dh$ at a depth $h$ from the free surface of a static incompressible fluid of density $\rho$.

**Forces on the element:**

1. Pressure force on the top face (acting downward) = $P \cdot A$
2. Pressure force on the bottom face (acting upward) = $(P + dP) \cdot A$
3. Weight of the element (acting downward) = $dm \cdot g = (\rho A dh) g$

**Equilibrium condition (static fluid, no acceleration):**

$$(P + dP) \cdot A - P \cdot A - \rho A g \, dh = 0$$

**Simplification:**

$$dP \cdot A = \rho g A \, dh$$

$$dP = \rho g \, dh$$

**Integration** from the free surface (where pressure = $P_0$ and depth = 0) to depth $h$ (where pressure = $P$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh$$

$$P - P_0 = \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Fluid is **incompressible** (density $\rho$ is constant)
2. Fluid is **static** (no motion)
3. Gravity is **uniform** ($g$ = 9.81 m/s² constant)
4. Container is open to the atmosphere (or $P_0$ is known)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given data:**
- Mercury density $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³
- Height difference $h = 30$ cm = 0.30 m
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1:** Apply the manometer principle. The gauge pressure of the gas is equal to the hydrostatic pressure of the mercury column difference:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$P_{gauge} = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.30$$

$$P_{gauge} = 40024.8 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.02 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Compute absolute pressure:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

$$P_{abs} = 40024.8 + 1.013 \times 10^5$$

$$P_{abs} = 40024.8 + 101300 = 141324.8 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} \approx 141.32 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $h$ from **cm to m** (a frequent unit error costing 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing gauge and absolute pressure — the question asks for **both**, so don't skip either.
> 3. Using $g = 10$ m/s² (KTU expects 9.81 unless specified).
> 4. **NOT showing the integration step** in the derivation — KTU examiners award full marks (7/7) only when the equilibrium equation and integration are explicitly written.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain Pascal's law. With a neat sketch, describe the working of a hydraulic lift and derive the expression for force amplification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a small piston of radius 4 cm and a large piston of radius 40 cm. A force of 200 N is applied on the small piston. Calculate: (i) the pressure transmitted through the fluid, (ii) the force exerted on the large piston, (iii) the mechanical advantage of the press. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Statement of Pascal's Law:**

*"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel."*

**Explanation:**

When pressure is applied at any point in a confined fluid, every molecule of the fluid transmits that pressure to its neighbours. As a result, the pressure at every point in the fluid increases by the same amount. This is why hydraulic systems can transmit force from one location to another without any loss in magnitude.

**Working of Hydraulic Lift:**

A hydraulic lift consists of two cylinders of different cross-sectional areas connected at the bottom through a pipe filled with incompressible hydraulic oil. A small piston fits in the smaller cylinder, and a larger piston (carrying the load platform) fits in the larger cylinder. When a small downward force is applied on the small piston, it creates pressure in the fluid. This pressure is transmitted equally to the large piston, which moves up and lifts the load placed on it.

**Derivation of Force Amplification:**

Let:
- $A_1$ = Area of small piston, $A_2$ = Area of large piston
- $F_1$ = Force applied on small piston, $F_2$ = Force on large piston
- $P$ = Pressure transmitted through the fluid

Pressure on small piston:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

By Pascal's law, this pressure is transmitted undiminished to the large piston. The force on the large piston is:

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_2} \cdot A_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Mechanical Advantage} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given data:**
- Radius of small piston $r_1 = 4$ cm = 0.04 m
- Radius of large piston $r_2 = 40$ cm = 0.40 m
- Applied force $F_1 = 200$ N
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1:** Compute the areas:

$$A_1 = \pi r_1^2 = \pi (0.04)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \pi r_2^2 = \pi (0.40)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (i):** Pressure transmitted through the fluid:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 39788.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 39.79 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Force on the large piston:

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 39788.7 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-1}$$

$$F_2 = 20000 \text{ N} = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 (iii):** Mechanical Advantage:

$$MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{20000}{200} = 100$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$MA = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{\pi (0.40)^2}{\pi (0.04)^2} = \frac{0.16}{0.0016} = 100 \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Hydraulic Press Problems:**
> 1. **Using diameter instead of radius** to compute area — this introduces a factor-of-4 error.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert cm to m** before calculating — a single unit slip loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up area ratio direction** — a larger output area gives a larger output force, NOT smaller.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** — KTU 2024 scheme requires a "neat sketch" for full marks on descriptive questions (carry 1–2 marks from the presentation rubric).
> 5. **Not stating Pascal's law explicitly** before the derivation — examiner may deduct 1 mark for missing the formal statement.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fluid Pressure**

- ☐ Fluid pressure is a **scalar** quantity with units of **Pa (N/m²)**.
- ☐ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure in an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — valid only for **incompressible, static** fluids.
- ☐ **Force amplification** in hydraulic systems: $F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1$.
- ☐ **Absolute pressure = Gauge pressure + Atmospheric pressure.**
- ☐ **Manometer equation:** $P_{gauge} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ (mercury density = 13600 kg/m³).
- ☐ **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.7 psi.
- ☐ **Always convert units to SI** (metres, kg, seconds) before substituting into equations.
- ☐ **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless the problem states otherwise.
- ☐ Depth $h$ is **vertical**, not along the slope of an inclined surface.
- ☐ In KTU 2024 mechatronics context, fluid pressure is a key **measurand** for sensors (piezoresistive, capacitive, strain-gauge, Bourdon tube).
- ☐ Pressure sensors convert mechanical energy → electrical signal, enabling **closed-loop control** in mechatronic systems.
- ☐ **Bernoulli's equation** (for moving fluids): $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ — used in flow-based pressure sensing.
- ☐ **Bulk modulus** $K$ of hydraulic fluid must be high (low compressibility) for efficient force transmission.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Flow Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquid Flow Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Liquid Flow Sensors

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

A **Liquid Flow Sensor** (or flowmeter) is a mechatronic instrumentation device used to measure the **volumetric flow rate** (m³/s) or **mass flow rate** (kg/s) of a liquid flowing through a pipe or open channel. The output may be an analog signal (voltage/current), a frequency signal, or a digital display proportional to the flow.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, flow sensors are categorized based on their **operating principle**: differential-pressure, variable-area, positive-displacement, velocity-based, and mass-flow based devices. They form the sensing backbone of process automation in industries such as petrochemical, water treatment, pharmaceutical, and automotive fuel systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to classify flow sensors, derive governing equations, and solve numerical problems on **Orifice plate, Venturi, Turbine, Electromagnetic, Vortex, Ultrasonic, and Coriolis** flow meters.

**Key Standard Metrics (SI Units):**

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | m³/s |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | kg/s |
| Fluid velocity | $v$ | m/s |
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s |
| Pressure difference | $\Delta P$ | Pa |
| Reynolds number | $Re$ | dimensionless |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Highway Toll Booth Model":**
> Imagine cars flowing on a single-lane highway. To know how many cars pass per minute, you could:
> 1. **Count each car** as it crosses a gate (Positive Displacement meter).
> 2. **Spin a propeller** in the lane — the faster cars pass, the faster it spins (Turbine meter).
> 3. **Push water through a narrow choke** — a pressure drop builds up, and that pressure drop tells you how fast cars are moving (Orifice / Venturi).
> 4. **Apply a magnetic field** across the road and measure the EMF induced by the moving conductive "cars" (Electromagnetic).
> 5. **Twist the pipe** back and forth and watch how the "cars" resist the twist (Coriolis mass flow).

In all cases, the flow rate $Q$ is the fundamental quantity, related to **pipe cross-section area** $A$ and **average fluid velocity** $v$ by the simple relation $Q = A \cdot v$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Liquid Flow Sensors

### 2.1.1 Differential-Pressure Flow Meters
Operate on **Bernoulli's principle**: as fluid accelerates through a constriction, its static pressure decreases. The pressure drop $\Delta P$ is measured and correlated to flow rate.

- **Orifice Plate:** A thin plate with a concentric bore. Cheap, widely used.
- **Venturi Meter:** Smooth converging–diverging nozzle, low permanent pressure loss.
- **Flow Nozzle:** A profiled restriction, more robust than orifice.
- **Pitot Tube:** Measures stagnation vs. static pressure to give **point velocity**.

### 2.1.2 Variable-Area Flow Meters (Rotameter)
A float rises inside a tapered vertical tube until the upward drag force balances its weight. The float height is proportional to flow rate.

### 2.1.3 Positive-Displacement Flow Meters
Capture and discharge fixed volumes of liquid repeatedly. Examples: **Oval gear, Nutating disc, Reciprocating piston**.

### 2.1.4 Velocity-Based Flow Meters
- **Turbine Meter:** Rotor blades rotate at speed proportional to flow; output is a pulse train whose frequency $f \propto Q$.
- **Electromagnetic (Magmeter):** Faraday's law — EMF induced is proportional to conductive liquid velocity.
- **Ultrasonic (Transit-Time):** Measures difference in upstream/downstream sound travel times.
- **Vortex Meter:** A bluff body sheds Kármán vortices at a frequency given by the Strouhal number.

### 2.1.5 Mass Flow Meters
- **Coriolis Meter:** The "gold standard" — measures mass flow directly, independent of fluid properties.
- **Thermal Mass Meter:** Heat transfer from a heated element is proportional to mass flow.

## 2.2 Why Each Principle Works — The Core Logic

- **Bernoulli's principle** conserves energy between upstream (1) and downstream (2) sections:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

- **Continuity equation** conserves mass: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$.
- **Faraday's law** of electromagnetic induction provides a non-intrusive way to sense velocity.
- **Coriolis effect** couples mass with motion in an oscillating reference frame — the only way to measure mass flow directly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as your last-night revision sheet.** Every equation below has appeared in KTU question papers between **2019 and 2024**.

| # | Flow Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $A$ = m², $v$ = m/s, $Q$ = m³/s |
| 2 | General mass | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | $\rho$ = kg/m³ |
| 3 | Orifice plate | $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $\beta = d/D$, $C_d$ = discharge coeff. |
| 4 | Venturi meter | $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Same form as orifice |
| 5 | Pitot tube | $v = \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | Stagnation vs static pressure |
| 6 | Rotameter | $Q = K \cdot h$ | $h$ = float height |
| 7 | Turbine meter | $f = K \cdot Q$ | $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz) |
| 8 | Electromagnetic | $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$ | $E$ = V, $B$ = T, $D$ = pipe diameter (m) |
| 9 | Electromagnetic Q | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D}{4 B} \cdot E$ | Derived from (8) |
| 10 | Vortex shedding | $f = S_t \cdot v / d$ | $S_t \approx 0.2$, $d$ = bluff body width |
| 11 | Coriolis mass | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{F_c}{2 \omega r}$ | $F_c$ = Coriolis force, $\omega$ = ang. vel. |
| 12 | Ultrasonic transit-time | $v = \dfrac{(t_2 - t_1) L^2}{2 L \cos\theta \cdot t_1 t_2}$ | $\theta$ = beam angle to flow |
| 13 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Laminar if $Re < 2300$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The electromagnetic flowmeter is the **only** practical non-intrusive meter for slurries, sewage, and conductive chemicals. The Coriolis meter is the standard for custody transfer of crude oil, milk, and LPG, where mass accuracy below **0.1 %** is mandatory.

## 2.4 Discharge Coefficient and β-Ratio

For differential-pressure meters, the theoretical equation over-predicts flow because of viscous effects and vena-contracta contraction. The **discharge coefficient** $C_d$ (typically 0.6 – 0.98) corrects this, while the **diameter ratio** $\beta = d/D$ captures geometric effects.

- For a venturi: $C_d \approx 0.98$ (smooth flow, low loss).
- For an orifice: $C_d \approx 0.62$ (vena-contracta effect).
- For a Pitot tube: $C_d \approx 1.0$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Orifice Plate Volumetric Flow Equation

**Setup:** A horizontal pipe of diameter $D$ carries an incompressible liquid (density $\rho$). An orifice plate of diameter $d$ creates a vena contracta at section 2 with effective area $A_2$.

**Apply Bernoulli between section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (vena contracta):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}$$

**Apply continuity (mass conservation):**

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v_1 = v_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Substitute $v_1$ in Bernoulli:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{1}{2}\left(v_2 \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}$$

**Rearrange to isolate $v_2^2$:**

$$v_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right] = \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}$$

$$v_2^2 \left[1 - \beta^4\right] = \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}, \quad \text{where } \beta = \frac{d}{D}$$

**Solve for $v_2$:**

$$v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Apply the discharge coefficient $C_d$** (empirical correction for viscosity and contraction):

$$\boxed{\;Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electromagnetic Flow Meter (Faraday's Law)

**Setup:** Conductive liquid flows with velocity $v$ through a pipe of inner diameter $D$ immersed in a transverse magnetic field of flux density $B$.

**Faraday's Law (motional EMF):**

$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

where $L$ is the effective conductor length = pipe diameter $D$.

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

**Solve for velocity:**

$$v = \frac{E}{B \cdot D}$$

**Convert velocity to volumetric flow rate** using $Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{E}{B D} = \frac{\pi D}{4 B} \cdot E$$

$$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{\pi D}{4 B} \cdot E\;}$$

**Conclusion:** $Q$ is independent of fluid density, viscosity, and turbulence — making the magmeter ideal for slurries and aggressive chemicals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coriolis Mass Flow Meter

**Setup:** Liquid of mass $m$ flows through a U-shaped tube oscillating at angular velocity $\omega$. The tube has effective radius $r$ from the axis of oscillation.

**Newton's 2nd law in the rotating frame:**

$$F_c = m \cdot a_c, \quad \text{where } a_c = 2 \omega v$$

(Acceleration arises because in the rotating frame, fluid particles experience a Coriolis acceleration perpendicular to both $\omega$ and $v$.)

**Mass per unit time entering = $\dot{m}$. The Coriolis force developed is:**

$$F_c = 2 \dot{m} \, \omega \, r$$

**Measure tube deflection $\Delta x$** — proportional to $F_c$. Knowing $\omega$ and $r$, $\dot{m}$ is found directly.

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m} = \frac{F_c}{2 \omega r}\;}$$

**Engineering insight:** Because the equation contains $\dot{m}$ directly (not $Q$ or $\rho$), the meter is insensitive to fluid density variations — perfect for measuring crude oil (where $\rho$ changes with temperature).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vortex Shedding Frequency

**Setup:** Fluid of velocity $v$ passes a bluff body of width $d$. Kármán vortices shed at frequency $f$.

**Define the dimensionless Strouhal number:**

$$S_t = \frac{f \cdot d}{v}$$

**Empirically, for cylindrical bluff bodies, $S_t \approx 0.2$ over a wide Reynolds range:**

$$\boxed{\;f = \frac{S_t \cdot v}{d}\;}$$

The pulse frequency $f$ is sensed by a piezoelectric pressure transducer behind the bluff body, giving $v$ and hence $Q$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Orifice | Venturi | Turbine | Electromagnetic | Vortex | Coriolis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±2 % | ±1 % | ±0.5 % | ±0.5 % | ±1 % | ±0.1 % |
| Pressure loss | High | Low | Medium | None | Medium | Medium |
| Intrusive? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| Works on dirty fluids? | Limited | Limited | Poor | **Yes** | Limited | Yes |
| Measures mass flow? | No | No | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Typical cost | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Classification of Liquid Flow Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Flow Sensor] --> B[Differential Pressure Type]
    A --> C[Variable Area Type]
    A --> D[Positive Displacement Type]
    A --> E[Velocity Based Type]
    A --> F[Mass Flow Type]
    B --> B1[Orifice Plate]
    B --> B2[Venturi Meter]
    B --> B3[Flow Nozzle]
    B --> B4[Pitot Tube]
    C --> C1[Rotameter]
    D --> D1[Oval Gear Meter]
    D --> D2[Nutating Disc Meter]
    D --> D3[Reciprocating Piston]
    E --> E1[Turbine Meter]
    E --> E2[Electromagnetic Magmeter]
    E --> E3[Ultrasonic Transit Time]
    E --> E4[Vortex Shedding Meter]
    F --> F1[Coriolis Mass Flow Meter]
    F --> F2[Thermal Mass Flow Meter]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Electromagnetic Flow Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[DC Power Supply] --> MC[Magnetic Field Coils]
    MC --> CV[Conductive Liquid in Pipe]
    CV -- Induced EMF --> EL[Electrodes on Pipe Wall]
    EL --> AMP[Low Noise Differential Amplifier]
    AMP --> FIL[Analog Low Pass Filter]
    FIL --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller DSP]
    MCU --> DISP[Digital Display L per min]
    MCU --> OUT[4 to 20 mA Current Loop Output]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Coriolis Mass Flow Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DRV[Drive Coil] --> TU[U Tube Sensor Flow Tube]
    TU -- Oscillates --> PZ1[Pickoff Sensor 1 Inlet Leg]
    TU -- Oscillates --> PZ2[Pickoff Sensor 2 Outlet Leg]
    PZ1 --> SIG[Signal Conditioning]
    PZ2 --> SIG
    SIG --> PHASE[Phase Difference Detector]
    PHASE --> MASS[Mass Flow Output]
    SIG --> FREQ[Frequency Measurement]
    FREQ --> DENS[Density Output]
    MASS --> DISP[Display kg per hour]
    DENS --> DISP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** In the Coriolis diagram, the **phase difference** between Pickoff 1 and Pickoff 2 is the key measurand — it is proportional to mass flow. The **frequency** of oscillation is inversely proportional to fluid density, giving a bonus density output for free.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Orifice Plate Flow Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIPE[Pipe Carrying Liquid] --> ORIF[Orifice Plate Restriction]
    ORIF --> TAP1[High Pressure Tap Upstream]
    ORIF --> TAP2[Low Pressure Tap Downstream]
    TAP1 --> DP[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    TAP2 --> DP
    DP --> SIG[Signal Conditioner 4 to 20 mA]
    SIG --> SCADA[PLC SCADA DCS Controller]
    SCADA --> VALVE[Control Valve Actuator]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the following terms with their SI units: (i) Volumetric flow rate, (ii) Mass flow rate, (iii) Reynolds number.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$):** The volume of liquid passing through a cross-section per unit time.

$$Q = A \cdot v \quad \text{[unit: m}^3/\text{s]}$$

- **(ii) Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$):** The mass of liquid passing through a cross-section per unit time.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q \quad \text{[unit: kg/s]}$$

- **(iii) Reynolds Number ($Re$):** A dimensionless number that quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces and predicts whether flow is laminar or turbulent.

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

Flow is **laminar** for $Re < 2300$ and **turbulent** for $Re > 4000$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between intrusive and non-intrusive flow sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Intrusive | Non-Intrusive |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sensor elements protrude into the flow stream | Sensor elements stay outside the pipe |
| Pressure drop | Creates additional pressure loss | Negligible pressure loss |
| Maintenance | Requires shutdown for cleaning/replacement | Can be serviced without shutdown |
| Suitable for | Clean fluids, slurries avoided | Clean, dirty, corrosive, sterile fluids |
| Cost & accuracy | Generally cheaper, high accuracy | Higher cost, very high accuracy |
| Examples | Orifice plate, Turbine, Venturi, Rotameter, Coriolis | Electromagnetic (clamp-on or inline), Ultrasonic (clamp-on) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Alternative 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of an electromagnetic flow meter with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for volumetric flow rate in terms of the induced EMF.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** An electromagnetic flow meter (magmeter) operates on **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**. When a conductive liquid flows through a transverse magnetic field, an EMF is induced across the liquid column, perpendicular to both the flow direction and the magnetic field.

**Construction:**
- A non-magnetic, non-conductive pipe (often lined with PTFE or rubber for chemical resistance).
- Two electromagnetic coils placed diametrically opposite on the outside of the pipe, energized by a DC or pulsed DC current to produce a uniform transverse magnetic field $B$.
- Two electrodes mounted flush with the inner pipe wall, perpendicular to both the flow and the field, to tap off the induced EMF.

**Working:**
The conductive liquid acts as a moving conductor of length $L = D$ (pipe diameter) moving with velocity $v$ in field $B$.

The motional EMF generated is:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

The induced voltage is detected by the electrodes, amplified, filtered, and converted to a 4–20 mA signal proportional to volumetric flow.

**Derivation:**

$$v = \frac{E}{B D}$$

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{E}{B D} = \boxed{\frac{\pi D}{4 B} \cdot E}$$

Since $Q \propto E$, the output is linear, and the meter reading is **independent of fluid density, viscosity, temperature, and turbulence**.

**Advantages:** No moving parts, no pressure drop, suitable for slurries and corrosive liquids, bidirectional.

**Limitations:** Requires minimum electrical conductivity of the liquid (typically $> 5\;\mu\text{S/cm}$); cannot measure hydrocarbons, oils, or gases.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A venturi meter has an inlet diameter of 20 cm and a throat diameter of 10 cm. It is fitted in a horizontal pipe carrying water of density 1000 kg/m³. The differential pressure recorded is 5 kPa. If the discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.98$, calculate the volumetric flow rate.**

**Given:**

- $D = 0.20$ m, $d = 0.10$ m
- $\rho = 1000\;\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta P = 5\;\text{kPa} = 5000\;\text{Pa}$
- $C_d = 0.98$

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.20)^2}{4} = 0.03142\;\text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 0.007854\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Diameter ratio:**

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.50$$

$$\beta^4 = 0.0625$$

$$1 - \beta^4 = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Apply Venturi formula:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot \frac{0.007854}{\sqrt{0.9375}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000}{1000}}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot \frac{0.007854}{0.9683} \cdot \sqrt{10}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \cdot 0.008112 \cdot 3.1623$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0251\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 25.1\;\text{L/s}}$$

---

### Question B — Alternative 2 [KTU University Exam — July 2022]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of a Coriolis mass flow meter with a suitable diagram. List its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A Coriolis mass flow meter operates on the **Coriolis effect**. Liquid flowing through an oscillating (vibrating) tube experiences a Coriolis acceleration in the rotating reference frame of the tube, producing a Coriolis force perpendicular to both the flow direction and the oscillation.

**Construction:**
- A sensor tube (U-shaped, straight, or delta-shaped) is oscillated at its natural frequency (typically 80–1000 Hz) by an electromagnetic drive coil.
- Two electromagnetic pickoff sensors measure the deflection/timing of the tube at the inlet and outlet legs.

**Working — Step by Step:**

1. The drive coil excites the tube into its primary mode of vibration.
2. As fluid enters and exits the tube, **Coriolis forces** deflect the two legs of the tube in opposite directions (the inlet leg pushes back, the outlet leg pushes forward).
3. The resulting **phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ between the inlet and outlet pickoff signals is **directly proportional to mass flow rate**.

$$\dot{m} = \frac{F_c}{2 \omega r} \;\;\propto\;\; \Delta\phi$$

4. The **oscillation frequency** is inversely related to total mass (fluid + tube). Since tube mass is constant, frequency variations yield fluid **density**.

**Advantages:**

- Measures **mass flow directly** (no need for density or volumetric compensation).
- Accuracy up to **±0.1 %** — the highest of all flow meters.
- Independent of fluid properties (density, viscosity, conductivity).
- Measures multiple variables: mass flow, density, temperature.
- Suitable for liquids, slurries, gases, and even corrosive fluids.

**Limitations:**

- Very high capital cost.
- Sensitive to external vibration and pipe mounting.
- Not recommended for very low flow rates.
- Pressure drop higher than magmeter; sensitive to entrained gas.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An orifice plate with 5 cm bore is fitted in a 15 cm diameter pipe carrying water. The differential pressure across the orifice is 10 kPa. Calculate the flow rate assuming $C_d = 0.62$. Take $\rho = 1000\;\text{kg/m}^3$.**

**Given:**

- $D = 0.15$ m, $d = 0.05$ m
- $\Delta P = 10\;\text{kPa} = 10{,}000\;\text{Pa}$
- $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho = 1000\;\text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767\;\text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 0.001963\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Diameter ratio:**

$$\beta = \frac{0.05}{0.15} = 0.3333$$

$$\beta^4 = 0.01235$$

$$1 - \beta^4 = 0.98765$$

**Step 3 — Velocity at vena contracta (theoretical):**

$$v_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 / 1000}}{\sqrt{0.98765}}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{\sqrt{20}}{0.99381} = \frac{4.4721}{0.99381} \approx 4.500\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply discharge coefficient:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot v_2 = 0.62 \cdot 0.001963 \cdot 4.500$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 5.48 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 5.48\;\text{L/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert cm → m, kPa → Pa, L → m³. Marks deducted systematically.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_d$:** Writing the theoretical formula without the discharge coefficient gives the ideal flow, not actual — typically 30–40 % error. Examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong $\beta$ power:** Orifice equation uses $\beta^4$, NOT $\beta^2$. This is the single most common error.
> 4. **Mixing up Coriolis principle:** Many students write "Coriolis flow meter uses Bernoulli's principle" — it does NOT; it uses the Coriolis effect in a rotating/oscillating frame.
> 5. **Magmeter misconception:** Stating that an electromagnetic flowmeter can measure oil or gas — it CANNOT, because the fluid must be electrically conductive.
> 6. **Skipping units in final answer:** Numerical problems must end with correct units (m³/s or L/s) to fetch the final mark.
> 7. **No diagram in long answers:** A neat labelled block diagram carries 1–2 marks. Skipping it costs at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Liquid flow sensors measure **volumetric flow $Q$** or **mass flow $\dot{m}$**.
- Master equation: $Q = A \cdot v$; mass flow: $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.
- **Differential-pressure meters** (orifice, Venturi, nozzle, Pitot) are governed by **Bernoulli's equation** + continuity, with $C_d$ correction.
- **Orifice formula:** $Q = C_d \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho}}$ — remember $\beta^4$, not $\beta^2$.
- **Venturi** has $C_d \approx 0.98$ and low pressure loss; **orifice** has $C_d \approx 0.62$ but is cheap.
- **Turbine meters** give frequency output: $f = K \cdot Q$.
- **Electromagnetic flow meter** uses Faraday's law: $E = B D v$, giving $Q = \dfrac{\pi D}{4B} E$. Requires conductive fluid.
- **Vortex meter** uses Strouhal relation: $f = S_t \cdot v / d$ with $S_t \approx 0.2$.
- **Coriolis meter** is the only direct **mass flow** meter; measures phase difference in oscillating tube.
- **Ultrasonic** is non-intrusive, ideal for clean fluids and large pipes.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ dictates laminar vs. turbulent flow.
- **Intrusive** meters cause pressure loss; **non-intrusive** (mag, ultrasonic) do not.
- **Selection criteria:** fluid conductivity, viscosity, cleanliness, accuracy, pressure drop tolerance, and budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "BEVU-TC"** for the six main types:
> **B**ernoulli (orifice, Venturi) · **E**lectromagnetic · **V**ortex · **U**ltrasonic · **T**urbine · **C**oriolis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature ($T$)** is a fundamental scalar physical quantity that quantitatively expresses the degree of *hotness* or *coldness* of a body or system. From a thermodynamic perspective, it is the intensive property that governs the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two systems placed in thermal contact. At the microscopic level, temperature is directly proportional to the **average kinetic energy of the random translational motion of the constituent particles** of a system, as established by the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**, which forms the axiomatic basis of thermometry.

The **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** (the foundational law for temperature measurement) states:

> *"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."*

This law is the philosophical bedrock of all temperature measurement, because it allows the existence of a **thermometric property** — a measurable physical quantity (such as the length of a mercury column, electrical resistance, or junction voltage) that varies **monotonically and reproducibly** with temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Temperature):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme expects the student to master: (1) Temperature scales and their inter-conversions, (2) Laws of thermometry and zeroth law, (3) Thermal expansion — linear, areal, and volumetric, (4) Bimetallic strip principle, (5) Thermocouples (Seebeck, Peltier, Thomson effects), (6) Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs), (7) Thermistors (NTC and PTC), (8) Semiconductor and IC temperature sensors, and (9) Radiation pyrometry basics (Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's laws).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine standing on a beach on a hot summer day. The sand is scorching, the air is warm, and the sea water is comparatively cool. Now imagine you have three thermometers — one pressed against the sand, one hanging in the air, and one dipped into the sea. Each thermometer gives a different reading, yet they all read **temperature** — the same physical quantity.

The intuitive picture is this:
- **Heat** is the *energy in transit* — like money being transferred between bank accounts.
- **Temperature** is the *level of thermal agitation* of the molecules — like the *account balance* itself.
- Two objects at the same temperature will exchange **no net heat** even when in contact (thermal equilibrium).
- A hotter object (higher $T$) will spontaneously transfer heat to a colder one (lower $T$) until equilibrium is reached.

In a mechatronic sensor, this "level of thermal agitation" is transduced into a measurable signal:
- The **expansion of mercury or alcohol** → mechanical displacement
- The **change in electrical resistance** of a metal wire → electrical signal (RTD)
- The **generation of a thermoelectric voltage** at a junction of dissimilar metals → electrical mV signal (Thermocouple)
- The **change in semiconductor junction voltage** with $T$ → analog voltage (IC sensor)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard Reference Temperature (Triple Point of Water)**: $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K (exact, by SI definition)
- **Absolute Zero**: $T = 0$ K $= -273.15^\circ$C (unattainable; the limit of thermal energy)
- **Normal Boiling Point of Water (at 1 atm)**: $T_b = 100^\circ$C $= 373.15$ K
- **Normal Freezing/Melting Point of Water (at 1 atm)**: $T_f = 0^\circ$C $= 273.15$ K
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant**: $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m$^{-2}$·K$^{-4}$
- **Wien's Displacement Constant**: $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K
- **Boltzmann Constant**: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

> [!NOTE]
> In SI, the **kelvin (K)** is the base unit of thermodynamic temperature, defined as $\frac{1}{273.16}$ of the thermodynamic temperature of the triple point of water. The Celsius scale is defined as $t(^\circ\text{C}) = T(\text{K}) - 273.15$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Temperature Scales and Their Inter-Relationship

The KTU 2024 syllabus lays strong emphasis on scale conversions. The four principal scales encountered in sensor engineering are:

| Scale | Symbol | Lower Fixed Point | Upper Fixed Point | Fundamental Nature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Celsius | $^\circ$C | $0^\circ$C (ice point) | $100^\circ$C (steam point) | Centigrade, relative |
| Fahrenheit | $^\circ$F | $32^\circ$F | $212^\circ$F | Used in US engineering |
| Kelvin | K | $273.15$ K | $373.15$ K | **Absolute (thermodynamic)** |
| Rankine | $^\circ$R | $491.67^\circ$R | $671.67^\circ$R | Absolute (imperial) |

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Scale Conversions

| From $\rightarrow$ To | Conversion Formula | Inverse Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $^\circ$C $\to$ K | $T_K = t_{^\circ\text{C}} + 273.15$ | $t_{^\circ\text{C}} = T_K - 273.15$ |
| $^\circ$C $\to$ $^\circ$F | $t_{^\circ\text{F}} = \dfrac{9}{5}\,t_{^\circ\text{C}} + 32$ | $t_{^\circ\text{C}} = \dfrac{5}{9}(t_{^\circ\text{F}} - 32)$ |
| K $\to$ $^\circ$R | $T_R = \dfrac{9}{5}\,T_K$ | $T_K = \dfrac{5}{9}\,T_R$ |
| $^\circ$F $\to$ $^\circ$R | $T_R = t_{^\circ\text{F}} + 459.67$ | $t_{^\circ\text{F}} = T_R - 459.67$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Linear Interpolation Rule:** Any two linear temperature scales $X$ and $Y$ are related by:
> $$\frac{X - X_{\text{lower}}}{X_{\text{upper}} - X_{\text{lower}}} = \frac{Y - Y_{\text{lower}}}{Y_{\text{upper}} - Y_{\text{lower}}}$$
> This single equation handles *all* inter-scale conversions when the fixed points are known.

## 2.2 Laws of Thermometry (Foundations of Sensor Design)

> [!NOTE]
> A **thermometric substance** is the material whose property (called the **thermometric property**) we measure. Examples: mercury (property = length of column), platinum (property = electrical resistance), and a thermocouple junction (property = thermo-emf).

**The three laws of thermometry are:**

1. **Law of Constant Volume Gas Thermometer / Avogadro's Law basis:** The pressure of a fixed mass of gas at constant volume is proportional to its thermodynamic temperature:
$$P \propto T \quad (\text{at } V = \text{const})$$

2. **Law of Constant Pressure Gas Thermometer:** The volume of a fixed mass of gas at constant pressure is proportional to its thermodynamic temperature:
$$V \propto T \quad (\text{at } P = \text{const})$$

3. **Ideal Gas Law (combined):**
$$PV = nRT$$
This is the master relation from which most temperature-dependent sensor calibrations are derived.

## 2.3 Thermal Expansion — The Physical Basis of Mechanical Temperature Sensors

When a solid is heated, its atoms vibrate with greater amplitude, increasing the average inter-atomic spacing. This manifests as **thermal expansion**, which underpins devices like the bimetallic strip and mercury-in-glass thermometer.

### 2.3.1 Linear (Length) Expansion
For a rod of original length $L_0$ subjected to a temperature change $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_T = L_0(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$$

where $\alpha$ = **coefficient of linear expansion** (units: per K or $^\circ$C$^{-1}$).

### 2.3.2 Areal (Surface) Expansion
For a plate of original area $A_0$:

$$\Delta A = \beta A_0 \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_T = A_0(1 + \beta \Delta T)$$

where $\beta$ = **coefficient of areal expansion**.

### 2.3.3 Volumetric (Cubical) Expansion
For a body of original volume $V_0$:

$$\Delta V = \gamma V_0 \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_T = V_0(1 + \gamma \Delta T)$$

where $\gamma$ = **coefficient of volumetric expansion**.

### 2.3.4 The Universal Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any homogeneous isotropic solid, the three coefficients are related by:
> $$\beta = 2\alpha, \quad \gamma = 3\alpha$$
> This is rigorously true in the limit of small temperature changes and for isotropic materials. In anisotropic crystals, a **tensor** formulation is required.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Thermal Expansion

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units of Coefficient |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length change | $\Delta L$ | $\alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | $\alpha$: K$^{-1}$ or $^\circ$C$^{-1}$ |
| Area change | $\Delta A$ | $\beta A_0 \Delta T$ | $\beta$: K$^{-1}$ |
| Volume change | $\Delta V$ | $\gamma V_0 \Delta T$ | $\gamma$: K$^{-1}$ |
| Relation (isotropic) | — | $\beta = 2\alpha$, $\gamma = 3\alpha$ | — |

### 2.3.5 The Bimetallic Strip
Two metals with **different** $\alpha$ values (e.g., **invar** $\alpha \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/K and **brass** $\alpha \approx 19 \times 10^{-6}$/K) are bonded. On heating, the brass expands more, causing the strip to **bend towards the lower-$\alpha$ metal**. The radius of curvature $R$ is given by:

$$R = \dfrac{t}{24(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T} \left[1 + \dfrac{(E_1 I_1 + E_2 I_2)}{E_1 I_1 + E_2 I_2} \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T \cdot h}{...}\right]$$

In the simplified engineering form (equal thickness $t$):

$$R \approx \dfrac{t}{2(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T}$$

This bending deflection is the working principle of **thermostats, circuit breakers, and oven control switches**.

## 2.4 Thermoelectric Effects (The Heart of Thermocouples)

When two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined at a junction, three distinct thermoelectric effects arise — all essential for KTU Module 1.

### 2.4.1 Seebeck Effect (1821) — The Generator Mode
When the two junctions of a thermocouple are maintained at different temperatures $T_h$ and $T_c$ (with $T_h > T_c$), a net **thermo-emf** $E$ is generated in the open circuit:

$$E = \alpha_{AB}(T_h - T_c) + \dfrac{1}{2}\beta_{AB}(T_h^2 - T_c^2) + \ldots$$

For small temperature differences (linear approximation):

$$E \approx \alpha_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\alpha_{AB}$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** of the pair (in V/K or $\mu$V/$^\circ$C). This is the **direct conversion of heat to electricity** — the principle of thermoelectric generators and all standard thermocouples.

> [!NOTE]
> Sign convention (KTU standard): If the current flows from metal A to B at the *hot* junction, then A is **thermoelectrically positive** with respect to B. Standard tables list each metal's polarity relative to **platinum (Pt)** as the reference.

### 2.4.2 Peltier Effect (1834) — The Refrigerator Mode
When a current $I$ is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either **absorbed** (cooling) or **released** (heating) at the junction, depending on the current direction:

$$Q_P = \pi_{AB} \cdot I = \pi_{AB} \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(for time } t\text{)}$$

where $\pi_{AB}$ is the **Peltier coefficient** (in V, equivalent to W/A). This is the principle of **thermoelectric coolers (TECs)** used in laser diode temperature control and portable refrigerators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kelvin's relation** connects the two coefficients:
> $$\pi_{AB} = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}$$
> This means the *same physical parameter* $\alpha_{AB}$ governs both the generator (Seebeck) and the refrigerator (Peltier) behaviour.

### 2.4.3 Thomson Effect (1856)
When a current $I$ flows through a single homogeneous conductor that has a temperature gradient $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ along it, heat is evolved or absorbed at a rate:

$$Q_{Th} = \sigma_T \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

where $\sigma_T$ is the **Thomson coefficient** (in V/K). For most metals, $\sigma_T$ is small (of order $\mu$V/K) but is non-negligible in precision thermocouple measurements.

## 2.5 Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)

An RTD exploits the linear (or near-linear) increase in electrical resistance of a pure metal with temperature. The standard **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** is:

$$R_T = R_0\left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C(T - 100)T^3 \right] \quad \text{(for } T < 0^\circ\text{C)}$$
$$R_T = R_0\left[1 + A T + B T^2\right] \quad \text{(for } 0 \le T \le 600^\circ\text{C)}$$

For the common **PT100 (platinum, $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at 0$^\circ$C)**, the IEC 60751 standard coefficients are:
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ $^\circ$C$^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ $^\circ$C$^{-2}$
- $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ $^\circ$C$^{-4}$ (for $T < 0$)

> [!NOTE]
> The **temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR)** is defined as:
> $$\alpha_R = \dfrac{1}{R_0}\dfrac{dR}{dT}$$
> For platinum, $\alpha_R \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ /K (a hallmark of the PT100 standard).

## 2.6 Thermistors (Thermally Sensitive Resistors)

Thermistors are sintered semiconductor devices (oxides of Mn, Ni, Co, Fe) whose resistance varies **strongly and non-linearly** with temperature.

### 2.6.1 NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)
Resistance **decreases** with rising temperature. The governing equation is the **Steinhart-Hart** or the simpler **$\beta$-parameter** model:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[\beta\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

where $R_0$ is the resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 298.15 K = 25$^\circ$C) and $\beta$ is a material constant (typically 3000–5000 K).

### 2.6.2 PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient)
Resistance **increases** with temperature in a specific region (e.g., barium titanate-based devices, which switch at the Curie temperature). Used as self-resetting fuses and inrush current limiters.

## 2.7 Radiation Thermometry (Non-Contact Temperature Sensing)

When a body's temperature exceeds ~600$^\circ$C, contact sensors become impractical. We then use the **thermal radiation** emitted by the body.

### 2.7.1 Stefan-Boltzmann Law
A **blackbody** at thermodynamic temperature $T$ emits total radiant exitance (power per unit area) over all wavelengths:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

For a real (gray) body with emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

### 2.7.2 Wien's Displacement Law
The wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which the spectral exitance of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

This principle underlies **optical pyrometers** and **infrared thermal imagers**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Temperature sensors are the **most deployed sensor class in industry** — every industrial process control loop (distillation column, furnace, HVAC, refrigerator, automotive ECU, jet engine) relies on precise temperature measurement. Modern cars contain 20–30 temperature sensors (coolant, intake air, exhaust gas, cabin, oil, battery). Mechatronic systems integrate these sensors with microcontrollers via **signal conditioning, cold-junction compensation (for thermocouples), and 3-wire/4-wire lead compensation (for RTDs)**.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: General Linear Conversion Between Two Temperature Scales

### Given
- Scale $X$ has lower fixed point $X_L$, upper fixed point $X_U$, and a reading $X$.
- Scale $Y$ has lower fixed point $Y_L$, upper fixed point $Y_U$, and a reading $Y$.

### To Find
- A general conversion formula between $X$ and $Y$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the fractional interval.**
On the $X$ scale, the reading $X$ lies a fraction $\eta$ of the total interval above the lower fixed point:
$$\eta = \dfrac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the fractions on both scales.**
By the **principle of linear correspondence** between two temperature scales (which assumes both are linear functions of an underlying "true" temperature), the fractional position on the $Y$ scale must be the same:
$$\dfrac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L} = \dfrac{Y - Y_L}{Y_U - Y_L}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Y$.**
Multiply both sides by $(Y_U - Y_L)$ and add $Y_L$:
$$Y - Y_L = \dfrac{(Y_U - Y_L)(X - X_L)}{X_U - X_L}$$

$$\boxed{\,Y = Y_L + (Y_U - Y_L) \cdot \dfrac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the Celsius-to-Fahrenheit case.**
With $X_L = 0$, $X_U = 100$ (Celsius) and $Y_L = 32$, $Y_U = 212$ (Fahrenheit):
$$t_{^\circ\text{F}} = 32 + (212 - 32) \cdot \dfrac{t_{^\circ\text{C}} - 0}{100 - 0} = 32 + \dfrac{180}{100}\,t_{^\circ\text{C}}$$
$$\boxed{\,t_{^\circ\text{F}} = \dfrac{9}{5}\,t_{^\circ\text{C}} + 32\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: The Relationship $\beta = 2\alpha$ and $\gamma = 3\alpha$ (Isotropic Solid)

### Given
A solid of initial dimensions $L_0 \times L_0 \times L_0$ (cube) with coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express new edge length after heating by $\Delta T$.**
$$L = L_0(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$$

**Step 2 — Compute new area.**
Original area $A_0 = L_0^2$. New area $A = L^2$:
$$A = L_0^2(1 + \alpha \Delta T)^2$$

**Step 3 — Expand using the binomial theorem (valid for small $\alpha \Delta T$).**
$$(1 + \alpha \Delta T)^2 = 1 + 2\alpha \Delta T + (\alpha \Delta T)^2 \approx 1 + 2\alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Identify the coefficient.**
By definition $A = A_0(1 + \beta \Delta T)$. Comparing:
$$1 + 2\alpha \Delta T = 1 + \beta \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = 2\alpha$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for volume.**
$V = L^3 = L_0^3(1 + \alpha \Delta T)^3 \approx L_0^3(1 + 3\alpha \Delta T)$, so:
$$\gamma = 3\alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> The high-order terms $(\alpha \Delta T)^2$ and $(\alpha \Delta T)^3$ are dropped because $\alpha$ is typically of order $10^{-5}$/K, so for $\Delta T \sim 100$ K, $\alpha \Delta T \sim 10^{-3}$, making the squared/cubed terms $\sim 10^{-6}$ — negligible.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Seebeck emf in the Linear Approximation

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Seebeck coefficient.**
For a pair of metals A and B, the **relative Seebeck coefficient** is:
$$\alpha_{AB} = \alpha_A - \alpha_B$$
where $\alpha_A$ and $\alpha_B$ are the **absolute Seebeck coefficients** of the two metals (small but finite, typically 0.1 to 50 $\mu$V/K).

**Step 2 — Write the differential emf.**
The infinitesimal emf $dE$ produced by an infinitesimal temperature difference $dT$ at a junction is:
$$dE = \alpha_{AB}\,dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate between cold and hot junctions.**
$$E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} \alpha_{AB}(T)\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Linearise.**
If $\alpha_{AB}$ is approximately independent of $T$ (valid over small ranges for standard thermocouples, typically $\pm$0.1% per 100$^\circ$C):
$$E \approx \alpha_{AB}(T_h - T_c) = \alpha_{AB}\,\Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why calibration is essential:** Real thermocouples (Type K, Type J, Type T, etc.) have **non-linear** $\alpha_{AB}(T)$. The reference tables (NIST ITS-90 thermocouple database) provide $E(T)$ in $\mu$V at discrete reference temperatures. The KTU 2024 exam commonly provides a thermocouple table and asks the student to compute $T$ by **interpolation** from the measured emf.

## 3.4 Derivation: Cold-Junction Compensation (Reference Junction at $0^\circ$C)

### Problem
A Type K thermocouple with its measuring (hot) junction at $T_h$ and its reference (cold) junction at ambient $T_{\text{amb}}$ (not $0^\circ$C) produces an emf $E_{\text{meas}}(T_h, T_{\text{amb}})$. Compute the equivalent emf $E(0^\circ\text{C}, T_h)$ that would be obtained if the reference junction were at $0^\circ$C.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Intermediate Metals.**
The thermocouple circuit is equivalent to a fictitious circuit with junctions at $T_h$ and $0^\circ$C:
$$E(T_h, 0) = E(T_h, T_{\text{amb}}) + E(T_{\text{amb}}, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the tabulated emf.**
The first term $E(T_h, T_{\text{amb}})$ is **directly measured**.
The second term $E(T_{\text{amb}}, 0)$ is **looked up from the standard Type K reference table** at $T_{\text{amb}}$ (measured by a separate sensor like an IC sensor or RTD).

**Step 3 — Sum.**
$$E_{\text{corr}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{tab}}(T_{\text{amb}})$$

**Step 4 — Look up the corrected temperature.**
Convert $E_{\text{corr}}$ to the actual hot-junction temperature $T_h$ via the same reference table.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern mechatronic systems implement this **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** in firmware on the microcontroller, using a separate on-board IC temperature sensor (e.g., LM35, DS18B20) for $T_{\text{amb}}$.

## 3.5 Derivation: Stefan-Boltzmann Emitted Power from a Real Surface

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Blackbody spectral exitance (Planck's law).**
$$M_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T) = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5}\dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over all wavelengths $\lambda \in [0, \infty)$ to get total blackbody exitance.**
$$E_b = \int_0^\infty M_{\lambda,b}\,d\lambda = \sigma T^4$$
where $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m$^{-2}$·K$^{-4}$.

**Step 3 — Real-body correction.**
A real body emits less than a blackbody by a factor of **emissivity** $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$:
$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

**Step 4 — Net radiation exchange between body at $T_1$ and surroundings at $T_2$ inside a small enclosure (formulas used in pyrometry).**
$$Q_{\text{net}} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

This is the working equation of every **radiation pyrometer** and **infrared camera**.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Bimetallic Strip Deflection

### Problem
A bimetallic strip is made of brass (top, $\alpha_1 = 19 \times 10^{-6}$/K) and invar (bottom, $\alpha_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/K). Each layer is $t/2 = 0.5$ mm thick, total thickness $t = 1$ mm, length $L = 50$ mm. Temperature rises by $\Delta T = 100$ K. Find the tip deflection $\delta$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Curvature formula.**
$$\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{t(14 + 1)}$$
For the simplified thin-strip case: $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{2(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{t}$

**Step 2 — Compute.**
$$\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{2 \times (19 - 1.2) \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 3.56 \text{ m}^{-1}$$
So $R = 0.281$ m.

**Step 3 — Tip deflection.**
$$\delta = R - \sqrt{R^2 - L^2} = 0.281 - \sqrt{0.281^2 - 0.05^2} = 0.281 - \sqrt{0.0789 - 0.0025} = 0.281 - \sqrt{0.0764}$$
$$\delta = 0.281 - 0.2764 = 0.0046 \text{ m} \approx 4.6 \text{ mm}$$

The strip tip moves by about **4.6 mm** — sufficient to actuate a microswitch in a thermostat.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example: PT100 at Elevated Temperature

### Problem
A PT100 RTD is used in a process at $T = 250^\circ$C. Using the Callendar-Van Dusen equation, compute its resistance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the equation valid for $0 \le T \le 600^\circ$C.**
$$R_T = R_0\left[1 + AT + BT^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the IEC 60751 coefficients.**
$$R_{250} = 100\left[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(250) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(250)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate term by term.**
- $AT = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 0.9771$
- $BT^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 62\,500 = -0.0361$

**Step 4 — Combine.**
$$R_{250} = 100 \times [1 + 0.9771 - 0.0361] = 100 \times 1.9410 = 194.10\,\Omega$$

The expected PT100 resistance at 250$^\circ$C is **194.10 $\Omega$**, in agreement with standard reference tables.
