# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. How does it form the basis of temperature measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics states that if two systems A and B are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other. This law establishes the concept of *temperature* as a unique property and allows the construction of a **thermometer**. A thermometric body C (e.g., mercury, a thermocouple junction, or a platinum wire) can be placed in contact with A, and the equilibrium condition ensures that a single physical parameter (length, emf, or resistance) uniquely indicates A's temperature. Without the Zeroth Law, no two thermometers would agree on a temperature scale, making measurement impossible.
*(Award 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for its role in measurement.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the Seebeck coefficient. Write its SI unit. Why is a thermocouple a "self-generating" sensor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Seebeck coefficient** ($\alpha_{AB}$) of a thermocouple made of metals A and B is defined as the incremental thermo-emf produced per unit temperature difference: $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{dE}{dT}$. Its SI unit is **V/K** (in practice, $\mu$V/$^\circ$C). A thermocouple is "self-generating" because it produces a measurable emf *without requiring any external excitation source* — heat applied to the measuring junction is directly converted into electrical energy via the Seebeck effect. This makes it ideal for remote, low-power, and high-temperature applications.
*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for self-generation explanation.)*

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### Question B (a) — OPTION A
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set A]**
**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **bimetallic strip**. Derive an expression for the deflection produced when subjected to a temperature change. List two industrial applications.

**(b) (7 marks)** A copper rod of length 2.0 m is heated from 20$^\circ$C to 120$^\circ$C. The coefficient of linear expansion of copper is $\alpha = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$/K. Find (i) the change in length, (ii) the change in area (assuming a square cross-section of side 10 mm), and (iii) the change in volume.

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A bimetallic strip consists of two metal strips with **different coefficients of linear expansion** ($\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$) — e.g., brass ($\alpha_1 = 19 \times 10^{-6}$/K) and invar ($\alpha_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/K) — firmly bonded along their entire length. When heated, the high-$\alpha$ layer expands more than the low-$\alpha$ layer, causing the composite strip to **bend (curl) towards the low-$\alpha$ side**. The tip moves through a measurable deflection $\delta$, which can actuate a switch pointer or a control valve.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
For equal thicknesses $t/2$ each and length $L$, the radius of curvature $R$ is:
$$R = \dfrac{t}{2(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}$$
The tip deflection (for $\delta \ll L$):
$$\delta = \dfrac{L^2}{2R} = \dfrac{L^2(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{t}$$

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Thermostats** in refrigerators, geysers, and irons.
2. **Circuit breakers** for overload protection.
3. **Temperature indicators** in cars and ovens.
4. **Compensation elements** in clock mechanisms and precision instruments.

**Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1 — Change in length (2 marks).**
$$\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T = (1.7 \times 10^{-5}) \times 2.0 \times (120 - 20) = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.0 \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\Delta L = 3.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.4 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Change in area (2.5 marks).**
Side of cross-section: $a_0 = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m, so $A_0 = a_0^2 = 10^{-4}$ m$^2$.
$\beta = 2\alpha = 3.4 \times 10^{-5}$/K.
$$\Delta A = \beta A_0 \Delta T = (3.4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 3.4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$
$$\boxed{\Delta A = 0.34 \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Change in volume (2.5 marks).**
$V_0 = L_0 A_0 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m$^3 = 200$ cm$^3$.
$\gamma = 3\alpha = 5.1 \times 10^{-5}$/K.
$$\Delta V = \gamma V_0 \Delta T = (5.1 \times 10^{-5}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 100 = 1.02 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$
$$\boxed{\Delta V = 1.02 \text{ cm}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Warning):** Students often incorrectly use $\alpha$ for area and volume calculations. The strict rule is: $\beta = 2\alpha$ and $\gamma = 3\alpha$ for **isotropic** solids only. Also, the *cross-section* in this problem is square (10 mm × 10 mm) — students sometimes misread this and treat it as 10 mm radius, leading to a $\pi$ factor error. **Always read dimensions carefully!**

---

### Question B (b) — OPTION B
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Set B]**
**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the **Seebeck and Peltier effects** in a thermocouple. State the relation between the Seebeck and Peltier coefficients.

**(b) (7 marks)** A Type K thermocouple has its hot junction at 500$^\circ$C and cold junction at 30$^\circ$C. The Seebeck coefficient can be approximated as $\alpha_{AB} = 40\,\mu$V/$^\circ$C. Calculate the open-circuit emf generated.

**Model Solution for B(a) — Option B:**

**Seebeck Effect (3 marks):**
A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metals (say, **Chromel** and **Alumel** for Type K) joined at one end to form the *hot (measuring) junction*. The other ends (the *cold* or *reference* junction) are kept at a known lower temperature. Due to the **Seebeck effect**, a continuous emf is developed in the circuit proportional to the temperature difference. The circuit diagram shows a closed loop with the two junctions and a voltmeter.

**Peltier Effect (3 marks):**
When a current $I$ is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either absorbed (one junction becomes cold) or liberated (the other becomes hot) at the rate $Q_P = \pi_{AB} I$, where $\pi_{AB}$ is the Peltier coefficient. Reversing the current reverses the direction of heat flow. This is the basis of **thermoelectric refrigeration** (Peltier coolers).

**Kelvin Relation (1 mark):**
$$\pi_{AB} = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}$$
where $T$ is the absolute temperature of the junction. This connects the two phenomena at the fundamental level.

**Model Solution for B(b) — Option B:**

**Step 1 — Identify the temperature difference (2 marks).**
$$\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 500 - 30 = 470\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear Seebeck equation (3 marks).**
$$E = \alpha_{AB} \cdot \Delta T = 40\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C} \times 470\,^\circ\text{C}$$
$$E = 18\,800\,\mu\text{V} = 18.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the NIST Type K reference table (2 marks).**
The standard Type K table at $T_h = 500\,^\circ$C, $T_{\text{ref}} = 0\,^\circ$C gives $E \approx 20.640$ mV. The linear approximation with constant $\alpha = 40\,\mu$V/$^\circ$C gives 20.0 mV, which is **3.1% low** — illustrating the non-linear nature of real thermocouples and the need for **lookup-table linearisation** in firmware.

$$\boxed{E_{\text{linear}} = 18.8 \text{ mV; \quad actual} \approx 20.64 \text{ mV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students use $\alpha_{AB} = 40\,\mu$V/K instead of $\mu$V/$^\circ$C. Since both are in the *same size* on the *interval* scale, the numerical answer is **the same** for temperature *differences* — but students should clearly state the unit convention. The real trap is **ignoring non-linearity** and writing $E = 20.64$ mV as the linear answer; the examiner expects a remark that the linear approximation underestimates by ~3% for Type K at 500$^\circ$C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Temperature (Module 1)**

- **Zeroth Law** is the *philosophical* basis of temperature measurement; without it, no two thermometers could agree.
- **Four scales**: Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin (absolute, SI), Rankine (absolute, imperial). Kelvin and Rankine differ by a factor of $9/5$.
- **Conversion between any two linear scales** uses the universal formula: $\dfrac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L} = \dfrac{Y - Y_L}{Y_U - Y_L}$.
- **Thermal expansion coefficients** for isotropic solids: $\beta = 2\alpha$ and $\gamma = 3\alpha$.
- **Bimetallic strip** uses *differential* expansion of two bonded metals — used in thermostats.
- **Seebeck effect** = heat-to-electricity (thermocouples, TEGs). $\alpha_{AB}$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** in V/K.
- **Peltier effect** = electricity-driven heat pumping (TECs). $\pi_{AB}$ is the **Peltier coefficient** in W/A.
- **Kelvin relation**: $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot \alpha_{AB}$ links Seebeck and Peltier.
- **Thomson effect**: heat evolved in a single conductor carrying current along a temperature gradient.
- **RTDs** (especially PT100) are linear, stable, and accurate; resistance increases with $T$ via the **Callendar-Van Dusen** equation.
- **Thermistors** (NTC, PTC) are highly non-linear but very sensitive; use the **$\beta$-parameter equation** or **Steinhart-Hart** model.
- **IC sensors** (LM35, LM335, AD590) use semiconductor junction voltage or current proportional to absolute temperature.
- **Radiation pyrometers** use **Stefan-Boltzmann** ($E = \varepsilon\sigma T^4$) and **Wien's displacement** ($\lambda_{\max} T = b$).
- **Cold-junction compensation (CJC)** is mandatory for accurate thermocouple measurements when the reference junction is not at 0$^\circ$C.
- **3-wire / 4-wire connections** cancel lead-resistance errors in RTD measurements.
- **KTU high-priority numericals**: (i) temperature scale conversions, (ii) thermal expansion (length/area/volume), (iii) Seebeck emf computation, (iv) PT100 resistance at given $T$, (v) Stefan-Boltzmann radiated power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory hooks:**
> - "**K**elvin is the *king* of temperature — all other scales bow to it (offset by 273.15)."
> - "**Seebeck sees, Peltier pumps, Thomson thinks** — three thermoelectric brothers."
> - "$\alpha$ for **L**ength, $\beta$ for **A**rea ($\beta=2\alpha$), $\gamma$ for **V**olume ($\gamma=3\alpha$) — remember LAV."
> - "PT100 = Platinum, 100 $\Omega$ at 0$^\circ$C — a mechatronic standard."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Light Sensor # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Light Sensor** (also called a **photosensor** or **optoelectronic sensor**) is a transducer that converts incident optical radiation (photons) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change). It is a fundamental component in mechatronic systems for non-contact detection, position sensing, object recognition, and feedback control in automated machinery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A light sensor is an optoelectronic device whose electrical output varies as a deterministic function of the intensity, wavelength, or phase of the incident electromagnetic radiation in the visible (≈380–750 nm), infrared (≈750 nm–1 mm), and ultraviolet (≈10–380 nm) spectra. ## 1.2 Classification of Light Sensors Light sensors are broadly classified based on the physical mechanism of photon–matter interaction: | Type | Mechanism | Output | |------|-----------|--------| | Photoresistor (LDR) | Photoconductivity | Resistance change | | Photodiode | Photovoltaic / Photoconductive | Current / Voltage | | Phototransistor | Photoconductivity + Transistor gain | Current | | Photovoltaic Cell (Solar Cell) | Photovoltaic effect | Voltage / Current | | CCD / CMOS Image Sensor | Charge generation in semiconductor arrays | Digital image | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Automatic Street Lamp:** > Imagine a street lamp that switches on automatically at dusk and switches off at dawn. How does it "know"? Inside its housing, a tiny light-sensitive eye (the LDR) is constantly watching the ambient brightness. When sunlight is strong, electrons inside the sensor are tightly bound, so it behaves like a high-resistance path (≈ MΩ), and the control circuit keeps the lamp OFF. As darkness falls, photons strike the sensor, liberating electrons, reducing its resistance to a few hundred ohms. The control circuit detects this drop and turns the lamp ON. This is the essence of a light sensor — **it is a photon-to-electron translator**. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ - **Energy of a photon:** $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ - **Standard luminous efficacy reference:** $K = 683 \ \text{lm/W}$ (at 555 nm) - **Visible spectrum wavelength range:** **380 nm to 750 nm** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic The working of any light sensor is governed by the **photoelectric effect** and **semiconductor band theory**. The logical flow is: 1. **Photon Incidence:** Light quanta (photons) strike the active semiconductor material (Si, Ge, GaAs, CdS). 2. **Energy Absorption:** Each photon must carry energy $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap energy) to liberate an electron from the valence band. 3. **Charge Carrier Generation:** An electron jumps from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving behind a hole — creating an **electron–hole pair (EHP)**. 4. **Charge Separation / Collection:** An applied electric field (reverse bias) or built-in junction potential separates the EHP, producing a measurable photocurrent $I_{ph}$. 5. **Signal Conditioning:** External circuitry amplifies and linearizes the output for control or display. ## 2.2 Three Foundational Photoelectric Effects > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Photoconductive Effect:** Resistance decreases with light intensity. Used in LDRs, phototransistors. > - **Photovoltaic Effect:** Light generates a voltage across a p-n junction. Used in solar cells, photodiodes (zero-bias mode). > - **Photoemissive Effect:** Light ejects electrons from a metal surface into vacuum. Used in photomultiplier tubes (PMTs). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) | | 2 | $E_g = \dfrac{1.24}{\lambda_c (\mu\text{m})}$ | Cutoff wavelength relation | $\lambda_c$ in μm, $E_g$ in eV | | 3 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi$ | Photocurrent equation | A, $\eta$ = quantum efficiency, $\Phi$ = photon flux (photons/s) | | 4 | $R = R_0 \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right)^\gamma$ | LDR resistance vs. illuminance | $R$ in Ω, $\gamma \approx 0.7$ to 0.9 | | 5 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ | Open-circuit voltage of photovoltaic cell | Volts | | 6 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right)$ | Photodiode I-V characteristic | Amperes | | 7 | $\text{Responsivity} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{q \eta \lambda}{hc}$ | Sensor responsivity | A/W | | 8 | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}$ | Specific detectivity | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | | 9 | $\Phi = \dfrac{P \lambda}{hc}$ | Photon flux from optical power $P$ | photons/s | | 10 | $\tau = RC$ | Response time of photodetector | seconds | > [!NOTE] > **Why these formulas matter in mechatronics:** In automated production lines, light sensors feed back positional and quality-control data to PLCs. The photocurrent equation governs sensitivity calibration; the LDR formula is critical for designing automatic lighting and sorting systems; responsivity determines sensor selection for laser-based alignment machines. ## 2.4 Engineering Applications Snapshot - **Industrial:** Object counting, conveyor belt sorting, bottle-filling level detection, robotic vision. - **Automotive:** Adaptive headlamps, rain-sensing wipers, lane-departure warning cameras. - **Medical:** Pulse oximeters (SpO$_2$), endoscopy, optical coherence tomography. - **Consumer:** Smartphones (ambient light sensing, camera auto-exposure), smart homes. - **Renewable:** Solar tracking systems, photovoltaic power generation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LDR Resistance as a Function of Illuminance **Statement:** Derive the empirical relationship governing the resistance of a Cadmium Sulfide (CdS) photoresistor under varying illuminance. **Starting Principle:** The conductivity of a photoconductor is proportional to the rate of generation of free charge carriers, which is in turn proportional to the incident photon flux. ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Deduction Let $n$ be the number density of photogenerated electrons, $\tau$ the carrier lifetime, and $\sigma$ the conductivity. $$ \sigma = q \cdot n \cdot \mu $$ where $\mu$ is the electron mobility. The carrier generation rate per unit volume is: $$ G = \eta \cdot \Phi_{photon} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{P}{h\nu} \cdot \dfrac{1}{A \cdot d} $$ Under steady-state equilibrium, the carrier density becomes: $$ n = G \cdot \tau = \eta \tau \cdot \dfrac{P}{h\nu \cdot A \cdot d} $$ Substituting illuminance $L = P / A$: $$ n = \dfrac{\eta \tau L}{h\nu \cdot d} $$ Therefore, resistance is inversely proportional to illuminance: $$ R \propto \dfrac{1}{L} $$ Experimentally, a power-law fit gives the **empirical LDR equation**: $$ R = R_0 \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right)^\gamma $$ **Conversion Logic Row:** - Photon absorption → carrier generation - Generation rate $\propto$ photon flux $\propto$ illuminance $L$ - Conductivity $\propto L$, hence resistance $R \propto 1/L^\gamma$ - Exponent $\gamma$ accounts for trap states, recombination, and material non-ideality ### Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem) **Given:** A CdS LDR has $R_1 = 1 \ \text{k}\Omega$ at $L_1 = 100 \ \text{lux}$ and $\gamma = 0.8$. Find the resistance at $L_2 = 500 \ \text{lux}$. $$ R_2 = R_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} \right)^\gamma = 1000 \cdot \left( \dfrac{100}{500} \right)^{0.8} $$ $$ R_2 = 1000 \cdot (0.2)^{0.8} = 1000 \cdot 0.2759 $$ $$ R_2 \approx 275.9 \ \Omega $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Photodiode I-V Characteristic (Shockley Equation with Photogeneration) **Statement:** Obtain the current–voltage equation of a p-n junction photodiode under illumination. ### Mathematical Deduction The total current in a p-n junction is the sum of the dark (diode) current and the photogenerated current. The dark diode current follows the Shockley equation: $$ I_{dark} = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right) $$ Under illumination, photogenerated carriers add a current $I_{ph}$ in the **reverse** direction: $$ I = I_{dark} - I_{ph} $$ Therefore, the complete photodiode equation is: $$ I = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right) - I_{ph} $$ **Open-Circuit Voltage Derivation (Set $I = 0$):** $$ 0 = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV_{oc}}{kT}} - 1 \right) - I_{ph} $$ $$ e^{\frac{qV_{oc}}{kT}} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 $$ $$ V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right) $$ **Short-Circuit Current:** At $V = 0$: $$ I_{sc} = -I_{ph} = -q \eta \Phi $$ **Conversion Logic Row:** - Dark junction behaves as a regular diode - Photons create additional EHPs, which the junction sweeps out as reverse current - Subtracting the photogenerated current from dark current yields the net I-V curve - $V_{oc}$ corresponds to the photovoltaic mode; $I_{sc}$ corresponds to photoconductive short-circuit ### Numerical Verification **Given:** $I_{ph} = 10 \ \text{mA}$, $I_0 = 1 \ \text{nA}$, $T = 300 \ \text{K}$, $kT/q \approx 25.85 \ \text{mV}$. $$ V_{oc} = 25.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} + 1 \right) $$ $$ V_{oc} = 0.02585 \cdot \ln(10^7) = 0.02585 \cdot 16.118 $$ $$ V_{oc} \approx 0.4167 \ \text{V} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutoff Wavelength from Bandgap Energy A photodetector only responds to photons with energy greater than its bandgap: $$ E_g = h\nu_c = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_c} $$ Converting $E_g$ to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J) and $\lambda_c$ to μm yields the practical **engineer's rule**: $$ \lambda_c (\mu\text{m}) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g (\text{eV})} $$ | Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda_c$ (nm) | Spectral Region | |----------|-----------|------------------|-----------------| | Silicon (Si) | 1.12 | 1107 | Near-IR / Visible | | Germanium (Ge) | 0.67 | 1851 | Near-IR | | GaAs | 1.42 | 873 | Near-IR / Red | | CdS | 2.42 | 512 | Visible (green) | | InGaAs | 0.75 | 1653 | IR telecom | ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance | Parameter | LDR (CdS) | Photodiode (Si PIN) | Phototransistor | Solar Cell | |-----------|-----------|---------------------|------------------|------------| | Spectral Range | 400–700 nm | 300–1100 nm | 400–1100 nm | 400–1100 nm | | Response Time | 50–100 ms | ns to μs | 1–10 μs | μs | | Sensitivity | Moderate | High | Very High | Moderate | | Linearity | Poor (logarithmic) | Excellent | Moderate | Good | | Output | Resistance | Current | Current | Voltage / Current | | Typical Use | Light meters | Optical comms, encoders | Object detection | Power generation | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Light Sensor Subsystem in Mechatronics ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Light Source
Ambient or Laser] --> B[Optical Component
Lens or Aperture] B --> C[Photosensitive Element
LDR, Photodiode, or Phototransistor] C --> D[Signal Conditioning Unit
Amplifier and Filter] D --> E[ADC Converter
Analog to Digital] E --> F[Microcontroller or PLC
Decision Logic] F --> G{Threshold
Comparison} G -- Above Threshold --> H[Actuator Activation
Motor, Relay, Display] G -- Below Threshold --> I[Hold or Alternate Action] H --> J[Feedback to System
Closed Loop Control] I --> J J --> A ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Photodiode Operating Modes ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Incident Photons] --> B[Photon Absorption
in Depletion Region] B --> C[Electron Hole Pair
Generation] C --> D{PN Junction
Bias Mode} D -- Zero Bias --> E[Photovoltaic Mode
Open Circuit Voltage] D -- Reverse Bias --> F[Photoconductive Mode
Linear Current Output] D -- Forward Bias --> G[Forward Conduction
No Photodetection] E --> H[Output Signal
Voltage V_oc] F --> I[Output Signal
Current I_ph] G --> J[Output Signal
No Response] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Classification Hierarchy of Light Sensors ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Light Sensors] --> B[Active Sensors
Require External Power] A --> C[Passive Sensors
Self Generating] B --> D[Photoresistor LDR] B --> E[Photodiode] B --> F[Phototransistor] B --> G[CCD and CMOS Arrays] C --> H[Photovoltaic Cell
Solar Cell] C --> I[Photoemissive Tube
PMT] D --> J[Output: Resistance Change] E --> K[Output: Current or Voltage] F --> L[Output: Amplified Current] G --> M[Output: Digital Image Matrix] H --> N[Output: DC Voltage and Current] I --> O[Output: Cascaded Electron Pulse] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Process Flow in a Mechatronic Sorting Application ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Conveyor Belt
Carries Objects] --> B[Light Emitter
LED or Laser Diode] B --> C[Object in Beam Path
Interception or Reflection] C --> D[Light Sensor
Detects Change in Light] D --> E[Signal Processor
Comparator and Amplifier] E --> F[Microcontroller
Classification Logic] F --> G[Pneumatic Pusher
Sorter Actuator] G --> H[Object Diverted to
Correct Bin] H --> I[Feedback Signal
System Status Update] I --> A ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define a light sensor. List any four types of light sensors used in mechatronic systems.** **Model Answer:** A light sensor is an optoelectronic transducer that converts incident optical energy (photons) into a measurable electrical signal. It operates on the photoelectric effect in semiconductor materials. **Four Types:** 1. **Photoresistor (LDR)** — resistance changes with light. 2. **Photodiode** — generates photocurrent when reverse-biased. 3. **Phototransistor** — photodiode with internal current amplification. 4. **Photovoltaic cell (solar cell)** — generates voltage under illumination. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the photoelectric effect. What is the threshold wavelength for a silicon photodiode with $E_g = 1.12$ eV?** **Model Answer:** The photoelectric effect is the phenomenon in which incident photons liberate electrons from a material, generating a measurable electrical signal. **Threshold wavelength:** $$ \lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{1.12} = 1.107 \ \mu\text{m} = 1107 \ \text{nm} $$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a photodiode operating in photoconductive mode. Derive its current–voltage characteristic equation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A silicon photodiode has quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ and is illuminated by light of wavelength $\lambda = 900 \ \text{nm}$ with optical power $P = 100 \ \mu\text{W}$. Calculate the photocurrent. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Q. A (a): **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid Block):** A photodiode is reverse-biased; incident photons create EHPs in the depletion region; the electric field sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, producing a reverse photocurrent. **Working Steps:** 1. Photons with $E \geq E_g$ strike the depletion region. 2. Each absorbed photon generates one EHP. 3. The reverse bias sweeps electrons and holes out before they recombine. 4. Photocurrent flows in the external circuit, proportional to incident light intensity. **Derivation:** $$ I = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right) - I_{ph} $$ In reverse bias ($V$ is large negative), the exponential term vanishes: $$ I \approx -I_0 - I_{ph} \approx -I_{ph} $$ Photocurrent expression: $$ I_{ph} = q \eta \Phi = q \eta \cdot \dfrac{P \lambda}{hc} $$ --- #### Solution to Q. A (b): **Given:** $\eta = 0.85$, $\lambda = 900 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $P = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ W, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C. $$ \Phi = \dfrac{P \lambda}{hc} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} $$ $$ \Phi = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-11}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.527 \times 10^{14} \ \text{photons/s} $$ $$ I_{ph} = q \eta \Phi = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.85 \times 4.527 \times 10^{14} $$ $$ I_{ph} = 6.165 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{A} \approx 61.65 \ \mu\text{A} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]` **Q. (a)** Explain the construction and working of an LDR. Derive the empirical relation between its resistance and illuminance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A CdS LDR has $R_1 = 5 \ \text{k}\Omega$ at $L_1 = 50 \ \text{lux}$. If the exponent $\gamma = 0.85$, determine the resistance when $L_2 = 500 \ \text{lux}$. Also comment on its suitability for fast switching applications. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Q. B (a): **Construction:** A Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) is made of a semiconductor material such as **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** deposited as a thin zigzag pattern on an insulating substrate. Two metal electrodes are connected at the ends. The zigzag geometry increases the effective light-collecting area and reduces dark resistance. **Working:** In darkness, almost all electrons in the CdS are trapped, and the resistance is very high (≈ MΩ). When photons strike the surface, electrons in the valence band absorb energy and jump to the conduction band, dramatically increasing conductivity and reducing resistance. **Derivation (as in Section 3.1):** $$ R = R_0 \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right)^\gamma $$ where $\gamma$ is a material constant typically between 0.7 and 0.9. --- #### Solution to Q. B (b): **Given:** $R_1 = 5 \ \text{k}\Omega$, $L_1 = 50 \ \text{lux}$, $L_2 = 500 \ \text{lux}$, $\gamma = 0.85$. $$ R_2 = R_1 \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} \right)^\gamma = 5000 \cdot \left( \dfrac{50}{500} \right)^{0.85} $$ $$ R_2 = 5000 \cdot (0.1)^{0.85} = 5000 \cdot 0.1413 $$ $$ R_2 \approx 706.5 \ \Omega $$ **Suitability for fast switching:** LDRs have a **slow response time** of 50–100 ms due to the slow recombination of charge carriers in the polycrystalline CdS structure. They are **NOT suitable** for high-speed switching or high-frequency applications. For such tasks, **photodiodes** (ns response) or **phototransistors** (μs response) must be used. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit mismatches:** Always convert wavelength to meters and time to seconds before substituting. Mixing nm and μm in photon energy calculations is a common 2-mark loss. > 2. **Sign convention confusion:** In the photodiode equation, $I_{ph}$ is conventionally written as a *positive* quantity subtracted from the dark current, since photogenerated carriers flow in the reverse direction. > 3. **Forgetting $V_{oc}$ approximation:** The "+1" inside the logarithm of $V_{oc}$ is often dropped. Always justify the simplification when $I_{ph} \gg I_0$. > 4. **LDR exponent mistake:** Students often incorrectly use $\gamma = 1$. Always state the empirical nature of $\gamma$. > 5. **Lack of diagrams:** In 7-mark questions, a missing block diagram costs 2 marks minimum. Always include a clear, labeled schematic. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Light Sensor** - 🔹 A light sensor converts **photons → electrical signal** via the photoelectric effect. - 🔹 Three core mechanisms: **photoconductive, photovoltaic, photoemissive**. - 🔹 **Photon energy:** $E = hc / \lambda$; threshold condition: $E \geq E_g$. - 🔹 **Cutoff wavelength formula:** $\lambda_c (\mu\text{m}) = 1.24 / E_g (\text{eV})$. - 🔹 **LDR:** resistance $\downarrow$ as light $\uparrow$; slow response (≈ 100 ms); $R = R_0 (L_0/L)^\gamma$. - 🔹 **Photodiode I-V:** $I = I_0(e^{qV/kT} - 1) - I_{ph}$; reverse bias for linear operation. - 🔹 **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} = (kT/q)\ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$. - 🔹 **Photocurrent:** $I_{ph} = q \eta \Phi = q \eta P\lambda / hc$. - 🔹 **Responsivity:** $R_\lambda = q\eta\lambda / hc$, expressed in A/W. - 🔹 **Phototransistor:** photodiode + BJT; internal gain of 100–1000; moderate speed. - 🔹 **Solar cell:** large-area photovoltaic; delivers power; characterized by $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, fill factor, efficiency. - 🔹 **CCD/CMOS:** pixel-array imaging sensors; used in machine vision and robotics. - 🔹 **PMT:** photoemissive + secondary emission; ultra-high sensitivity; detects single photons. - 🔹 **Mechatronic role:** non-contact object detection, position feedback, optical encoders, quality inspection, solar tracking. - 🔹 **Selection criteria:** spectral range, response time, sensitivity, linearity, operating environment. --- **End of Module 1 — Light Sensor Notes** | *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes.*

topics.title: Acceleration and Vibration Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acceleration and Vibration Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acceleration measurement** is the process of quantifying the rate of change of velocity of a body with respect to time, expressed in SI units of **m/s²** (or the dimensionless quantity **g**, where **1 g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**). In the context of mechatronic systems, acceleration is rarely measured by direct differentiation of velocity signals (which amplifies noise); instead, it is sensed through the inertial reaction of a **seismic mass** mounted on a compliant suspension within a transducer known as an **accelerometer**.

**Vibration measurement** is the experimental determination of oscillatory motion parameters — namely **displacement, velocity, and acceleration** — as a function of frequency and time, using dedicated seismic transducers. A vibration measurement system typically comprises a **transducer (accelerometer), signal conditioner (charge amplifier or IEPE interface), and an analyzer (FFT spectrum analyzer or data acquisition system)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCPET502 – Module 1):**
> The student must be able to explain the working principle of seismic-type acceleration and vibration transducers, derive the governing equation of motion, identify the usable frequency range, classify the various types of accelerometers, and evaluate mounting and calibration techniques for engineering applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a car at a traffic signal. When the light turns green and the driver presses the accelerator, you are gently **pushed back into your seat**. Your body (the *seismic mass*) resists the change in motion due to its **inertia**, while the car seat (the *casing/housing*) moves forward. The relative motion between your body and the seat is what an accelerometer measures.

A more vivid physical analogy: **a mass hanging on a spring inside a box**.
- When the box accelerates forward, the mass lags behind due to inertia.
- The spring stretches, and the relative displacement between the mass and the box is proportional to the input acceleration.
- A displacement sensor between the mass and the box converts this into an electrical signal.

This is the essence of every **seismic accelerometer** — convert inertial relative motion into a measurable electrical quantity.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Frequency of vibration | $f$ | **Hz (cycles per second)** |
| Angular frequency | $\omega$ | **rad/s**, where $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| Acceleration | $a$ | **m/s²** or **g** |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta$ | **dimensionless**, ideal ≈ 0.6 – 0.7 for accelerometers |
| Sensitivity (piezo) | $S_q$ or $S_v$ | **pC/g** or **mV/g** |

> [!NOTE]
> The value of $g$ used in instrument calibration is the **standard gravity = 9.80665 m/s²** (defined by the International Committee for Weights and Measures, 1901). Always convert to SI when working through derivations.

### 1.4 Classification of Acceleration / Vibration Transducers

Vibration transducers are broadly classified based on the **reference frame** and the **output quantity**:

1. **Seismic (Inertial) Transducers** — use a proof mass-spring-damper system.
   - **Displacement Vibrometer** (seismometer) — measures relative displacement.
   - **Velocity Pickup (Velometer)** — measures relative velocity using a moving coil in a magnetic field.
   - **Accelerometer** — measures relative displacement proportional to acceleration when operated well below natural frequency.

2. **Non-Seismic Transducers** — measure absolute motion (e.g., Laser Doppler Vibrometer, capacitive probe with stationary frame).

3. **Based on transduction principle:**
   - **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** (most common, passive, high bandwidth).
   - **Piezoresistive Accelerometer** (DC response, used in crash testing).
   - **Capacitive Accelerometer** (MEMS, low cost, low power).
   - **Servo (Force-Balance) Accelerometer** (high accuracy, used in tilt & inertial navigation).
   - **Hall-Effect / LVDT-based Accelerometer** (industrial use).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seismic Mass–Spring–Damper Model

The foundational model for any inertial vibration transducer is a **single degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass-spring-damper system** subjected to a base excitation $y(t)$. The seismic (proof) mass $m$ is connected to the transducer casing by a spring of stiffness $k$ and a damper of damping coefficient $c$.

Let:
- $y(t)$ = absolute displacement of the **casing/base** (the input vibration).
- $x(t)$ = absolute displacement of the **seismic mass**.
- $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$ = **relative displacement** of the mass with respect to the casing.

### 2.2 Deriving the Governing Equation of Motion

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the mass:**

The forces acting on the seismic mass are the spring force and the damper force (both proportional to the *relative* motion $z$).

$$m \frac{d^{2} x}{dt^{2}} = -k \cdot z - c \cdot \frac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $x = y + z$:**

$$m \frac{d^{2} (y + z)}{dt^{2}} = -k \cdot z - c \cdot \frac{dz}{dt}$$

$$m \frac{d^{2} y}{dt^{2}} + m \frac{d^{2} z}{dt^{2}} = -k z - c \frac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange in standard form:**

$$m \frac{d^{2} z}{dt^{2}} + c \frac{dz}{dt} + k z = -m \frac{d^{2} y}{dt^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **right-hand side $-m \ddot{y}$** is the **inertial forcing term** — proportional to the input acceleration $\ddot{y}(t)$. This single equation is the heart of every seismic transducer analysis.

### 2.3 The Three Operating Regimes of a Seismic Transducer

Depending on the operating frequency $\omega$ relative to the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$:

| Regime | Frequency Condition | Quantity Measured | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Displacement Vibrometer** | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | Relative displacement $\approx$ base displacement | Seismology, low-frequency vibration |
| **Velocity Pickup** | $\omega \approx \omega_n$ (resonance) | Relative velocity $\approx$ base velocity | Industrial velocity probes |
| **Accelerometer** | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ (well below) | Relative displacement $\approx$ $-\frac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \cdot y$ (i.e., proportional to acceleration) | Machine condition monitoring, modal analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most important takeaway for KTU:** An accelerometer is **NOT** a device that directly measures acceleration mechanically. It measures **relative displacement** between a proof mass and its housing. It is the *transfer function* of the SDOF system that makes this relative displacement proportional to input acceleration, **provided** we operate well below the natural frequency.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | Sets the upper usable limit for an accelerometer |
| 2 | Natural frequency (Hz) | $f_{n} = \dfrac{\omega_{n}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Compare with signal of interest |
| 3 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{2 m \omega_{n}}$ | dimensionless | Accelerometers: $\zeta \approx 0.6 - 0.7$ |
| 4 | Critical damping | $c_{c} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2 m \omega_{n}$ | N·s/m | Minimum to prevent oscillation |
| 5 | Equation of motion | $m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$ | — | Base excitation model |
| 6 | Transfer function | $H(s) = \dfrac{Z(s)}{Y(s)} = \dfrac{-m s^{2}}{m s^{2} + c s + k}$ | — | From $Z$ to $Y$ |
| 7 | Magnitude ratio $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | $\dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}}}$ | dimensionless | (sign ignored) |
| 8 | Accelerometer regime | $\omega \ll \omega_{n} \Rightarrow z \approx -\dfrac{\ddot{y}}{\omega_{n}^{2}}$ | — | Output proportional to acceleration |
| 9 | Charge sensitivity (piezo) | $S_{q} = \dfrac{q}{a}$ | pC/g | Quoted on datasheets |
| 10 | Voltage sensitivity | $S_{v} = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | mV/g | After charge amplifier |
| 11 | Sensitivity to acceleration | $S_{a} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{a_{in}}$ | mV/g | End-to-end |
| 12 | Sensitivity to displacement (vibrometer) | $S_{d} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{y_{in}}$ | V/m or V/mm | |
| 13 | Usable frequency range (accelerometer) | $f_{max} \approx 0.4 \, f_{n}$ (with $\zeta = 0.7$) | Hz | Roughly 20% error tolerance |
| 14 | Lower usable limit (piezo) | $f_{min} \approx 0.01 - 1 \text{ Hz}$ (set by electronics) | Hz | |
| 15 | Cross-axis sensitivity | typically **< 5 %** | — | Ideally 0 % |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Predictive maintenance of rotating machinery:** Accelerometers mounted on motor housings detect bearing defects, imbalance, misalignment via vibration spectra.
- **Automotive crash testing:** Piezoresistive accelerometers measure the harsh decelerations in airbag deployment control units.
- **Aerospace & inertial navigation:** Servo accelerometers are the primary feedback in AHRS (Attitude Heading Reference Systems) and INS (Inertial Navigation Systems) — they are the basis of every smartphone's tilt detection.
- **Structural health monitoring (SHM):** MEMS accelerometers embedded in bridges and tall buildings monitor modal frequencies and damage.
- **Seismology:** Low-frequency seismometers use the same seismic principle to record ground displacement.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Equation of Motion to Transfer Function

We start from the SDOF base-excitation equation derived in §2.2:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$$

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of both sides.** We assume zero initial conditions ($z(0) = 0$, $\dot{z}(0) = 0$).

$$m s^{2} Z(s) + c s Z(s) + k Z(s) = -m s^{2} Y(s)$$

**Step 2 — Factor $Z(s)$ on the left-hand side.**

$$Z(s) \left[ m s^{2} + c s + k \right] = -m s^{2} Y(s)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{Z(s)}{Y(s)}$.**

$$H(s) = \frac{Z(s)}{Y(s)} = \frac{-m s^{2}}{m s^{2} + c s + k}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by dividing numerator and denominator by $m$.**

$$H(s) = \frac{-s^{2}}{s^{2} + \left( \dfrac{c}{m} \right) s + \dfrac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce standard notation.**
Recall: $\omega_{n}^{2} = \dfrac{k}{m}$ and $2 \zeta \omega_{n} = \dfrac{c}{m}$.

$$H(s) = \frac{-s^{2}}{s^{2} + 2 \zeta \omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The transfer function is a **high-pass filter of order 2** with respect to the input displacement $Y(s)$. As $s \to 0$ (DC), $H(s) \to 0$. As $s \to \infty$, $H(s) \to -1$. This means an accelerometer **cannot measure steady (DC) displacement** — it requires *changes* in motion, i.e., acceleration.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Frequency Response and Magnitude Ratio

Substitute $s = j \omega$ into the transfer function:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-(j\omega)^{2}}{(j\omega)^{2} + 2 \zeta \omega_{n} (j\omega) + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Simplify $(j\omega)^{2} = -\omega^{2}$.**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-(-\omega^{2})}{-\omega^{2} + 2 j \zeta \omega_{n} \omega + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{\omega^{2}}{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2}) + j (2 \zeta \omega_{n} \omega)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude (ignore sign for gain).**

$$\left\vert H(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{\omega^{2}}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2 \zeta \omega_{n} \omega)^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by defining the frequency ratio $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{n}}$.**

$$\left\vert H(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^{2})^{2} + (2 \zeta r)^{2}}}$$

---

### 3.3 Behaviour in the Three Regimes

#### **Regime A — $\omega \ll \omega_n$ (Accelerometer Region, $r \to 0$)**

For $r \ll 1$:

$$(1 - r^{2})^{2} \approx 1, \quad (2 \zeta r)^{2} \approx 0$$

$$\left\vert H(j\omega) \right\vert \approx \frac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} = r^{2}$$

Thus the **relative displacement amplitude** is:

$$Z_{0} = \frac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \cdot Y_{0} = \frac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} \cdot Y_{0}$$

Since the input acceleration amplitude is $A_{0} = \omega^{2} Y_{0}$:

$$Z_{0} = \frac{A_{0}}{\omega_{n}^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the key result:** In the accelerometer region, the **relative displacement is directly proportional to the input acceleration** with a constant of proportionality equal to $\dfrac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}}$. This is why a stiffness-mounted, low-$\omega_n$ seismic mass behaves as an accelerometer.

---

#### **Regime B — $\omega \approx \omega_n$ (Velocity Pickup Region, $r = 1$)**

At resonance, $(1 - r^2) = 0$:

$$\left\vert H(j\omega_{n}) \right\vert = \frac{1}{2 \zeta}$$

The amplitude is amplified by $\dfrac{1}{2 \zeta}$. For small damping, this resonance peak is large. Devices tuned to this region respond most strongly to base velocity, and the output is **proportional to velocity** (90° phase shift exists between input and output).

---

#### **Regime C — $\omega \gg \omega_n$ (Vibrometer Region, $r \to \infty$)**

For $r \gg 1$:

$$(1 - r^{2})^{2} \approx r^{4}, \quad (2 \zeta r)^{2} \ll r^{4}$$

$$\left\vert H(j\omega) \right\vert \approx \frac{r^{2}}{r^{2}} = 1$$

So $Z_{0} \approx Y_{0}$ — the mass stays *stationary in space* (a near-infinite reference), and the relative displacement equals the input displacement. This is the **vibrometer** or **seismometer** regime.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Numerical)

> **[Problem]** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of $m = 50$ g and a spring stiffness of $k = 1.0 \times 10^{6}$ N/m. Calculate:
> (a) the natural frequency,
> (b) the critical damping coefficient,
> (c) the relative displacement when subjected to an acceleration of 5 g at 50 Hz (assume accelerometer regime).

**Given:** $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$, $k = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}$, $a_{in} = 5g = 49.033 \text{ m/s}^{2}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

#### **(a) Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^{6}}{0.05}} = \sqrt{2.0 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\omega_{n} = 4472.14 \text{ rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{n} = \frac{\omega_{n}}{2\pi} = 711.76 \text{ Hz}$$

#### **(b) Critical Damping Coefficient**

$$c_{c} = 2 \sqrt{k m} = 2 \sqrt{(1.0 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (0.05)}$$

$$c_{c} = 2 \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{4}} = 2 \times 223.607 = 447.21 \text{ N·s/m}$$

#### **(c) Relative Displacement at 50 Hz**

Since $f = 50 \text{ Hz} \ll f_{n} = 711.76 \text{ Hz}$, the accelerometer regime applies.

$$z_{0} = \frac{a_{in}}{\omega_{n}^{2}} = \frac{49.033}{(4472.14)^{2}} = \frac{49.033}{2.0 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$z_{0} = 2.4517 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2.45 \; \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** Input displacement amplitude $Y_0 = a_{in}/\omega^{2} = 49.033 / (2\pi \cdot 50)^{2} = 4.969 \times 10^{-4}$ m. Thus $Z_0 / Y_0 = 4.93 \times 10^{-3}$, which equals $(\omega/\omega_n)^2 = (50/711.76)^2 = 4.93 \times 10^{-3}$ — consistent with the high-pass behavior.

### 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Input Quantity | Frequency | Transfer Function Simplifies To | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration $A_0$ | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | $\dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}}$ (per unit $Y_0$) | $Z_0 = A_0 / \omega_n^2$ — **Accelerometer** |
| Velocity $V_0 = \omega Y_0$ | $\omega \approx \omega_n$ | $\dfrac{1}{2 \zeta}$ | Resonance peak — **Velometer** |
| Displacement $Y_0$ | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | $1$ | $Z_0 = Y_0$ — **Vibrometer** |
