# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. State one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Air/Oil supply | One port (cap end only) | Two ports (cap & rod) |
| Return stroke | By external spring or load | By pressurized fluid |
| Force direction | One direction only | Both directions |
| Application | Hydraulic jack for car lifting | Hydraulic press for sheet metal stamping |

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for differentiation table, 1 for application, 0.5 for neatness/diagram.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2.** List the three functional blocks of an FRL unit and state the purpose of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Filter** — Removes particulate contamination (≥ 5 µm) from compressed air; protects downstream valves and actuators. (1 mark)
2. **Regulator** — Maintains constant downstream pressure (e.g., 6 bar) despite fluctuations in upstream supply; uses diaphragm-spring mechanism. (1 mark)
3. **Lubricator** — Injects fine oil mist into the air stream to lubricate moving parts of downstream pneumatic tools and cylinders. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Hydraulic System Design**

(a) **Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an axial piston pump. State its advantages and applications.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The axial piston pump consists of:
- A **cylindrical barrel** rotating about its axis, containing 7 or 9 pistons arranged axially in bores.
- A **swash plate** (or bent-axis mechanism) inclined at an angle $\beta$ (typically 15°–25°).
- **Pistons** connected to the swash plate via slipper pads or ball joints.
- **Valve plate** with kidney-shaped inlet and outlet ports.

**Working (2 marks):**
As the barrel rotates, each piston slides in its bore. The inclined swash plate causes the pistons to:
- **Retract** (creating suction, drawing oil from the inlet port) during half-revolution.
- **Extend** (discharging oil through the outlet port at high pressure) during the other half.

**Displacement per revolution:** $V_D = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot Z \cdot e$, where $e$ = piston stroke = $D_{b} \tan \beta$ ($D_b$ = barrel pitch diameter).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Highest working pressure (up to **420 bar**).
- Variable displacement by changing swash plate angle.
- High overall efficiency (~90–95%).

**Applications (1 mark):**
- CNC machine tool feed drives, mobile hydraulics (excavators), aircraft systems.

---

(b) **A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 50 mm. The pump supplies oil at 160 bar, and the back pressure is 8 bar. If the friction force is 1200 N, calculate: (i) the extension force, (ii) the retraction force, and (iii) the extension velocity if the pump flow is 40 LPM.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.08$ m, $d = 0.05$ m
- $P_1 = 160 \times 10^5$ Pa, $P_2 = 8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $F_f = 1200$ N, $Q = 40$ LPM $= 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**
$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi [(0.08)^2 - (0.05)^2]}{4} = 3.067 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension force (i):**
$$F_{ext} = P_1 A_p - P_2 A_r - F_f$$
$$F_{ext} = (160 \times 10^5)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (8 \times 10^5)(3.067 \times 10^{-3}) - 1200$$
$$F_{ext} = 80{,}432 - 2453.6 - 1200 = 76{,}778.4 \text{ N} \approx 76.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Retraction force (ii):**
$$F_{ret} = P_1 A_r - P_2 A_p - F_f$$
$$F_{ret} = (160 \times 10^5)(3.067 \times 10^{-3}) - (8 \times 10^5)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - 1200$$
$$F_{ret} = 49{,}072 - 4021.6 - 1200 = 43{,}850.4 \text{ N} \approx 43.85 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Extension velocity (iii):**
$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.96 \text{ m/min}$$

**Answers:** (i) $F_{ext} = 76.78$ kN, (ii) $F_{ret} = 43.85$ kN, (iii) $v_{ext} = 0.133$ m/s.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Pneumatic System & Components**

(a) **Describe with a neat sketch the working of a reciprocating air compressor. State the role of an intercooler in a multi-stage compressor.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A reciprocating compressor has:
- A **cylinder** with inlet (suction) valve and outlet (delivery) valve (both one-way).
- A **piston** driven by a crankshaft (via connecting rod).
- **Crankshaft** driven by an electric motor or IC engine.
- **Flywheel** for smooth rotation; **lubrication system** for piston rings.

**Working — Single-Stage (3 marks):**
1. **Suction stroke:** Piston moves down (TDC to BDC), inlet valve opens, atmospheric air at $P_1$ enters the cylinder (volume = $V_1$).
2. **Compression stroke:** Piston moves up (BDC to TDC), both valves close, air is compressed polytropically to $P_2, V_2$.
3. **Discharge stroke:** When cylinder pressure exceeds receiver pressure, outlet valve opens, compressed air is delivered to the storage tank.

**Multi-stage with intercooler (2 marks):**
In a two-stage compressor, the air is compressed in a low-pressure (LP) cylinder, then **cooled back to near-atmospheric temperature in an intercooler** before entering the high-pressure (HP) cylinder. **Role of intercooler:**
- Reduces the specific volume of air entering HP cylinder → **less work required**.
- Removes heat of compression → improves **volumetric efficiency**.
- Reduces discharge temperature → safer operation, longer valve life.
- Approximates **isothermal compression** (lowest theoretical work).

**Mathematical justification:** For ideal isothermal: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$. For adiabatic: $W_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$. Multi-stage + intercooling approaches $W_{iso}$, which is the minimum work.

---

(b) **A single-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore of 75 mm and a stroke of 250 mm. It operates at 6 bar gauge pressure. If the cylinder completes 25 cycles per minute, calculate the free air consumption in m³/min. Take atmospheric pressure as 1.013 bar.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.075$ m, $L = 0.25$ m
- $P_g = 6$ bar, $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar
- $n = 25$ cycles/min

**Step 1 — Geometric (stroke) volume of cylinder:**
$$V_g = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2 (0.25)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.005625 \times 0.25}{4}$$
$$V_g = 1.104 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 1.104 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Absolute working pressure:**
$$P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm} = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Free air volume per stroke (Boyle's Law):**
$$V_{free} = V_g \times \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} = 1.104 \times \frac{7.013}{1.013} = 1.104 \times 6.924 = 7.644 \text{ L/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Total free air consumption per minute:**
$$V_{FAM} = V_{free} \times n = 7.644 \times 25 = 191.1 \text{ L/min} = 0.1911 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Compressor sizing (with 25% margin):**
$$Q_{comp} = 1.25 \times 0.1911 = 0.2389 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \approx 0.24 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Answer:** Free air consumption = **0.191 m³/min**; recommended compressor capacity ≈ **0.24 m³/min** (≈ 8.45 CFM).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 to –3 marks):** Forgetting to convert bar to Pa (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa) and LPM to m³/s. Always do unit conversion as Step 1.
> 2. **Pneumatic absolute pressure trap (–1 to –2 marks):** Gauge pressure ≠ absolute pressure. When applying Boyle's law in a pneumatic cylinder, **always add atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar)** to get $P_{abs}$.
> 3. **Wrong area for retraction:** In double-acting cylinders, students often use $A_p$ for both strokes. The rod side uses **annular area** $A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$. This is a **classic 2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Ignoring friction force $F_f$** in numerical problems → **–1 mark** loss.
> 5. **Forgetting the cushioning device in sketches** → examiner deducts **0.5–1 mark** for incomplete diagram.
> 6. **Writing 4/3 valve without specifying "closed center / tandem center / float center"** → **–1 mark** for incomplete DCV naming.
> 7. **Pneumatic free air:** Examiner checks if you correctly multiplied by $P_{abs}/P_{atm}$ ratio — skipping this step is **–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Hydraulic & Pneumatic Actuation: Quick-Fire Recap**

### 🔑 Core Principles
- **Pascal's Law:** Pressure is transmitted undiminished in an incompressible fluid.
- **Boyle's / Polytropic Law:** $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ — governs pneumatics.
- **Continuity:** $Q = A v$ — flow rate = area × velocity.

### 🔧 Key Components
- **Power source:** Pump (hydraulic) / Compressor (pneumatic).
- **Conditioning:** FRL unit (Filter → Regulator → Lubricator).
- **Control:** 4/3 DCV, 3/2 DCV, Pressure Relief Valve, Flow Control Valve.
- **Output:** Single/Double-acting cylinder, hydraulic motor, rotary actuator.
- **Storage:** Accumulator (hydraulic), Air Receiver Tank (pneumatic).

### 📊 Numerical Must-Knows
- Cylinder forces: $F_{ext} = P_1 A_p - P_2 A_r - F_f$
- Retraction force: $F_{ret} = P_1 A_r - P_2 A_p - F_f$
- Pump flow: $Q = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$
- Motor torque: $T = \frac{P \cdot V_D \cdot \eta_m}{2\pi}$
- Accumulator: $P_0 V_0 = P_1 V_1$ (isothermal)
- Free air: $V_{free} = V_g \cdot \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}}$

### ⚠️ Standard Pressures
- Hydraulic industrial: **70–350 bar**
- Pneumatic industrial: **4–8 bar**
- Aircraft hydraulic: **210 bar (3000 psi)**
- Automotive power steering: **100–150 bar**

### 🎯 Selection Heuristics
- **High force, precise control, low speed** → Hydraulic
- **Light, fast, clean, low cost, safe** → Pneumatic
- **Hazardous (mining, refinery)** → Pneumatic (no fire risk, no oil)
- **Food/Pharma** → Pneumatic (oil-free) or special food-grade hydraulic fluid

### 🧠 Common KTU Exam Keywords to Memorize
- *Swash plate angle* → controls displacement of axial piston pump.
- *Cushioning* → reduces end-of-stroke impact.
- *Tandem center* → locks cylinder in position (no pump unloading).
- *Float center* → allows free flow to tank (full unloading of pump).
- *Meter-in* → throttle on inlet side (controls extension speed).
- *Meter-out* → throttle on outlet side (controls retraction speed, smoother).
- *Pre-charge pressure* $P_0$ in accumulator: typically **0.9 × min working pressure**.

### 🛠️ Maintenance & Safety Points
- Hydraulic oil change: every **2000–4000 hours** (or 1 year).
- Filter element: replace when $\Delta P$ across filter > **0.5 bar**.
- Pneumatic drain traps: drain daily to prevent water carryover.
- Maximum recommended sound level: **85 dB(A)** at operator station.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Actuation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Electrical Actuation System ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrical Actuation System (KTU 2024 Definition)** > An **Electrical Actuation System** is a mechatronic subsystem that converts **electrical energy** into **mechanical energy** (linear or rotary motion) using electromagnetic, electrostatic, or piezoelectric principles. It serves as the *muscle* of any mechatronic system, executing the control commands generated by the controller and acting upon the physical process (load) via mechanical linkages. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (NEP 2020 aligned), the actuation system is the **final control element** in the mechatronic signal chain: **Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Controller (Microcontroller/PLC) → Driver (Power Electronics) → Actuator → Mechanical Load.** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like your hand picking up a glass of water.** > Your *brain* (controller) decides to lift the glass. Your *nerves* (signal wires + driver) carry the command. Your *bicep muscle* (electrical actuator) contracts, generating force. The *forearm* (mechanical linkage) transmits this force, and your *fingers* (end-effector) grasp the glass. **Replace the bicep with a DC motor, stepper motor, or solenoid — and you have an electrical actuation system in a robot, CNC machine, or 3D printer.** ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (as per KTU Module 1 syllabus) | Class | Principle | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **DC Servo Motor** | Electromagnetic commutation | Robotics, CNC feed drives | | **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** | Electronic commutation | Drones, EVs, hard drives | | **Stepper Motor** | Discrete step rotation | 3D printers, plotters | | **Solenoid** | Linear electromagnetic push/pull | Valves, door locks | | **Relay** | Electromagnetic switching | High-power switching | | **Voice Coil Actuator** | Lorentz force, limited stroke | Hard disk head positioning | | **Piezoelectric Actuator** | Inverse piezoelectric effect | Nanopositioning, inkjet | ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold Highlight) - **Permeability of free space (μ₀) = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** - **Force per unit current in a magnetic field: F = BIL** (where **B** is flux density in **Tesla (T)**, **I** is current in **Amperes (A)**, **L** is conductor length in **meters (m)**) - **Power rating** of actuators is expressed in **Watts (W)**, torque in **Newton-meters (N·m)**, speed in **RPM** or **rad/s**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Universal Electromagnetic Force Law (The Heart of All Electromagnetic Actuators) Every motor, solenoid, and voice coil ultimately derives its mechanical force from the **Lorentz Force Law**: $$\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$$ **Breakdown of the logic:** - **Why?** A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force. This is the fundamental physical phenomenon. - **How?** The current (I) flows through a conductor of length (L) perpendicular to magnetic flux density (B). The cross product dictates the force direction (given by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule). - **Magnitude (when L ⊥ B):** F = BIL - For a coil of N turns in a uniform field: F = NBIL ### 2.2 The Universal Motor Torque Law (Lorentz Torque) When a current-carrying coil rotates in a magnetic field, force becomes torque: $$\tau = N B I A \sin(\theta)$$ Where: - **τ** = electromagnetic torque (N·m) - **N** = number of turns - **B** = flux density (T) - **I** = current (A) - **A** = coil area (m²) - **θ** = angle between B and coil normal (rad) > [!NOTE] > For a DC motor, commutation ensures the current switches at θ = 90°, so the effective torque becomes constant: **τ = K_t · I**, where **K_t** is the **motor torque constant** (N·m/A). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol / Formula | Units | KTU 2024 Critical Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Lorentz Force | F = BIL | N | Foundation of all motor action | | 2 | Lorentz Torque | τ = K_t · I | N·m | Linear in current for DC motors | | 3 | Back-EMF | e = K_e · ω | V | Opposes applied voltage (Lenz's Law) | | 4 | DC Motor Armature Voltage Eqn. | V = I_a R_a + L_a (dI_a/dt) + e | V | Must include inductance transient term | | 5 | DC Motor Mechanical Eqn. | J(dω/dt) + Bω + τ_L = τ_m | N·m | Standard 2nd-order rotational system | | 6 | Steady-State Speed | ω_ss = (V − I_a R_a) / K_e | rad/s | Used to derive torque-speed curve | | 7 | No-Load Speed | ω_0 = V / K_e | rad/s | When τ_L = 0 | | 8 | Stall Torque | τ_s = K_t · V / R_a | N·m | When ω = 0 | | 9 | Stepper Motor Step Angle | θ_step = 360° / (N_s · N_p) | degrees | N_s = stator teeth, N_p = rotor pole pairs | | 10 | Stepper Holding Torque | τ_h ∝ N_s · I² | N·m | Quadratic in phase current | | 11 | Solenoid Force | F = (μ₀ N² I² A) / (2 g²) | N | g = air gap length | | 12 | Solenoid Inductance | L = μ₀ N² A / g | H | Inductance grows as gap closes | | 13 | Piezoelectric Displacement | ΔL = d_33 · V · n | m | d_33 = strain coefficient, n = disc count | | 14 | Motor Power (Mechanical) | P_m = τ · ω | W | Useful output power | | 15 | Motor Efficiency | η = P_m / P_electrical | unitless | Always < 100% | | 16 | Transfer Function (DC Motor) | ω(s)/V(s) = K_t / [(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e] | rad/s/V | Classic mechatronics block | | 17 | Time Constant (Armature) | τ_a = L_a / R_a | s | Electrical time constant | | 18 | Time Constant (Mechanical) | τ_m = J / B | s | Mechanical time constant | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall Note on Constants:** In SI units, the **motor torque constant K_t** and **back-EMF constant K_e** are numerically equal: **K_t (N·m/A) = K_e (V·s/rad)**. Many students write them as different quantities. They are the *same physical constant* in two different units (energy conservation). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Robotics:** DC servo and BLDC motors drive joints with closed-loop PID feedback from encoders. - **CNC Machines:** Stepper and servo motors position tools with micrometer accuracy. - **Automotive (EVs):** BLDC and PMSM motors (power levels up to 250 kW) drive traction. - **Medical Devices:** Piezoelectric actuators enable micro-surgery and drug delivery micropumps. - **Aerospace:** Voice coil actuators position flight control surfaces with millisecond response. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Motor Transfer Function (Most Asked KTU Derivative) **Starting Point — Two Governing Physical Laws:** **(a) Electrical (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around armature circuit):** $$V(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e(t)$$ **Where:** V = applied armature voltage (V), R_a = armature resistance (Ω), L_a = armature inductance (H), i_a = armature current (A), e = back-EMF (V). **(b) Mechanical (Newton's 2nd Law for rotation):** $$J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B\omega(t) + \tau_L(t) = \tau_m(t)$$ **Where:** J = moment of inertia (kg·m²), B = viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad), ω = angular velocity (rad/s), τ_L = load torque (N·m), τ_m = motor electromagnetic torque (N·m). **(c) Coupling Laws (electromechanical energy conversion):** $$\tau_m(t) = K_t i_a(t)$$ $$e(t) = K_e \omega(t)$$ **Step 1: Take the Laplace transform of the electrical equation** (assuming zero initial conditions): $$V(s) = R_a I_a(s) + L_a s I_a(s) + K_e \Omega(s)$$ $$V(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_e \Omega(s)$$ **Step 2: Solve for I_a(s):** $$I_a(s) = \frac{V(s) - K_e \Omega(s)}{L_a s + R_a}$$ **Step 3: Take the Laplace transform of the mechanical equation:** $$J s \Omega(s) + B \Omega(s) + T_L(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$ $$(J s + B) \Omega(s) + T_L(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$ **Step 4: Substitute I_a(s) from Step 2 into Step 3:** $$(J s + B) \Omega(s) + T_L(s) = K_t \cdot \frac{V(s) - K_e \Omega(s)}{L_a s + R_a}$$ **Step 5: Multiply both sides by (L_a s + R_a) to clear the denominator:** $$(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) \Omega(s) + (L_a s + R_a) T_L(s) = K_t V(s) - K_t K_e \Omega(s)$$ **Step 6: Collect Ω(s) terms on the left-hand side:** $$[(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_t K_e] \Omega(s) = K_t V(s) - (L_a s + R_a) T_L(s)$$ **Step 7: Solve for the transfer function ω(s)/V(s)** (ignoring load torque T_L(s) for control design): $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_t K_e}$$ **Step 8: Expand the denominator to standard 2nd-order form:** $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_e)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the canonical KTU 2024 exam result for DC motor control.** The denominator shows that the DC motor is inherently a **2nd-order system** with two energy-storing elements: the armature inductance (electrical) and the rotor inertia (mechanical). ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stepper Motor Step Angle **Given:** A hybrid stepper motor with N_s = 50 stator teeth per phase and N_p = 50 rotor pole teeth. **Step 1: Total number of effective steps per revolution for a full-step drive:** $$N_{steps} = N_s \cdot N_p = 50 \cdot 50 = 2500 \text{ steps/rev}$$ **Step 2: Step angle (mechanical degrees per step):** $$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}} = \frac{360°}{2500} = 0.144°/\text{step}$$ **Step 3: In radians:** $$\theta_{step} = \frac{2\pi}{2500} = 2.513 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/step}$$ **Step 4: If microstepping with m = 10 microsteps per full step:** $$\theta_{micro} = \frac{0.144°}{10} = 0.0144°/\text{microstep}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Solenoid Force from Energy Method **Energy Stored in Magnetic Field:** $$W = \frac{1}{2} L i^2$$ **Where Inductance of an N-turn solenoid with air gap g:** $$L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g}$$ **Step 1: Substituting L into the energy equation:** $$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g} \cdot i^2 = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A i^2}{2 g}$$ **Step 2: The magnetic force is the negative gradient of co-energy with respect to displacement x = g (air gap):** $$F = \frac{\partial W}{\partial g} \bigg|_{i=\text{const}}$$ **Step 3: Differentiating W with respect to g:** $$F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A i^2}{2 g^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Solenoid force is **inversely proportional to the SQUARE of the air gap**. This means as the plunger closes the gap, force grows quadratically — the "snap action" characteristic that makes solenoids excellent for fast valve actuation. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Electrical Actuators | Parameter | DC Servo | BLDC | Stepper | Solenoid | Piezo | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Motion Type** | Rotary, continuous | Rotary, continuous | Rotary, discrete | Linear, ON/OFF | Linear, nanometer | | **Precision** | High (with encoder) | High (with Hall sensors) | Open-loop precise | Low | Ultra-high | | **Holding Torque** | Zero at rest (gear) | Zero (no excitation) | **High (detent)** | High (when energized) | High | | **Speed Range** | 0–10,000 RPM | 0–100,000 RPM | 0–2,000 RPM | < 100 ms stroke | kHz bandwidth | | **Driver Complexity** | Medium (H-bridge) | High (3-phase inverter) | Medium (unipolar/bipolar) | Simple (relay/MOSFET) | High (HV amp) | | **Efficiency** | 60–75% | 85–95% | 30–60% | 40–60% | 90%+ | | **Cost** | Medium | High | Low | Very low | Very high | | **Typical KTUTopic** | Robotics | Drones, EVs | 3D printers | Valves, locks | Nanopositioning | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Electrical Actuation System ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CONTROLLER
Microcontroller or PLC"] B["DRIVER STAGE
H-Bridge or 3-Phase Inverter"] C["ACTUATOR
DC Motor or Stepper or BLDC"] D["MECHANICAL LOAD
Joint or Wheel or Valve"] E["FEEDBACK SENSOR
Encoder or Resolver or Hall"] A -- PWM Control Signal --> B B -- High Current Power --> C C -- Torque and Rotation --> D D -- Position and Speed --> E E -- Encoder Pulses or Hall Signals --> A style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style C fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff style D fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff style E fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 DC Motor H-Bridge Driver Circuit Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB VCC["DC Supply V+"] GND["Ground 0V"] Q1["MOSFET Q1 High-Side Left"] Q2["MOSFET Q2 Low-Side Left"] Q3["MOSFET Q3 High-Side Right"] Q4["MOSFET Q4 Low-Side Right"] M["DC Motor Armature"] PWM1["PWM Input IN1"] PWM2["PWM Input IN2"] VCC --- Q1 Q1 --- M M --- Q4 Q4 --- GND VCC --- Q3 Q3 --- M M --- Q2 Q2 --- GND PWM1 -. Controls .-> Q1 PWM1 -. Controls .-> Q2 PWM2 -. Controls .-> Q3 PWM2 -. Controls .-> Q4 style VCC fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style GND fill:#374151,color:#ffffff style M fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style Q1 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style Q2 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style Q3 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style Q4 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Stepper Motor Sequencing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["Step 0: Phase A ON, Phase B OFF
Current in Coil A only"] S1["Step 1: Phase A ON, Phase B ON
Both coils energized"] S2["Step 2: Phase A OFF, Phase B ON
Current in Coil B only"] S3["Step 3: Phase A OFF, Phase B OFF
Detent position hold"] S4["Step 4: Sequence Repeats
Full revolution after N_steps"] S0 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 -. Return to .-> S0 style S0 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#9f1239,color:#ffffff style S4 fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.4 Solenoid Actuation Working Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Energized Coil
Current I flows"] A2["Magnetic Field Builds
in Air Gap g"] A3["Plunger Pulled In
Force F proportional to 1 by g squared"] A4["Air Gap Collapses to minimum"] A5["Coil De-energized
Spring Returns Plunger"] A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A5 -. Next cycle .-> A1 style A1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style A2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style A3 fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff style A4 fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff style A5 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.5 Closed-Loop Servo Control System (KTU High-Yield Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR REF["Reference Position
theta_ref"] SUM["Summing Junction
Error Detector"] PID["PID Controller
C s Transfer Function"] DRV["Power Driver
H-Bridge"] MOT["DC Servo Motor
G s Transfer Function"] LD["Load and Gear Train
1 over J s plus B"] FBK["Position Sensor
Encoder Gain K_s"] OUT["Output Position
theta_actual"] REF --> SUM SUM -- Error e --> PID PID -- Control u --> DRV DRV -- Voltage V --> MOT MOT -- Torque tau --> LD LD --> OUT OUT --> FBK FBK -- Feedback --> SUM style REF fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style SUM fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style PID fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style DRV fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style MOT fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff style LD fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff style FBK fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style OUT fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define an electrical actuation system. List any four types of electrical actuators used in mechatronic systems. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** An electrical actuation system is a device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (linear or rotary motion) to perform a useful task in a mechatronic system, driven by the controller via a power electronic driver. - **Four Types (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **DC Servo Motor** — closed-loop rotary actuation 2. **Stepper Motor** — open-loop discrete-step rotation 3. **Solenoid** — linear push/pull electromagnetic actuator 4. **Piezoelectric Actuator** — nanometer-precision linear motion --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State and explain the Lorentz force law. How is it fundamental to electrical actuation? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Statement (1.5 marks):** The Lorentz force law states that the force experienced by a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field is given by **F = BIL**, where B is the magnetic flux density (T), I is the current (A), and L is the effective length of the conductor (m) perpendicular to the field. - **Relation to Actuation (1.5 marks):** All electromagnetic actuators (DC motors, stepper motors, solenoids, voice coils) generate mechanical force or torque using this principle. The conductor carrying current interacts with the magnetic field of a permanent magnet or electromagnet, producing the mechanical motion that drives the load. The direction of force follows **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 1] — Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC motor. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks) **(b)** A DC servo motor has the following specifications: R_a = 2 Ω, L_a = 0.5 H, K_t = 0.05 N·m/A, K_e = 0.05 V·s/rad, J = 0.01 kg·m², B = 0.001 N·m·s/rad. Determine: (i) No-load speed when V = 24 V. (ii) Stall torque. (iii) Time constant of the system. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** *(Full derivation as given in Section 3.1 above — examiner expects students to: state both governing equations, perform Laplace transform, eliminate I_a(s), and arrive at the standard 2nd-order transfer function. Each step carries partial marks.)* **Final Result:** $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_e)}$$ **Assumptions (0.5 marks):** Linear magnetic circuit, negligible saturation, friction modeled as viscous (linear in ω), no external load torque during transfer function derivation. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) No-Load Speed (2 Marks):** At no load, τ_L = 0. From the mechanical equation at steady state (dω/dt = 0): $$B\omega_0 = \tau_m = K_t i_a$$ And from electrical equation at steady state (di_a/dt = 0): $$V = i_a R_a + K_e \omega_0$$ Solving simultaneously: $$i_a = \frac{V - K_e \omega_0}{R_a}$$ Substituting: $$B\omega_0 = K_t \cdot \frac{V - K_e \omega_0}{R_a}$$ $$B R_a \omega_0 = K_t V - K_t K_e \omega_0$$ $$\omega_0 (B R_a + K_t K_e) = K_t V$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{K_t V}{B R_a + K_t K_e}$$ **Plugging in values:** - B R_a = 0.001 × 2 = 0.002 - K_t K_e = 0.05 × 0.05 = 0.0025 - Numerator: 0.05 × 24 = 1.2 $$\omega_0 = \frac{1.2}{0.002 + 0.0025} = \frac{1.2}{0.0045} = 266.67 \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$\omega_0 = 266.67 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 2546.5 \text{ RPM}$$ **(ii) Stall Torque (2.5 Marks):** At stall, ω = 0, so back-EMF e = 0. All applied voltage drops across armature resistance: $$i_{a,stall} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{24}{2} = 12 \text{ A}$$ $$\tau_{stall} = K_t \cdot i_{a,stall} = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6 \text{ N·m}$$ **(iii) Time Constant (2.5 Marks):** Electrical time constant: $$\tau_a = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ s}$$ Mechanical time constant: $$\tau_m = \frac{J}{B} = \frac{0.01}{0.001} = 10 \text{ s}$$ Overall dominant time constant (since τ_m >> τ_a): $$\tau_{dominant} \approx \tau_m = 10 \text{ s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Using the formula ω_0 = V / K_e directly without considering the friction term B. This formula is only valid when B = 0. (Lose 1 mark) > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert rad/s to RPM when the question expects RPM, or vice versa. Always check the unit convention. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the no-load speed with the no-load speed under rated conditions. The friction B causes a small drop from V/K_e. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Using K_t ≠ K_e in calculations. In SI units, K_t = K_e numerically — do not double the constant. --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1] — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a permanent magnet (PM) stepper motor with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for step angle. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare stepper motor and DC servo motor based on: open vs closed loop, holding torque, speed range, applications, and cost. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) PM Stepper Motor (7 Marks):** **Construction (3 Marks):** A PM stepper motor consists of: - **Stator:** Made of laminated silicon steel with salient poles (teeth). It carries the field windings. The stator is divided into two phases (Phase A and Phase B), each with its own set of windings. - **Rotor:** A permanent magnet (usually ceramic or rare-earth) with alternating N and S poles around its circumference. - **Bearings and Shaft:** For smooth rotation. - **Lead Wires:** For connecting the stator windings to the driver circuit. **Working (3 Marks):** When the stator windings are energized in a specific sequence, the stator poles become magnetized (alternating N and S). The permanent magnet rotor aligns itself to minimize the magnetic reluctance, causing rotation. By sequentially switching the current between Phase A and Phase B, the rotor rotates in discrete steps. **For a 2-phase PM stepper with 50 rotor teeth, the step angle is 360°/50 = 7.2°/step in full-step mode.** **Step Angle Derivation (1 Mark):** $$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_p}$$ Where N_s = number of stator teeth per phase, N_p = number of rotor pole teeth. **(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1.4 marks per point):** | Parameter | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor | |---|---|---| | **Open vs Closed Loop** | Open-loop (no feedback needed) | Closed-loop (requires encoder) | | **Holding Torque** | High detent torque at standstill | Zero (requires brake or gear) | | **Speed Range** | Low to medium (0–2000 RPM) | Wide range (0–10,000+ RPM) | | **Applications** | 3D printers, plotters, small CNC | Robotics joints, CNC, automation | | **Cost** | Low (no feedback sensor) | High (encoder + driver) | | **Position Error** | Can lose steps under overload | Corrected automatically by feedback | | **Efficiency** | Low–Medium (30–60%) | Higher (60–75%) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Stepper Questions:** > - **Mistake #1:** Confusing **PM stepper** with **VR (Variable Reluctance) stepper**. VR steppers have a soft iron rotor (not permanent magnet) and rely purely on reluctance variation. > - **Mistake #2:** Forgetting the factor of 2 for full-step mode. In half-step mode, the step angle halves. > - **Mistake #3:** Not mentioning **detent torque** in holding torque comparison. Stepper motors hold position by detent + magnetic locking even without excitation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Bulletins:** - ✅ **Lorentz Force F = BIL** is the foundation — derive motor torque and solenoid force from it. - ✅ **K_t = K_e** in SI units — same constant, different units. Energy conservation. - ✅ **DC Motor Transfer Function** is **2nd order** with two energy stores: L_a (electrical) and J (mechanical). - ✅ **Back-EMF e = K_e ω** is the **opposition voltage** that limits speed — it is *why* DC motors self-regulate. - ✅ **Step Angle** = 360° / (N_s × N_p). Microstepping divides this further. - ✅ **Solenoid Force** ∝ **1/g²** (inverse square of air gap) — explains the snap action. - ✅ **Piezo Displacement** = d_33 × V × n — voltage-driven, no current, no magnetic field. - ✅ **H-Bridge** is mandatory for bidirectional DC motor control — 4 switches, never shoot-through. - ✅ **Stepper motors are open-loop** but lose steps if overloaded; **DC servos are closed-loop** and self-correct. - ✅ **Voice coil actuators** have linear I-to-F relationship, no cogging, and are used in HDDs. - ✅ **BLDC motors** use electronic commutation (3-phase inverter) — higher efficiency, longer life than brushed DC. - ✅ For KTU 2024 numerical problems, **always state units** in the final answer — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. - ✅ **Solenoid force equation F = μ₀N²Ai²/(2g²)** — remember the **square of current** and **inverse square of gap**. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Electrical Actuation System** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*

topics.title: Data Presentation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Presentation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Data Presentation System (DPS)** is the final stage of a measurement or mechatronic system that converts processed electrical signals into a human-perceivable form — visual, audible, or permanent record — for the purpose of monitoring, analysis, decision-making, and control feedback. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it forms the **output/termination element** of the generalized measurement chain:

> *Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Data Processor → **Data Presentation System** → Human Operator / Storage*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Data Presentation System encompasses **Display Devices** (analog meters, oscilloscopes, LED/LCD, 7-segment, dot-matrix), **Recording Devices** (strip-chart, X-Y, magnetic tape, data loggers), and **Modern HMI/Computer-based Presentation** (VDU, SCADA, touch screens, virtual instrumentation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **Data Presentation System as the "Face" of a mechatronic machine**.

- Imagine a car dashboard: the speedometer needle, the fuel gauge, the digital odometer, and the warning beeper are all *Data Presentation Systems*. They take invisible engine parameters (rotational speed, fuel level, distance) and convert them into something the driver can **see, hear, and understand** in real time.
- Just like a teacher writing on a blackboard, the DPS **"presents"** the internal numerical/physical data of the system in a format that the human brain (or a downstream recorder) can interpret.
- Without a presentation system, even the most accurate sensor and processor is like a brilliant mind with no mouth — it knows the answer but cannot communicate it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — ISO/IEC Vocabulary:**
> "A device or system that provides a perceptible representation of data, whether as a transient visual indication, an audible signal, a printed record, or a digital storage medium."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Display luminance | **50 – 1000 cd/m²** | Readability in various lighting |
| Response time of LCD | **10 – 100 ms** | Real-time display capability |
| Human eye persistence | **~ 1/16 s (≈ 62.5 ms)** | Determines refresh-rate requirements |
| Standard refresh rate | **≥ 60 Hz** | Flicker-free display criterion |
| Audio alarm level | **65 – 90 dB** | OSHA/IEC 60204-1 industrial standards |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Classification of Data Presentation Systems

The Data Presentation System in a mechatronic chain can be broadly classified into **three functional categories**:

### A. Display Devices (Visual / Indicating)
These present instantaneous values of the measured quantity. They are **non-permanent** (unless backed by memory).

- **Analog Indicators:** Moving-coil meters, moving-iron meters, electrodynamometer, thermocouple meters.
- **Digital Displays:** 7-segment LED, 14/16-segment alphanumeric, dot-matrix, LCD (TN, STN, IPS), OLED.
- **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT):** Historically used; now obsolete; useful for high-speed transient visualization.
- **Oscilloscope (CRO/DSO):** Time-domain waveform display.
- **Indicating Lamps & LEDs:** Binary (ON/OFF) state indication.
- **Bar-Graph Displays:** Quasi-analog using stacked LEDs or LCD segments.

### B. Recording Devices (Permanent / Time-Variant)
These produce a **permanent record** of measured data over time or against another variable.

- **Strip-Chart Recorder:** Pen traces value vs. time on a moving paper strip.
- **X-Y Recorder:** Plots one variable against another (e.g., force vs. displacement).
- **Magnetic Tape Recorder:** Analog signal recorded as magnetic flux variations.
- **Data Logger:** Microprocessor-based digital storage at sampled intervals.
- **Hard-Copy Printers:** Impact, thermal, inkjet, laser, dot-matrix.

### C. Modern HMI / Computer-Based Presentation
- **Visual Display Unit (VDU):** Computer monitor showing virtual instrumentation.
- **Touch-Screen HMI Panels:** Industrial-grade resistive/capacitive touchscreens.
- **SCADA / DCS Screens:** Graphical mimic diagrams of the plant.
- **Virtual Instrumentation (LabVIEW, MATLAB):** Software-based display.
- **Audible Alarms & Voice Synthesizers:** For non-visual feedback.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Signal Reception:**  
The DPS receives either an **analog voltage/current** (e.g., 4–20 mA, 0–10 V) or a **digital code** (e.g., parallel BCD, serial UART, SPI, I²C).

**Step 2 — Decoding/Conversion:**  
If digital, a decoder driver (e.g., **BCD-to-7-segment decoder IC 7447/4511**) translates the code to segment ON/OFF states. If analog, a D/A or direct meter movement converts it to deflection.

**Step 3 — Actuation/Driving:**  
Driver circuitry activates the visual, audible, or recording element (LEDs, LCD electrodes, pen motor, speaker coil, hard-drive write-head).

**Step 4 — Human Perception:**  
The human eye/ear perceives the output. The operator interprets the displayed value relative to a calibrated scale.

**Step 5 — Feedback / Storage:**  
The displayed/recorded data is either acted upon (control decision) or stored for later analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the eye need ≥ 60 Hz refresh?**  
> The **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)** of the human eye is approximately **60 Hz** at moderate brightness. Below this, displays appear to flicker, causing fatigue and misinterpretation. This is why analog CRTs scanned at 60 Hz and modern LCDs/OLEDs refresh at 60–240 Hz.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full-scale deflection current (PMMC) | $I_{fsd} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | A | Meter design |
| 2 | Shunt resistor (ammeter extension) | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$ | Ω | Ammeter ranging |
| 3 | Multiplier resistor (voltmeter extension) | $R_{s} = R_m (m - 1), \; m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ | Ω | Voltmeter ranging |
| 4 | Display power (7-segment LED) | $P_{seg} = V_f \times I_f \times n_{ON}$ | W | Power budget |
| 5 | Recording speed (strip-chart) | $v = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | mm/s | Time-base setting |
| 6 | Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Data logger |
| 7 | Display resolution | $R_d = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | V/bit | Digital display |
| 8 | Dynamic range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | dB | Recorder selection |
| 9 | Luminous intensity (LED) | $I_v = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega}$ | cd | Display design |
| 10 | Persistence of vision | $\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{16}$ s | s | Refresh-rate rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** Always quote the **4–20 mA standard** when discussing industrial DPS signal levels — it is the universally accepted current-loop standard for noise-immune analog transmission (ANSI/ISA-50.1).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Speedometer, RPM gauge, fuel-level indicator, infotainment HMI.
- **Process Industries:** SCADA screens, panel meters, chart recorders, alarm annunciators.
- **Medical:** Patient monitor (ECG, SpO₂ display), infusion-pump display.
- **Aerospace:** Glass cockpit MFDs (Multi-Function Displays), HUDs (Head-Up Displays).
- **Robotics:** Teach-pendant screens, status LEDs, audio cues.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smart-watch OLED, smartphone touch UI.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shunt Resistor for Ammeter Range Extension

A moving-coil meter has full-scale deflection current $I_m$ and internal resistance $R_m$. To extend its range to a higher current $I$, a shunt resistor $R_{sh}$ is connected in **parallel**.

The current through the meter $I_m$ produces a voltage across both the meter and the shunt:

$$V_{sh} = V_m$$

$$I_m \cdot R_m = (I - I_m) \cdot R_{sh}$$

Solving for $R_{sh}$:

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$$

Define the **multiplying factor** $m = I / I_m$. Then:

$$\boxed{\;R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}\;}$$

**Worked Example (KTU style):**  
A PMMC meter with $I_m = 1\ \text{mA}$ and $R_m = 100\ \Omega$ is to be used to measure $I = 1\ \text{A}$. Find $R_{sh}$.

**Step 1:** Calculate multiplying factor.

$$m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000$$

**Step 2:** Apply the shunt formula.

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1} = \dfrac{100}{1000 - 1} = \dfrac{100}{999}$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{sh} \approx 0.1001\ \Omega\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shunt resistance is much smaller than the meter resistance, ensuring most of the current bypasses the delicate meter movement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multiplier Resistor for Voltmeter Range Extension

To use the same meter as a voltmeter measuring up to $V$, a multiplier resistor $R_s$ is connected in **series** with the meter.

The same current $I_m$ flows through both. The total voltage drop is $V$:

$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

$$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m = \dfrac{V - I_m R_m}{I_m}$$

Using $m = V / V_m$ (where $V_m = I_m R_m$ is the meter's own voltage rating):

$$\boxed{\;R_s = R_m (m - 1)\;}$$

**Worked Example:**  
Same meter ($I_m = 1\ \text{mA}, R_m = 100\ \Omega$) is to measure $V = 10\ \text{V}$. Find $R_s$.

**Step 1:** Find $V_m$.

$$V_m = I_m R_m = 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.1\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $m$.

$$m = \dfrac{10}{0.1} = 100$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula.

$$R_s = 100 \times (100 - 1) = 9900\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\;R_s = 9.9\ \text{k}\Omega\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Digital Display Resolution and Quantization

A digital display driven by an $n$-bit ADC has **quantization step (LSB)**:

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

The smallest *visible* increment corresponds to **1 LSB** change in the binary code. Therefore, the **display resolution** is:

$$\boxed{\;R_d = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}\; \text{V per bit}}$$

**Worked Example:**  
A 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10\ \text{V}$ drives a 7-segment display.

**Step 1:** Quantization step.

$$Q = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} = 2.44\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2:** Display resolution.

$$R_d = \dfrac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442\ \text{mV/bit}$$

**Step 3:** For a sensor producing $V = 3.725\ \text{V}$, the display code:

$$N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{3.725}{2.44 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = 1526$$

So the display reads **1526 counts** corresponding to 3.725 V.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Nyquist Sampling Criterion for Data Loggers

To faithfully reconstruct a signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling frequency must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

This is **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** (1949, formalized by Shannon building on Nyquist 1928 and Kotelnikov 1933).

**Worked Example:**  
A vibration sensor outputs up to $f_{max} = 5\ \text{kHz}$. Minimum sampling rate for the data logger:

$$f_s = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 10\ \text{kHz}$$

In practice, engineers use **5× to 10× oversampling** (so 50–100 kHz) to allow anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Choosing sampling rate *equal* to $f_{max}$ (instead of $2f_{max}$) causes **aliasing**, producing false low-frequency artefacts. Always use $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of DPS for Given Application

| Application | Best DPS Choice | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Car speedometer | Analog PMMC + eddy-current damping | Direct visual, smooth response, no flicker |
| ECG monitor | Digital LCD/OLED + audio beep | High resolution, alarm required |
| Industrial furnace temperature (long-term log) | Strip-chart recorder | Permanent record, slow-changing signal |
| Robotic arm joint angle | Digital encoder + LCD | High precision, real-time |
| Strain vs. load test | X-Y recorder | Two-variable plot |
| Aircraft altitude | MFD (Multi-Function Display) | Compact, multi-parameter, redundant |
| Boiler pressure alarm | Annunciator + horn | Safety-critical binary alert |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Data Presentation System. List any four types of display devices used in mechatronic systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Presentation System (DPS)** is the final element of a measurement/mechatronic chain that converts processed electrical signals into a human-perceivable form (visual, audible, or permanent record) for monitoring, analysis, and control.

**Four types of display devices:**

1. **Analog Pointer Meters** (PMMC, Moving Iron) — continuous needle deflection.
2. **7-Segment LED / LCD Displays** — discrete numeric readout.
3. **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO/DSO)** — waveform visualization.
4. **Dot-Matrix / Alphanumeric LCD** — multi-character text and graphics.

> *Examiner Note:* Students often forget to mention **recording devices** alongside displays — both belong to DPS.

---

### Q2. What is the significance of the 4–20 mA current loop standard in data presentation? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **4–20 mA current loop** is an industry-standard (ANSI/ISA-50.1) for transmitting analog signals from a sensor to a Data Presentation System.

**Significance:**

- **Zero at 4 mA (live-zero):** Allows distinction between *true zero* (4 mA) and *broken wire / power failure* (0 mA), enabling fault detection — a critical feature for **safety displays**.
- **Noise immunity:** Current signals are unaffected by line resistance and EM interference over long cable runs (up to hundreds of metres).
- **Loop-powered compatibility:** The same pair of wires carries both signal and supply to the field transmitter.
- **Universally accepted by:** Panel meters, chart recorders, PLCs, SCADA, DCS displays.

> *Examiner Note:* Many students write "4–20 mA is the standard" but fail to explain *why 4 mA and not 0 mA* — mentioning the **live-zero concept** fetches the third mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### (a) Explain with neat block diagram the generalized Data Presentation System and describe any two display devices in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

The generalized DPS receives either an analog or digital input from the previous stage (signal conditioner / processor). The block diagram is:

```
Input Signal → Signal Adaptor → Decoder/Driver → Display Element → Output Reading
                                  ↑
                          Power Supply
```

**Two Display Devices — Detailed Explanation:**

**1. PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil) Meter:**

- *Construction:* A coil wound on a light aluminum former, suspended in the field of a permanent magnet. A pointer is attached to the coil.
- *Working:* When current $I$ flows through the coil placed in magnetic field $B$, a force $F = BIl$ is produced, generating a torque:

$$T_d = NBIA$$

where $N$ = number of turns, $A$ = coil area. This torque is opposed by the spring torque $T_c = K\theta$, giving equilibrium:

$$\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$$

Thus, deflection $\theta$ is **linearly proportional** to current $I$.

- *Advantages:* High accuracy (class 0.1–1.0), linear scale, low power consumption.
- *Disadvantage:* Works for **DC only** (AC requires rectifier or moving-iron variant).

**2. Seven-Segment LED Display:**

- *Construction:* Seven LEDs (a–g) arranged in a figure-of-8, plus an optional decimal point (dp).
- *Working:* A BCD input (e.g., 0101 for "5") is fed to a **BCD-to-7-segment decoder** (IC 7447 for common-anode or 4511 for common-cathode). The decoder drives the appropriate segments ON/OFF to display the digit.
- *Driving Current:* Typically 10–20 mA per segment, limited by series resistor $R = (V_{CC} - V_f) / I_f$.
- *Advantages:* Compact, bright, fast response, easily multiplexed for multi-digit displays.
- *Disadvantage:* Limited to numeric (or simple alpha) characters; requires decoder and driver circuitry.

#### (b) A PMMC meter has $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A}$ full-scale and internal resistance $R_m = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. Convert it into: (i) an ammeter to read 0–100 mA, and (ii) a voltmeter to read 0–10 V. Compute the required shunt and series multiplier resistances. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$, $R_m = 1000\ \Omega$.

**(i) Ammeter conversion (0–100 mA):**

Step 1 — Multiplying factor:

$$m_I = \dfrac{I}{I_m} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10^{-1}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2000$$

Step 2 — Shunt resistance:

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m_I - 1} = \dfrac{1000}{2000 - 1} = \dfrac{1000}{1999}$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{sh} \approx 0.5003\ \Omega\;}$$

**(ii) Voltmeter conversion (0–10 V):**

Step 1 — Meter's own voltage rating:

$$V_m = I_m \cdot R_m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 0.05\ \text{V}$$

Step 2 — Multiplying factor:

$$m_V = \dfrac{V}{V_m} = \dfrac{10}{0.05} = 200$$

Step 3 — Series multiplier resistance:

$$R_s = R_m (m_V - 1) = 1000 \times (200 - 1) = 1000 \times 199$$

$$\boxed{\;R_s = 199\ \text{k}\Omega\;}$$

> *Examiner Note:* Students frequently invert the multiplying factor or use $R_s = R_m \cdot m$ (instead of $R_m (m-1)$). The correct expression is **always $R_m (m-1)$**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### (a) Describe the construction and working of a Strip-Chart Recorder and an X-Y Recorder with suitable diagrams. Compare their applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Strip-Chart Recorder:**

- *Construction:* A long paper strip is moved at a constant speed by a motor-driven roller mechanism. A pen (inked or thermal) is mounted on a moving carriage, deflected horizontally by the input signal through a servo-amplifier and PMMC-type movement.
- *Working:* The input signal deflects the pen proportionally across the chart width, while time advances on the x-axis. Result: a continuous trace of the measured variable vs. time.
- *Applications:* Long-term monitoring of slow-changing process variables (temperature, pressure, humidity).
- *Chart speeds:* Typically 1 mm/hr to 100 mm/s.

**X-Y Recorder:**

- *Construction:* Has **two independent servo-driven axes** — one pen moves along the X-axis (proportional to one input), another along the Y-axis (proportional to a second input). The paper is held stationary on a flat platen.
- *Working:* Plots one variable as a function of another (e.g., stress vs. strain, B-H curve, frequency response).
- *Applications:* Material testing, transducer characterization, plotting of any pair of related variables.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Strip-Chart | X-Y Recorder |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variable | Time (always) | Any chosen variable |
| Paper motion | Continuous | Stationary |
| Typical use | Long-term trend logs | Two-variable plots |
| Accuracy | ±0.5% FS | ±0.25% FS |

#### (b) An industrial data logger records a vibration signal. The signal has a maximum frequency component of 4 kHz. Determine the minimum sampling rate as per the Nyquist criterion. If the logger uses a 10-bit ADC with a full-scale input of ±5 V, find the quantization error and the display resolution. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{max} = 4\ \text{kHz}$, $n = 10\ \text{bits}$, $V_{FS} = 5 - (-5) = 10\ \text{V}$ (bipolar full-scale range).

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist):**

$$\boxed{\;f_s = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000\ \text{Hz} = 8\ \text{kHz}\;}$$

**Practical recommendation:** Use $f_s = 5 \cdot f_{max} = 20\ \text{kHz}$ to allow anti-aliasing filter.

**(ii) Quantization Step (LSB):**

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{10}{1024}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 9.766\ \text{mV}\;}$$

**(iii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

The maximum quantization error is **± ½ LSB**:

$$E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{9.766}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{max} = \pm 4.883\ \text{mV}\;}$$

**(iv) Display Resolution (per bit):**

$$R_d = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{10}{1023}$$

$$\boxed{\;R_d \approx 9.775\ \text{mV/bit}\;}$$

**Conclusion:** The data logger must sample at ≥ 8 kHz, with each bit of the 10-bit code representing approximately 9.77 mV on the display.

> *Examiner Note:* A common error is using $V_{FS} = 5\ \text{V}$ (peak) for a bipolar signal instead of the **total span = 10 V**. Also, students often confuse **resolution** with **quantization error** — they are related but distinct.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in DPS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Resolution and Quantization Error:** Resolution = 1 LSB; Quantization Error = ±½ LSB.
> 2. **Wrong Shunt Formula:** Using $R_{sh} = R_m / m$ (incorrect) instead of $R_m / (m - 1)$.
> 3. **Ignoring Live-Zero:** Writing 4–20 mA without explaining the **4 mA live-zero** fault-detection feature.
> 4. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** Even a labelled block diagram is mandatory for 7-mark sub-parts.
> 5. **Mixing AC and DC Meter Concepts:** PMMC is DC-only — using it directly for AC without a rectifier is a serious conceptual error.
> 6. **Forgetting Units:** Always quote values with units (Ω, mA, Hz, V); a numerical answer without units loses the final mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Data Presentation System (Module 1):**

- ✅ **DPS** = Output stage of measurement chain (Sensor → Conditioner → Processor → **DPS** → Human).
- ✅ **Three Categories:** Display (transient), Recording (permanent), HMI/Computer-based.
- ✅ **PMMC:** Linear scale, DC only; deflection $\theta \propto I$; torque $T_d = NBIA$.
- ✅ **Shunt formula:** $R_{sh} = R_m / (m - 1)$, used for **ammeter** range extension.
- ✅ **Multiplier formula:** $R_s = R_m (m - 1)$, used for **voltmeter** range extension.
- ✅ **4–20 mA** is the industrial current-loop standard with **live-zero** at 4 mA for fault detection.
- ✅ **7-Segment Display:** Requires BCD-to-7-segment decoder (IC 4511/7447); driven by 10–20 mA per segment.
- ✅ **LCD vs LED:** LCD = low power, needs ambient/backlight; LED = self-luminous, higher power.
- ✅ **Oscilloscope:** Best for time-domain transient signal visualization.
- ✅ **Strip-Chart Recorder:** Time on x-axis, variable on y-axis; long-term logging.
- ✅ **X-Y Recorder:** Two independent inputs; plots variable against another variable.
- ✅ **Data Logger:** Microprocessor-based digital storage; obeys **Nyquist** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- ✅ **Quantization step:** $Q = V_{FS} / 2^n$; **Max error:** $\pm \dfrac{Q}{2}$.
- ✅ **Persistence of vision** = 1/16 s; therefore refresh rate must be **≥ 60 Hz** to avoid flicker.
- ✅ **Alarm standard:** 65–90 dB (per OSHA/IEC 60204-1).
- ✅ **Dynamic range:** $DR = 20 \log_{10}(V_{max}/V_{min})$ in dB.
- ✅ **Audible alarms** supplement visual displays in safety-critical applications.
- ✅ **Modern DPS** trend: VDU, SCADA, touch-HMI, virtual instrumentation, IoT dashboards.

**One-line exam mantra:** *"Data Presentation = Sensor's Voice to the Operator."*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Classification of Data Presentation Systems

The Data Presentation System in a mechatronic chain can be broadly classified into **three functional categories**:

### A. Display Devices (Visual / Indicating)
These present instantaneous values of the measured quantity. They are **non-permanent** (unless backed by memory).

- **Analog Indicators:** Moving-coil meters, moving-iron meters, electrodynamometer, thermocouple meters.
- **Digital Displays:** 7-segment LED, 14/16-segment alphanumeric, dot-matrix, LCD (TN, STN, IPS), OLED.
- **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT):** Historically used; now obsolete; useful for high-speed transient visualization.
- **Oscilloscope (CRO/DSO):** Time-domain waveform display.
- **Indicating Lamps & LEDs:** Binary (ON/OFF) state indication.
- **Bar-Graph Displays:** Quasi-analog using stacked LEDs or LCD segments.

### B. Recording Devices (Permanent / Time-Variant)
These produce a **permanent record** of measured data over time or against another variable.

- **Strip-Chart Recorder:** Pen traces value vs. time on a moving paper strip.
- **X-Y Recorder:** Plots one variable against another (e.g., force vs. displacement).
- **Magnetic Tape Recorder:** Analog signal recorded as magnetic flux variations.
- **Data Logger:** Microprocessor-based digital storage at sampled intervals.
- **Hard-Copy Printers:** Impact, thermal, inkjet, laser, dot-matrix.

### C. Modern HMI / Computer-Based Presentation
- **Visual Display Unit (VDU):** Computer monitor showing virtual instrumentation.
- **Touch-Screen HMI Panels:** Industrial-grade resistive/capacitive touchscreens.
- **SCADA / DCS Screens:** Graphical mimic diagrams of the plant.
- **Virtual Instrumentation (LabVIEW, MATLAB):** Software-based display.
- **Audible Alarms & Voice Synthesizers:** For non-visual feedback.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Signal Reception:**  
The DPS receives either an **analog voltage/current** (e.g., 4–20 mA, 0–10 V) or a **digital code** (e.g., parallel BCD, serial UART, SPI, I²C).

**Step 2 — Decoding/Conversion:**  
If digital, a decoder driver (e.g., **BCD-to-7-segment decoder IC 7447/4511**) translates the code to segment ON/OFF states. If analog, a D/A or direct meter movement converts it to deflection.

**Step 3 — Actuation/Driving:**  
Driver circuitry activates the visual, audible, or recording element (LEDs, LCD electrodes, pen motor, speaker coil, hard-drive write-head).

**Step 4 — Human Perception:**  
The human eye/ear perceives the output. The operator interprets the displayed value relative to a calibrated scale.

**Step 5 — Feedback / Storage:**  
The displayed/recorded data is either acted upon (control decision) or stored for later analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the eye need ≥ 60 Hz refresh?**  
> The **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)** of the human eye is approximately **60 Hz** at moderate brightness. Below this, displays appear to flicker, causing fatigue and misinterpretation. This is why analog CRTs scanned at 60 Hz and modern LCDs/OLEDs refresh at 60–240 Hz.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full-scale deflection current (PMMC) | $I_{fsd} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | A | Meter design |
| 2 | Shunt resistor (ammeter extension) | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$ | Ω | Ammeter ranging |
| 3 | Multiplier resistor (voltmeter extension) | $R_{s} = R_m (m - 1), \; m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ | Ω | Voltmeter ranging |
| 4 | Display power (7-segment LED) | $P_{seg} = V_f \times I_f \times n_{ON}$ | W | Power budget |
| 5 | Recording speed (strip-chart) | $v = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | mm/s | Time-base setting |
| 6 | Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Data logger |
| 7 | Display resolution | $R_d = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | V/bit | Digital display |
| 8 | Dynamic range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | dB | Recorder selection |
| 9 | Luminous intensity (LED) | $I_v = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega}$ | cd | Display design |
| 10 | Persistence of vision | $\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{16}$ s | s | Refresh-rate rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** Always quote the **4–20 mA standard** when discussing industrial DPS signal levels — it is the universally accepted current-loop standard for noise-immune analog transmission (ANSI/ISA-50.1).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Speedometer, RPM gauge, fuel-level indicator, infotainment HMI.
- **Process Industries:** SCADA screens, panel meters, chart recorders, alarm annunciators.
- **Medical:** Patient monitor (ECG, SpO₂ display), infusion-pump display.
- **Aerospace:** Glass cockpit MFDs (Multi-Function Displays), HUDs (Head-Up Displays).
- **Robotics:** Teach-pendant screens, status LEDs, audio cues.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smart-watch OLED, smartphone touch UI.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shunt Resistor for Ammeter Range Extension

A moving-coil meter has full-scale deflection current $I_m$ and internal resistance $R_m$. To extend its range to a higher current $I$, a shunt resistor $R_{sh}$ is connected in **parallel**.

The current through the meter $I_m$ produces a voltage across both the meter and the shunt:

$$V_{sh} = V_m$$

$$I_m \cdot R_m = (I - I_m) \cdot R_{sh}$$

Solving for $R_{sh}$:

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m}$$

Define the **multiplying factor** $m = I / I_m$. Then:

$$\boxed{\;R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}\;}$$

**Worked Example (KTU style):**  
A PMMC meter with $I_m = 1\ \text{mA}$ and $R_m = 100\ \Omega$ is to be used to measure $I = 1\ \text{A}$. Find $R_{sh}$.

**Step 1:** Calculate multiplying factor.

$$m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000$$

**Step 2:** Apply the shunt formula.

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1} = \dfrac{100}{1000 - 1} = \dfrac{100}{999}$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{sh} \approx 0.1001\ \Omega\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shunt resistance is much smaller than the meter resistance, ensuring most of the current bypasses the delicate meter movement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multiplier Resistor for Voltmeter Range Extension

To use the same meter as a voltmeter measuring up to $V$, a multiplier resistor $R_s$ is connected in **series** with the meter.

The same current $I_m$ flows through both. The total voltage drop is $V$:

$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

$$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m = \dfrac{V - I_m R_m}{I_m}$$

Using $m = V / V_m$ (where $V_m = I_m R_m$ is the meter's own voltage rating):

$$\boxed{\;R_s = R_m (m - 1)\;}$$

**Worked Example:**  
Same meter ($I_m = 1\ \text{mA}, R_m = 100\ \Omega$) is to measure $V = 10\ \text{V}$. Find $R_s$.

**Step 1:** Find $V_m$.

$$V_m = I_m R_m = 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.1\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $m$.

$$m = \dfrac{10}{0.1} = 100$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula.

$$R_s = 100 \times (100 - 1) = 9900\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\;R_s = 9.9\ \text{k}\Omega\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Digital Display Resolution and Quantization

A digital display driven by an $n$-bit ADC has **quantization step (LSB)**:

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

The smallest *visible* increment corresponds to **1 LSB** change in the binary code. Therefore, the **display resolution** is:

$$\boxed{\;R_d = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}\; \text{V per bit}}$$

**Worked Example:**  
A 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10\ \text{V}$ drives a 7-segment display.

**Step 1:** Quantization step.

$$Q = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} = 2.44\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2:** Display resolution.

$$R_d = \dfrac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442\ \text{mV/bit}$$

**Step 3:** For a sensor producing $V = 3.725\ \text{V}$, the display code:

$$N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{3.725}{2.44 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = 1526$$

So the display reads **1526 counts** corresponding to 3.725 V.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Nyquist Sampling Criterion for Data Loggers

To faithfully reconstruct a signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling frequency must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

This is **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** (1949, formalized by Shannon building on Nyquist 1928 and Kotelnikov 1933).

**Worked Example:**  
A vibration sensor outputs up to $f_{max} = 5\ \text{kHz}$. Minimum sampling rate for the data logger:

$$f_s = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 10\ \text{kHz}$$

In practice, engineers use **5× to 10× oversampling** (so 50–100 kHz) to allow anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Choosing sampling rate *equal* to $f_{max}$ (instead of $2f_{max}$) causes **aliasing**, producing false low-frequency artefacts. Always use $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of DPS for Given Application

| Application | Best DPS Choice | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Car speedometer | Analog PMMC + eddy-current damping | Direct visual, smooth response, no flicker |
| ECG monitor | Digital LCD/OLED + audio beep | High resolution, alarm required |
| Industrial furnace temperature (long-term log) | Strip-chart recorder | Permanent record, slow-changing signal |
| Robotic arm joint angle | Digital encoder + LCD | High precision, real-time |
| Strain vs. load test | X-Y recorder | Two-variable plot |
| Aircraft altitude | MFD (Multi-Function Display) | Compact, multi-parameter, redundant |
| Boiler pressure alarm | Annunciator + horn | Safety-critical binary alert |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Position of Data Presentation System in Mechatronic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYS["Physical World"]
        SP["Physical Quantity"]
    end

    subgraph SENS["Sensing Stage"]
        SS["Sensor + Transducer"]
    end

    subgraph SC["Signal Conditioning"]
        AMP["Amplifier"]
        FILT["Filter"]
        LIN["Linearizer"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["Processing Stage"]
        ADC["ADC / Microcontroller"]
        DSP1["DSP / CPU"]
    end

    subgraph DPS["Data Presentation System"]
        DD["Display Device"]
        RD["Recording Device"]
        HMI1["HMI / Computer"]
        AUD["Audio Alarm"]
    end

    subgraph OP["Operator / User"]
        HU["Human Decision / Storage"]
    end

    SP --> SS --> AMP --> FILT --> LIN --> ADC --> DSP1
    DSP1 --> DD
    DSP1 --> RD
    DSP1 --> HMI1
    DSP1 --> AUD
    DD --> HU
    RD --> HU
    HMI1 --> HU
    AUD --> HU
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Internal Architecture of a Digital Display Module

```mermaid

    INP["Input Signal: 4-20 mA or Digital Code"]
    DEC["Decoder: BCD-to-7-segment IC 4511"]
    DRV["Driver Stage: Transistor / MOSFET array"]

    OPR["Operator Reading"]
    PWR["Power Supply: 5V regulated"]
    REF["Reference / Calibration Memory"]

    INP --> DEC
    PWR --> DEC
    PWR --> DRV
    DEC --> DRV
    DRV --> DSP1A
    REF --> DEC
    DSP1A --> OPR
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selection Procedure for DPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{"Is permanent record needed?"}
    S2["Use: Strip-chart / X-Y / Data Logger"]

    S5{"Is the signal slow-changing and continuous?"}
    S6["Use: Analog PMMC meter"]
    S7{"Multi-parameter or complex?"}

    S1 -- Yes --> S2
    S1 -- No --> S3
    S3 -- Yes --> S4
    S3 -- No --> S5
    S5 -- Yes --> S6
    S5 -- No --> S7
    S7 -- Yes --> S8

```


**Truth-table snippet (for digit '5'):**

| a | b | c | d | e | f | g |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

> Segments **a, c, d, f, g** glow, producing the numeral "5" on the display.
