<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**State and explain the two golden rules of an ideal op-amp used in negative feedback configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule 1 — Infinite Input Impedance:** The input terminals of an ideal op-amp draw **no current**, i.e., $I^+ = I^- = 0$. This implies $R_{in} = \infty$.
>
> **Golden Rule 2 — Virtual Short / Infinite Voltage Gain:** Since the open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$, and $V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$ is finite, the differential input must approach zero, i.e., $V^+ = V^-$. In a configuration with $V^+ = 0$, this forces the inverting terminal to also be at **0 V**, called a **virtual ground**.
>
> These two rules enable all op-amp analysis using simple KCL at the inverting node, making them the foundation of every op-amp circuit problem. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
**Define Slew Rate and Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an op-amp. State one significance of each in sensor signal conditioning.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Slew Rate (SR):** It is defined as the **maximum rate of change of output voltage** with respect to time, expressed in **V/µs**.
>
> $$SR = \left. \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \right|_{max}$$
>
> *Significance:* In sensor signal conditioning, the input signal from a vibration or high-frequency sensor may require the op-amp to track fast changes. An insufficient SR causes **slew-rate induced distortion (SID)**, where the triangular edges of square waves become sloped and sine waves become triangular.
>
> **CMRR:** It is the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain, expressed in dB.
>
> $$CMRR = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert \text{ dB}$$
>
> *Significance:* In Wheatstone bridge or thermocouple circuits, large common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup) appears equally on both inputs. A high CMRR (> 100 dB) ensures this noise is rejected while the small differential sensor signal is amplified — critical for strain gauge and biomedical sensor interfaces. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the closed-loop voltage gain of an **inverting amplifier** using an ideal op-amp. Mention the input and output impedances of this configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **summing amplifier** using an ideal op-amp to realize the following output equation:
$$V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 5V_2 + 10V_3)$$
Choose $R_f = 100$ kΩ and determine the input resistors. What is the maximum allowable input voltage if the op-amp saturates at ±13 V? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Inverting Amplifier Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal (−) via resistor $R_1$. A feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting terminal. The non-inverting terminal (+) is grounded.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (call it node A):**

$$\frac{V_A - V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_A - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Golden Rule 1** ($I^- = 0$) — already implicit in KCL above.

**Step 4 — Apply Golden Rule 2 (Virtual Short):** Since the non-inverting input is grounded, $V^+ = 0$, and by the virtual short principle:

$$V_A = V^- = V^+ = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$-\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$$

**Step 6 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

**Step 7 — Impedances:**
- **Input impedance** $Z_{in} = R_1$ (limited, low).
- **Output impedance** $Z_{out} \to 0$ (very low, due to negative feedback). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — Summing Amplifier Design:**

**Step 1 — Standard summing-amplifier output equation:**

$$V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compare with target equation $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 5V_2 + 10V_3)$:**

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 = \frac{R_f}{2} = \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{2} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\frac{R_f}{R_2} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_2 = \frac{R_f}{5} = \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{5} = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\frac{R_f}{R_3} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_3 = \frac{R_f}{10} = \frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{10} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Maximum input voltage:**
Saturation condition: $V_{out} \leq V_{sat} = 13$ V. Worst case: all three inputs at maximum positive value $V_{max}$:

$$V_{out,max} = (2 + 5 + 10) V_{max} = 17 V_{max} \leq 13 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{max} \leq \frac{13}{17} = 0.765 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$\boxed{R_1 = 50 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_2 = 20 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad V_{max} = 0.765 \text{ V}}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Derive the output expression of a **non-inverting amplifier** using an ideal op-amp. State why the input impedance of this configuration is very high. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has an open-loop gain $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, open-loop bandwidth $f_1 = 5$ Hz, and slew rate $SR = 0.5$ V/µs. It is used in a non-inverting configuration with a closed-loop gain of 100.
   (i) Calculate the **closed-loop bandwidth**.
   (ii) Calculate the **maximum undistorted sine wave frequency** at $V_{out,peak} = 8$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Non-Inverting Amplifier Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:** $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal (+). A feedback network of $R_f$ (in series) and $R_1$ (to ground) is connected from output to the inverting terminal (−).

**Step 2 — Non-inverting node voltage:** $V^+ = V_{in}$.

**Step 3 — Apply virtual short:** $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$.

**Step 4 — Voltage divider at the inverting node:**

$$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $V^-$:**

$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Step 7 — Why input impedance is high:** Since the non-inverting input of an ideal op-amp draws zero current (Golden Rule 1), the source $V_{in}$ sees an effective input impedance approaching infinity. Practically, the input impedance is the common-mode input impedance of the op-amp, which is in the range of $10^{12} \ \Omega$. This makes the non-inverting configuration ideal for buffering high-impedance sensors (e.g., piezoelectric sensors, pH electrodes). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — Op-Amp Dynamic Performance:**

**(i) Closed-loop bandwidth:**

The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is a constant for a given op-amp:

$$GBW = A_{OL} \cdot f_1 = 2 \times 10^5 \cdot 5 = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

The closed-loop bandwidth is:

$$f_{CL} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{10^6}{100} = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency (slew-rate limited):**

The slew-rate-limited maximum frequency is:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2 \pi V_{peak}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}}{2 \pi \cdot 8 \text{ V}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{50.265} \approx 9{,}947 \text{ Hz} \approx 9.95 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The **smaller of $f_{CL}$ and $f_{max}$** is the true maximum usable frequency.

$$f_{usable} = \min(10 \text{ kHz}, \ 9.95 \text{ kHz}) = 9.95 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this case, the **slew rate** is the limiting factor, not the bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the inverting amplifier gain $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_1$. Examiners specifically test this — full **½ mark is cut** if the sign is missing.
> 2. **Confusing $V^+$ and $V^-$** when applying the virtual short. Remember: $V^+ = V^-$ is enforced by the **feedback action**, not by direct connection.
> 3. **Not stating Golden Rules explicitly** before applying them in derivation questions. Examiners expect the two golden rules to be **mentioned before** the KCL step.
> 4. **Unit confusion in slew rate** — answers must be in V/µs, not V/s. Always convert.
> 5. **Forgetting the saturation limit** in summing-amp design problems. The output $V_{out}$ cannot exceed $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 2\text{V}$ for a typical op-amp.
> 6. **Differentiator instability** — practical differentiator circuits are rarely used without a series input resistor and feedback capacitor; mention this to fetch full marks in design problems.
> 7. **Not drawing the circuit diagram** — KTU valuation key deducts up to **2 marks** if a circuit is missing in a 7-mark design sub-question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp:**
  - $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (infinite input impedance)
  - $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short / infinite gain)

- **Inverting Amplifier Gain:** $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$ (180° phase shift, $Z_{in} = R_1$)

- **Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain:** $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f / R_1$ (0° phase shift, $Z_{in} \to \infty$)

- **Voltage Follower:** $A_{CL} = 1$ (buffer, impedance matching, $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$)

- **Summing Amplifier:** $V_{out} = -R_f \sum (V_i / R_i)$ — equal resistors give pure adder.

- **Difference Amplifier:** $V_{out} = (R_f/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ — requires matched resistor ratios.

- **Integrator:** $V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f} \int V_{in} \, dt$

- **Differentiator:** $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$

- **Slew Rate:** $SR = 2\pi f_{max} V_{peak}$ — must be satisfied for distortion-free operation.

- **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $GBW = A_{OL} \cdot f_1$ (constant); $BW_{CL} = GBW / A_{CL}$.

- **CMRR:** $20 \log_{10}\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ in dB — higher is better for sensor noise rejection.

- **Instrumentation Amplifier Gain:** $V_{out} = (1 + 2R_1/R_g)(R_3/R_2)(V_2 - V_1)$ — three-op-amp topology, used for strain gauge / bridge sensors.

- **PSRR:** measures how well the op-amp rejects ripples in the power supply — important in noisy industrial environments.

- **Saturation Voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5$ V to $V_{CC} - 2$ V (for ±15 V supply, $V_{sat} \approx 13$ V).

- **Practical Op-Amp ICs:** µA741 (general purpose), LM358 (single supply), TL081 (JFET input, high $Z$), OP07 (precision, low offset), INA128 (instrumentation amp).

- **Sensor Interfacing Rule of Thumb:** *Always* use an instrumentation amplifier for bridge-type sensors; use a non-inverting buffer for high-impedance sensors; use an active low-pass filter before the ADC to prevent aliasing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** Practice deriving the inverting, non-inverting, summing, and difference amplifier gain equations by heart with proper KCL + virtual short methodology. Always **draw a labelled circuit diagram** before the derivation. In numerical problems, **carry units throughout** the calculation and present the final answer in a **boxed equation**. This matches the official KTU valuation key pattern and maximizes score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-Amp as Signal Conditioner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MEHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Op-Amp as Signal Conditioner # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)? An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, direct-coupled, differential-input electronic voltage amplifier that uses external feedback components to control its transfer function. In mechatronic systems, the op-amp is the *workhorse* of analog **Signal Conditioning** — the critical bridge between raw sensor output and the analog-to-digital converter (ADC) or controller. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (PCPET502):** > An Operational Amplifier is a DC-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs (V⁺ and V⁻) and a single-ended output, used in signal conditioning to perform amplification, filtering, level shifting, impedance matching, and mathematical operations on signals obtained from sensors. ### Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (For KTU Analysis) | Parameter | Ideal Value | Practical 741 Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Open-Loop Gain (A_OL) | **∞** | ~2 × 10⁵ | | Input Impedance (Z_in) | **∞** | ~2 MΩ | | Output Impedance (Z_out) | **0** | ~75 Ω | | Bandwidth | **∞** | ~1 MHz | | Offset Voltage | **0** | Few mV | | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) | **∞** | ~90 dB | ## 1.2 What is Signal Conditioning? **Signal Conditioning** is the process of manipulating a signal from a sensor into a form suitable for further processing (ADC, microcontroller, display, controller). A raw sensor signal is rarely directly usable — it may be too weak, noisy, offset, non-linear, or contain unwanted frequencies. > [!NOTE] > **Core Functions of Signal Conditioning:** > 1. **Amplification** – Boosting low-level signals (e.g., thermocouple, strain gauge). > 2. **Filtering** – Removing noise / unwanted frequency bands. > 3. **Level Shifting** – Making bipolar signals unipolar (e.g., 0–5 V) for ADC. > 4. **Isolation** – Galvanic isolation for safety & noise rejection. > 5. **Linearization** – Correcting non-linear sensor response. > 6. **Impedance Matching** – Buffering high-impedance sources. ## 1.3 The Conceptual Analogy 🍳 > [!TIP] > **Analogy: The "Smart Kitchen Mixer"** > Imagine a raw sensor output as **raw, unprocessed wheat** coming from the field. The op-amp circuit is the **smart kitchen mixer**: > - It **grinds** (amplifies) the wheat into fine flour. > - It **strains** (filters) out the husks and stones. > - It **packs** (level-shifts) the flour into a clean 1-kg bag (e.g., 0–5 V range). > - It **labels** (linearizes) the bag for the customer (ADC / microcontroller). > > Without the mixer, downstream machines cannot process the wheat — just like without a signal conditioner, a microcontroller cannot read a raw sensor signal reliably. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Supply Voltage:** Typically **±15 V** (dual supply) or **0–5 V / 0–12 V** (single supply). - **Saturation Voltage (V_sat):** ~ **±13 V** for ±15 V supply (for 741-type op-amps). - **Unity-Gain Bandwidth (GBW):** Product of gain and bandwidth, e.g., **1 MHz** for 741. - **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum rate of change of output, e.g., **0.5 V/μs** for 741. # 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Op-Amp Golden Rules (Foundation of All Analysis) Every KTU op-amp problem is solved using only **two rules** — assuming the op-amp is in its **linear (negative feedback)** region: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 1 — Virtual Short:** > $$V^+ = V^-$$ > (The two input terminals are at the same potential — a direct consequence of infinite open-loop gain and finite output.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 2 — Virtual Open (No Current into Inputs):** > $$i^+ = i^- = 0$$ > (The input impedance is infinite, so no current flows into the op-amp's input terminals.) ## 2.2 Standard Op-Amp Configurations for Signal Conditioning ### (A) Inverting Amplifier - Input applied to the **inverting (−)** terminal via resistor R_in. - Feedback resistor R_f between output and inverting input. - Non-inverting (+) input is **grounded**. - Output is **180° out of phase** with input. ### (B) Non-Inverting Amplifier - Input applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal. - Feedback network (R_f, R_in) sets gain. - Output is **in phase** with input. ### (C) Voltage Follower (Unity-Gain Buffer) - A special case of non-inverting amp with R_f = 0 and R_in = ∞. - Provides **impedance transformation** — high Z_in, low Z_out. ### (D) Difference Amplifier - Subtractor: V_out = (R_f / R_1) (V_2 − V_1). - Rejects common-mode noise (e.g., for bridge sensors). ### (E) Instrumentation Amplifier - Three op-amp topology: provides very high CMRR and adjustable gain via a single resistor. - Standard for **strain gauges, thermocouples, biomedical signals** (ECG, EMG). ### (F) Active Filters - **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Passes low frequencies, blocks high. Removes HF noise. - **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Blocks DC, passes AC. Removes DC offset. - **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Combination of LPF and HPF. ### (G) Precision Rectifier - Uses op-amp + diode to rectify signals < 0.6 V (where normal diodes fail). ### (H) Integrator & Differentiator - **Integrator:** Output is the time integral of input. Used in analog computers, ramp generators. - **Differentiator:** Output is the time derivative of input. Used in edge detectors. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU University Exam — Op-Amp Signal Conditioning** | # | Configuration | Transfer Function (V_out / V_in) | Key Parameters | Engineering Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Input impedance = R_in | Inverting signal amplifier, summing amp | | 2 | **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Input impedance ≈ ∞ | Buffer / pre-amplifier for sensors | | 3 | **Voltage Follower** | $A_v = 1$ | Z_in = ∞, Z_out ≈ 0 | Impedance matching, ADC driver | | 4 | **Difference Amplifier** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Requires R_f/R_1 = R_3/R_2 | Wheatstone bridge readout | | 5 | **Instrumentation Amp** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_2 - V_1)$ | CMRR > 100 dB | Strain gauge, ECG, thermocouple | | 6 | **Active LPF (1st order)** | $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{-1}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | Cutoff: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Anti-aliasing filter before ADC | | 7 | **Active HPF (1st order)** | $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{-j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ | Cutoff: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | AC coupling, DC removal | | 8 | **Integrator** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \displaystyle\int V_{in}\,dt$ | Frequency-dependent gain | Ramp generator, PID controller | | 9 | **Differentiator** | $V_{out} = -RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | High noise gain at high freq | Edge detector, rate sensor | | 10 | **Summing Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2}V_2 + \ldots\right)$ | Independent gain per input | Audio mixer, weighted adder | | 11 | **Precision Half-Wave Rectifier** | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (when V_in > 0); 0 otherwise | No diode drop error | AC voltmeter, signal demodulation | | 12 | **Comparator** | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ if V⁺ > V⁻; −V_sat otherwise | No feedback (open-loop) | Threshold detector, Schmitt trigger | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where Each Configuration is Used in Mechatronics:** > - **Strain Gauge Bridge → Instrumentation Amplifier** → load cell readout in CNC machines. > - **Thermocouple (μV level) → Instrumentation Amp** → temperature monitoring in industrial ovens. > - **Encoder / Hall sensor → Comparator** → clean digital pulses for microcontroller interrupts. > - **Accelerometer output → LPF + Level Shifter** → 0–3.3 V signal ready for ARM Cortex ADC. > - **ECG electrode → Instrumentation Amp + HPF + LPF** → band-pass filtered 0.05–150 Hz signal. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations in KTU exams are worth 4–7 marks. Showing every step is the *only* way to secure full marks. Skipping even the "obvious" substitution (e.g., V⁺ = V⁻) will cost you. --- ## 3.1 Derivation #1: Inverting Amplifier Transfer Function **Given Circuit:** A non-inverting input (+) is grounded. Input V_in is applied via R_in to the inverting input (−). A feedback resistor R_f connects output V_out to the inverting input. **Goal:** Derive the closed-loop voltage gain $A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (V⁻)** The currents flowing into the inverting node from R_in and R_f must sum to the current entering the op-amp. By the **virtual open rule** (i⁻ = 0), the current from R_in equals the current through R_f. $$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_{in}} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Virtual Short Rule** The non-inverting terminal is grounded, so V⁺ = 0. By virtual short, V⁻ = V⁺ = 0 V. This is the famous **"Virtual Ground"** — a 0 V node that is not physically connected to ground but behaves as such for analysis. $$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute V⁻ = 0 into the KCL Equation** $$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify** $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 5 — Cross-Multiply** $$V_{in} \cdot R_f = -V_{out} \cdot R_{in}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for V_out / V_in** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$ The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion between input and output. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Non-Inverting Amplifier Transfer Function **Given Circuit:** Input V_in is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal (+). The feedback network R_f (from output to inverting input) and R_in (from inverting input to ground) sets the gain. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the Voltage Divider at the Inverting Input** Since the inverting input draws no current (i⁻ = 0), R_f and R_in form a simple voltage divider from V_out to ground. The voltage at the inverting node is: $$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_f}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Virtual Short Rule** By virtual short, V⁺ = V⁻. Since V⁺ = V_in: $$V_{in} = V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{in} + R_f}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for V_out / V_in** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_{in} + R_f}{R_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$ The gain is always **> 1** and the output is **in phase** with the input. --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3: Difference Amplifier Transfer Function **Given Circuit:** V_1 applied via R_1 to inverting input; V_2 applied via R_2 to non-inverting input; R_f as feedback; R_3 from non-inverting input to ground. **Goal:** Find V_out in terms of V_1 and V_2. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Find Voltage at Non-Inverting Input (V⁺)** By voltage divider (since i⁺ = 0): $$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$ **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node** Current from V_1 through R_1 = current through R_f to V_out: $$\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Virtual Short: V⁻ = V⁺** $$\frac{V_1 - V^+}{R_1} = \frac{V^+ - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for V_out** Rearranging: $$V^+ - V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_1 - V^+)$$ $$V_{out} = V^+ - \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_1 - V^+)$$ $$V_{out} = V^+\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$ **Step 5 — Substitute V⁺ from Step 1** Let the matching condition hold: $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} = k$. Then $\dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3} = \dfrac{k}{1+k}$. $$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2+R_3}\left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1}V_1$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}\left(V_2 - V_1\right)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}\left(V_2 - V_1\right)}$$ This subtracts V_1 from V_2 — useful for reading **Wheatstone bridge** sensors. --- ## 3.4 Derivation #4: First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (Inverting Topology) **Given Circuit:** An inverting amplifier where the feedback element is a **parallel RC** combination (R_f in parallel with C_f), and input through R_in. **Goal:** Derive the transfer function and cutoff frequency. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Impedance of Feedback Network** The feedback impedance is the parallel combination: $$Z_f = \frac{R_f \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_f}}{R_f + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_f}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + j\omega R_f C_f}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Inverting Amplifier Formula with Complex Impedances** For an inverting amplifier with general impedances: $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}}$$ Substituting Z_in = R_in and Z_f from Step 1: $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{1}{R_{in}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{1 + j\omega R_f C_f} = -\frac{R_f/R_{in}}{1 + j\omega R_f C_f}$$ **Step 3 — Identify the Cutoff Frequency** The denominator becomes 0 dB (in magnitude) when: $$\omega R_f C_f = 1 \implies \omega_c = \frac{1}{R_f C_f}$$ Converting to Hz: $$f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$ **Step 4 — Standard Form** $$\boxed{\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{A_0}{1 + j(f/f_c)}}$$ where $A_0 = \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ is the DC gain and $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ is the −3 dB cutoff frequency. **Magnitude Response:** $$\left|\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right| = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$ - At $f \ll f_c$: gain ≈ A_0 (passband). - At $f = f_c$: gain = $A_0 / \sqrt{2}$ (−3 dB point). - At $f \gg f_c$: gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**. --- ## 3.5 Derivation #5: Op-Amp Integrator **Given Circuit:** Inverting amplifier with capacitor C in feedback, resistor R at input. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — KCL at Inverting Node (V⁻ = 0, virtual ground)** $$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$ (Current through capacitor: $i_C = C \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$, with $V_C = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$.) **Step 2 — Integrate Both Sides** $$V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)}$$ If V_in is a constant DC voltage V_s, then $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{V_s}{RC}\,t$ — a **negative-going ramp** (used in ramp generators, ADC, function generators). --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** An inverting amplifier uses R_in = 10 kΩ and R_f = 100 kΩ. If V_in = 50 mV, find V_out. Also calculate the input impedance of the circuit. **Solution:** Using the inverting amplifier formula: $$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} = -\frac{100 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = -10$$ $$V_{out} = A_v \times V_{in} = -10 \times 50 \text{ mV} = -500 \text{ mV} = -0.5 \text{ V}$$ **Input Impedance:** For an inverting amplifier, the input impedance equals R_in (since V⁻ is virtual ground): $$Z_{in} = R_{in} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$ This low input impedance is a **key disadvantage** of the inverting topology. # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Op-Amp Signal Conditioning Chain This diagram shows the **typical end-to-end flow** in a mechatronic measurement system, highlighting the role of the op-amp signal conditioner at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSICAL
PHENOMENON] --> B[SENSOR
TRANSDUCER] B --> C[RAW
SIGNAL
uV to mV] C --> D1[STAGE 1
PREAMP
Instr Amp] D1 --> D2[STAGE 2
FILTERING
LPF or HPF] D2 --> D3[STAGE 3
LEVEL SHIFT
Summing Amp] D3 --> D4[STAGE 4
BUFFER
Voltage Follower] D4 --> E[ADC
0 to 5 V] E --> F[MCU
CONTROLLER] F --> G[ACTUATOR
DRIVER] style A fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#ffffff style B fill:#374151,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#ffffff style C fill:#4b5563,stroke:#f87171,color:#ffffff style D1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff style D2 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff style D3 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff style D4 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff style E fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff style F fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style G fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Op-Amp Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SENSOR OUTPUT
NEEDS CONDITIONING] --> B{SIGNAL TYPE} B -->|Small differential uV to mV| C[INSTRUMENTATION
