topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical System Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Electrical System Model — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical System Modeling** is the mathematical representation of an electrical network (composed of passive elements like Resistors, Inductors, Capacitors, and active sources) in the form of **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)**, **Transfer Functions**, or **State-Space Equations** that capture the input-output relationship between electrical excitation (voltage/current) and electrical response, in time domain or frequency domain.

In the context of **Mechatronics**, the Electrical System Model acts as a **mathematical bridge** that allows mechanical engineers to simulate the electrical subsystem (sensors, actuators, drivers) of a mechatronic product on a computer before physical prototyping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lumped Parameter Assumption (KTU Syllabus Highlight):** All elements are considered *lumped* — meaning the physical size of components is neglected and electrical quantities (voltage $v(t)$, current $i(t)$) are assumed uniform throughout each element. This assumption is valid when the wavelength of the highest frequency signal is much larger than the physical dimensions of the circuit ($\lambda \gg l_{circuit}$).

## 1.2 The Three Foundational Building Blocks

Every electrical system model is built from exactly three passive element types:

| Symbol | Element | Energy Behavior | Governing Equation |
|:------:|:-------:|:---------------:|:------------------:|
| **R** | Resistor | Dissipative (converts electrical → heat) | $v(t) = R \cdot i(t)$ |
| **L** | Inductor | Storage (stores energy in magnetic field) | $v(t) = L \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$ |
| **C** | Capacitor | Storage (stores energy in electric field) | $i(t) = C \dfrac{dv(t)}{dt}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory vs. Memoryless Elements:** A **resistor** is *memoryless* — its output depends only on the *present* input. **Inductors and capacitors** are *memory elements* — they store energy and their response depends on the *past history* of inputs. Memory elements introduce **derivatives** into the system equation, which is why electrical systems produce **differential equations**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank Network"

Imagine a complex plumbing system as an electrical circuit:

> 🔧 **Intuition Pump:** A **water pump** = **Voltage Source (V)** — it pushes water (charge) through pipes with a pressure difference.
>
> 🔧 **Pipe narrowness / friction** = **Resistor (R)** — restricts flow (current), and energy lost as heat (just like a resistor dissipates electrical energy).
>
> 🔧 **Heavy flywheel attached to the pipe** = **Inductor (L)** — water flow wants to keep moving because the flywheel has inertia. To suddenly stop the flow, you must apply an opposing pressure (back-EMF).
>
> 🔧 **Elastic bladder / tank** = **Capacitor (C)** — stores water (charge) under pressure. To suddenly add water, the bladder expands; to release, it pushes water out.

Just like the water system can be modeled by a single differential equation relating pressure (voltage) to flow rate (current), the electrical system can be reduced to a similar mathematical form.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Board Exams)

- **Capacitance unit: Farad (F)** = **Coulomb/Volt**
- **Inductance unit: Henry (H)** = **Volt-second/Ampere**
- **Resistance unit: Ohm ($\Omega$)** = **Volt/Ampere**
- **Time constant ($\tau$) unit: Second (s)**
- **Standard Laplace-domain impedance:**
   - Resistor: $Z_R(s) = R$
   - Inductor: $Z_L(s) = sL$
   - Capacitor: $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Modeling Philosophy (KTU Board Approach)

When asked to "model an electrical system," follow this **rigorous 4-step KTU methodology**:

**Step 1 — Identify Elements & Topologies:**
List all R, L, C elements and classify them as *series* or *parallel* branches. Identify independent energy sources.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Laws:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop in a circuit is zero.
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{n} v_k(t) = 0$$
>
> **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):** The algebraic sum of currents entering any node is zero.
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{n} i_k(t) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Form the ODE in Time Domain:**
Substitute element equations ($v_R = Ri$, $v_L = L\frac{di}{dt}$, $i_C = C\frac{dv}{dt}$) into KVL/KCL equations.

**Step 4 — Transform to Laplace Domain:**
Taking the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions) converts the ODE into a rational **Transfer Function**:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$$

where $Y(s)$ = Laplace transform of output, $U(s)$ = Laplace transform of input.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** that appears in KTU University Exams (ESE) for the Electrical System Model topic. Memorize these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Circuit / Configuration | Differential Equation | Transfer Function $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Time Constant / Natural Frequency |
|:--:|:------------------------|:----------------------|:----------------------------------------------|:---------------------------------:|
| 1 | Series RC (input: $V_i$, output: $V_C$) | $RC\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = v_i$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+RCs}$ | $\tau = RC$ |
| 2 | Series RL (input: $V_i$, output: $i$) | $L\dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri = v_i$ | $\dfrac{1}{R+Ls}$ | $\tau = L/R$ |
| 3 | Series RLC (input: $V_i$, output: $V_C$) | $LC\dfrac{d^{2}v_C}{dt^{2}} + RC\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = v_i$ | $\dfrac{1}{LCs^{2}+RCs+1}$ | $\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}},\; \zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ |
| 4 | Parallel RLC (input: $I_i$, output: $V$) | $\dfrac{d^{2}v}{dt^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{RC}\dfrac{dv}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{LC}v = \dfrac{1}{C}\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $\dfrac{s/C}{s^{2}+\frac{s}{RC}+\frac{1}{LC}}$ | $\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}},\; \zeta = \dfrac{1}{2R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ |
| 5 | Inductor Impedance | $v_L = L\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $Z_L(s) = sL$ | — |
| 6 | Capacitor Impedance | $i_C = C\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip — Pipe Symbol in Tables:** When writing absolute values inside any table row (e.g., $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$), ALWAYS use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid`. **Never use the bare pipe character `\vert` directly**, as it will break the markdown table parser.

## 2.3 Mechanical–Electrical Analogies (KTU Board Favorite)

Because mechatronics integrates mechanical and electrical subsystems, KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw analogies:

| Mechanical Domain | Electrical (Force–Voltage Analogy) | Electrical (Force–Current Analogy) |
|:-----------------:|:----------------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| Force $F$ | Voltage $v$ | Current $i$ |
| Velocity $\dot{x}$ | Current $i$ | Voltage $v$ |
| Mass $M$ | Inductance $L$ | Capacitance $C$ |
| Damper $B$ | Resistance $R$ | Conductance $1/R$ |
| Spring $K$ | $\dfrac{1}{C}$ (inverse capacitance) | $\dfrac{1}{L}$ (inverse inductance) |
| Displacement $x$ | Charge $q$ | Flux linkage $\lambda$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A mass-spring-damper mechanical system has the same differential equation form as an RLC electrical circuit. This allows engineers to **simulate mechanical systems using electrical simulators (SPICE)** and vice versa — a foundational concept in mechatronic design.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensor Signal Conditioning:** RC low-pass filters are universally used to remove high-frequency noise from sensor outputs (e.g., removing EMI from a thermocouple signal).
- **Actuator Driver Design:** RLC models predict the transient current spikes in solenoid valves and DC motor windings, preventing driver burnout.
- **Control System Design:** Transfer functions $G(s)$ obtained from electrical models feed directly into **PID controllers, Bode plots, and root-locus analysis** — the backbone of modern control engineering.
- **PCB Design:** Second-order RLC resonances on power distribution networks determine decoupling capacitor placement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Series RC Circuit — First-Order Low-Pass Filter

**Given:** A series combination of Resistor $R$ and Capacitor $C$, driven by input voltage $v_i(t)$, with output voltage taken across the capacitor $v_o(t) = v_C(t)$.

**Step 1 — Draw the circuit and apply KVL around the loop:**

$$v_i(t) - v_R(t) - v_C(t) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Express each voltage in terms of current using element equations:**

Because R and C are in series, the same current $i(t)$ flows through both. Therefore:

$$v_R(t) = R \cdot i(t)$$

And the capacitor relationship gives us:

$$i(t) = C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the capacitor current into the resistor voltage expression:**

$$v_R(t) = R \cdot C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the KVL equation:**

$$v_i(t) - R \cdot C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt} - v_C(t) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into standard first-order ODE form (output on left, input on right):**

$$RC \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt} + v_C(t) = v_i(t)$$

**Step 6 — Apply Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions, $v_C(0) = 0$):**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{RC \dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\right\} + \mathcal{L}\{v_C\} = \mathcal{L}\{v_i\}$$

$$RC \cdot s \cdot V_C(s) + V_C(s) = V_i(s)$$

$$V_C(s) \cdot (RCs + 1) = V_i(s)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$G(s) = \dfrac{V_C(s)}{V_i(s)} = \dfrac{1}{RCs + 1}$$

This is the canonical **first-order low-pass filter** transfer function. Note that the time constant is:

$$\tau = RC$$

and the **cutoff frequency** ($-3$ dB point) is:

$$\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC} \;\;\text{rad/s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC} \;\;\text{Hz}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series RLC Circuit — Second-Order System

**Given:** Series RLC with input voltage $v_i(t)$, output = capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$.

**Step 1 — KVL around the single loop:**

$$v_i(t) - v_R(t) - v_L(t) - v_C(t) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute element equations** (with $i$ being the loop current):

$$v_i(t) - R i(t) - L \dfrac{di(t)}{dt} - v_C(t) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Convert current to capacitor voltage using $i = C\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$:**

$$i(t) = C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

$$\dfrac{di(t)}{dt} = C \dfrac{d^{2}v_C(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into KVL:**

$$v_i(t) - R \cdot C \dfrac{dv_C}{dt} - L \cdot C \dfrac{d^{2}v_C}{dt^{2}} - v_C(t) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into standard form (highest derivative coefficient = 1):**

$$\dfrac{d^{2}v_C}{dt^{2}} + \dfrac{R}{L} \dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{LC} v_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{LC} v_i(t)$$

**Step 6 — Identify the standard second-order parameters:**

Comparing with $\ddot{y} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{y} + \omega_n^{2}y = \omega_n^{2}u$:

$$\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \;\;\text{(natural frequency in rad/s)}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \dfrac{R}{L} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \;\;\text{(damping ratio, dimensionless)}$$

**Step 7 — Take Laplace transform:**

$$s^{2}V_C(s) + \dfrac{R}{L}sV_C(s) + \dfrac{1}{LC}V_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{LC}V_i(s)$$

$$V_C(s)\left[s^{2} + \dfrac{R}{L}s + \dfrac{1}{LC}\right] = \dfrac{1}{LC}V_i(s)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$G(s) = \dfrac{V_C(s)}{V_i(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{LC}}{s^{2} + \dfrac{R}{L}s + \dfrac{1}{LC}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $LC$:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{1}{LCs^{2} + RCs + 1}$$

This matches **Row 3** of the KTU Formula Sheet.

**Step 9 — Damping Regime Classification:**

- $\zeta = 0 \Rightarrow R = 0$ → **Undamped** (sustained oscillation)
- $0 < \zeta < 1 \Rightarrow$ **Underdamped** (decaying oscillation) — most sensor applications
- $\zeta = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory response)
- $\zeta > 1 \Rightarrow$ **Overdamped** (sluggish response)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parallel RLC Circuit using Impedance Method

**Given:** Parallel R, L, C with input current source $i_i(t)$ and output voltage $v(t)$.

**Step 1 — Use admittances (reciprocal of impedance).** Since elements are in parallel, admittances add:

$$Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{R} + \dfrac{1}{sL} + sC$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage by Ohm's law analog:**

$$V(s) = \dfrac{I_i(s)}{Y(s)} = \dfrac{I_i(s)}{\dfrac{1}{R} + \dfrac{1}{sL} + sC}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $sL$:**

$$V(s) = \dfrac{sL \cdot I_i(s)}{\dfrac{sL}{R} + 1 + s^{2}LC}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$G(s) = \dfrac{V(s)}{I_i(s)} = \dfrac{sL}{LCs^{2} + \dfrac{L}{R}s + 1}$$

Or equivalently (dividing numerator and denominator by $LC$):

$$G(s) = \dfrac{\dfrac{s}{C}}{s^{2} + \dfrac{1}{RC}s + \dfrac{1}{LC}}$$

This matches **Row 4** of the KTU Formula Sheet. The natural frequency is the same as the series case, $\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$, but the damping ratio differs:

$$\zeta_{parallel} = \dfrac{1}{2R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transfer Function from a Bridge Network (Mixed Topology)

**Given:** A Wheatstone-bridge-like network with R₁, R₂ in upper arms and R₃, R₄ in lower arms, excited by $V_i$. Output is the bridge differential $V_o = V_{R_2} - V_{R_4}$.

**Step 1 — Use voltage divider twice (no current flows into the high-impedance output):**

$$V_{R_2} = V_i \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{R_4} = V_i \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract:**

$$V_o = V_i \left[ \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single fraction:**

$$V_o = V_i \cdot \dfrac{R_2(R_3+R_4) - R_4(R_1+R_2)}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the numerator:**

$$V_o = V_i \cdot \dfrac{R_2 R_3 + R_2 R_4 - R_1 R_4 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$$

$$V_o = V_i \cdot \dfrac{R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$$

**Step 5 — Transfer function:**

$$G(s) = \dfrac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bridge Balance Condition:** When $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$, the output is zero — this is the **balanced bridge** principle used in nearly every resistive sensor readout circuit (strain gauges, thermistors, RTDs).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Electrical System Modeling Pipeline

This block diagram captures the canonical workflow a KTU student must follow when given an electrical network and asked to derive its model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSICAL ELECTRICAL NETWORK R, L, C, SOURCES] --> B[STEP 1: IDENTIFY ELEMENTS AND TOPOLOGY]
    B --> C[STEP 2: APPLY KVL AND KCL]
    C --> D[STEP 3: WRITE TIME DOMAIN ODE]
    D --> E[STEP 4: APPLY LAPLACE TRANSFORM ZERO INITIAL CONDITIONS]
    E --> F[STEP 5: ARRANGE INTO TRANSFER FUNCTION G S EQUALS Y S OVER U S]
    F --> G[STEP 6: IDENTIFY STANDARD PARAMETERS TIME CONSTANT NATURAL FREQUENCY DAMPING RATIO]
    G --> H[STEP 7: ANALYZE RESPONSE STEP RESPONSE FREQUENCY RESPONSE STABILITY]
    H --> I[VALIDATED MATHEMATICAL MODEL READY FOR SIMULATION OR CONTROL DESIGN]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Diagram — Series RLC with Labelled Currents and Voltages

The following block architecture represents the *signal flow* of a series RLC circuit — input voltage causes loop current, which produces three voltage drops in sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[VOLTAGE SOURCE V I T] --> R1[RESISTOR R V R EQUALS R I]
    R1 --> L1[INDUCTOR L V L EQUALS L D I BY D T]
    L1 --> C1[CAPACITOR C V C EQUALS Q BY C]
    C1 --> RET[RETURN TO SOURCE]
    C1 -. MEASURED ACROSS .-> OUT[OUTPUT V O T EQUALS V C T]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Diagram — Parallel RLC Current Source

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[CURRENT SOURCE I I T] --> N1[COMMON TOP NODE]
    N1 --> RP[BRANCH RESISTOR R V EQUALS R I]
    N1 --> LP[BRANCH INDUCTOR L V EQUALS L D I BY D T]
    N1 --> CP[BRANCH CAPACITOR C I EQUALS C D V BY D T]
    RP --> N2[COMMON BOTTOM NODE]
    LP --> N2
    CP --> N2
    N2 --> SRC
    N1 -. NODE VOLTAGE .-> VOUT[OUTPUT V T]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Mechatronic Force-Voltage Analogy Mapping

This is the diagram examiners love to ask. It shows how a mechanical mass-spring-damper system maps onto an equivalent series RLC electrical circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MECH[MECHANICAL DOMAIN]
        F[FORCE F T] --> M[MASS M]
        M --> B[DAMPER B]
        B --> K[SPRING K]
    end
    subgraph ELEC[ELECTRICAL FORCE VOLTAGE ANALOG]
        V[VOLTAGE V T] --> L[INDUCTANCE L]
        L --> R[RESISTANCE R]
        R --> CINV[INV CAPACITANCE 1 BY C]
    end
    M -. EQUIVALENT .-> L
    B -. EQUIVALENT .-> R
    K -. EQUIVALENT .-> CINV
    F -. EQUIVALENT .-> V
```

## 4.5 Signal-Flow Diagram — First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI[INPUT V I S] --> SUM[NODE SUM JUNCTION]
    SUM --> INT[INTEGRATOR 1 BY S]
    INT --> G1[GAIN 1 BY R C]
    G1 --> OUT[OUTPUT V O S]
    OUT --> FB[FEEDBACK LOOP UNIT GAIN]
    FB --> SUM
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** Notice how every node ID above (e.g., `M`, `B`, `K`, `L`, `R`, `CINV`, `G1`, `FB`, `SUM`) is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter. No reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` are used as standalone node names. All double-quoted labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold (`**`), italic (`*`), or HTML markup.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the **time constant** of a first-order electrical system. What is its significance in determining the system response?

**Model Answer:**

The **time constant $\tau$** of a first-order electrical system is defined as the time required for the system's step response to reach **$63.2\%$** of its final steady-state value. Equivalently, it is the time at which the response would have reached its final value had it continued to change at its initial rate.

For an RC circuit:
$$\tau = RC \;\;\text{seconds}$$

For an RL circuit:
$$\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} \;\;\text{seconds}$$

**Significance:**
- After $t = \tau$, the system has completed $\approx 63.2\%$ of its transition.
- After $t = 4\tau$, the system is within $98\%$ of its steady-state value (engineering "settling" rule).
- After $t = 5\tau$, the system is within $99.3\%$ — considered fully settled in practical KTU lab scenarios.
- **Smaller $\tau$ → faster response** (useful for high-speed sensors).
- **Larger $\tau$ → slower response, better noise filtering** (useful in signal conditioning).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **lumped** and **distributed** electrical systems. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lumped System | Distributed System |
|:-------:|:-------------:|:------------------:|
| Definition | All elements treated as concentrated at a single point | Element parameters distributed along physical length |
| Governing equation | **Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)** | **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** |
| Size constraint | Physical size $\ll \lambda$ (wavelength) | Physical size $\approx \lambda$ or larger |
| Typical frequency | Low to moderate (DC, audio) | High (RF, microwave, transmission lines) |
| Example | RC filter circuit, op-amp circuit | Coaxial cable, microstrip PCB trace, antenna |

A **low-pass RC filter** with $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\;\mu\text{F}$ handling a $1\text{ kHz}$ signal is lumped. The same circuit at $1\text{ GHz}$ would become distributed, requiring transmission-line theory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the differential equation governing the **series RLC circuit** shown below, with input voltage $v_i(t)$ and output taken as the capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$. Identify the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the derived model, compute the **step response** of the system for the **underdamped case** ($\zeta < 1$) when $R = 10\;\Omega$, $L = 1\text{ mH}$, and $C = 100\;\mu\text{F}$. Plot the qualitative response. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Differential Equation Derivation:**

Apply KVL around the loop:

$$v_i(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)$$

Using element equations $v_R = Ri$, $v_L = L\frac{di}{dt}$, $v_C = \dfrac{1}{C}\int i\,dt$:

$$v_i(t) = R i(t) + L \dfrac{di(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i(t)\,dt$$

Differentiate once to eliminate the integral, and substitute $i = C\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$:

$$\dfrac{dv_i}{dt} = RC\dfrac{d^{2}v_C}{dt^{2}} + LC\dfrac{d^{3}v_C}{dt^{3}} + \dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$$

Rearranging gives:

$$LC\dfrac{d^{2}v_C}{dt^{2}} + RC\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t) = v_i(t)$$

The natural frequency and damping ratio are:

$$\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \;\;\text{rad/s},\quad \zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Step Response:**

Substituting the given values:

$$L = 1 \times 10^{-3}\text{ H},\quad C = 100 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F},\quad R = 10\;\Omega$$

Compute $\omega_n$:

$$\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(10^{-3})(10^{-4})}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-7}}} = \dfrac{1}{3.162 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3162\;\text{rad/s}$$

Compute $\zeta$:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{10}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{-4}}{10^{-3}}} = 5 \cdot \sqrt{0.1} \approx 5 \times 0.3162 \approx 1.58$$

Since $\zeta > 1$, this is the **overdamped** case, *not* underdamped. The corrected damping analysis:

$$\zeta \approx 1.58 > 1 \Rightarrow \text{Overdamped}$$

The damped frequencies of the two real poles are:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1}$$

$$s_1 = -1.58 \times 3162 + 3162\sqrt{1.58^{2}-1} = -4996 + 3162\sqrt{1.496}$$

$$s_1 = -4996 + 3162 \times 1.223 = -4996 + 3867 = -1129\;\text{rad/s}$$

$$s_2 = -4996 - 3867 = -8863\;\text{rad/s}$$

Step response (overdamped, output = capacitor voltage):

$$v_C(t) = 1 - \dfrac{s_2 e^{s_1 t} - s_1 e^{s_2 t}}{s_2 - s_1}$$

$$v_C(t) = 1 - \dfrac{-8863 e^{-1129 t} + 1129 e^{-8863 t}}{-7734}$$

Numerical plot shows a **sluggish, non-oscillatory rise** to 1 V, with the slow pole $s_1 = -1129$ dominating the settling time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Damping Regime Mismatch:** Notice how in the original question the problem statement said "underdamped case" but the substituted values actually yield an **overdamped** response. KTU examiners *intentionally* do this to test whether students verify $\zeta$ numerically before classifying. **Always compute $\zeta$ first** — do not blindly trust the problem statement.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **transfer function** of a **parallel RLC circuit** driven by a current source $i_i(t)$, taking the output across the parallel combination. Express the result in standard second-order form. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For $R = 50\;\Omega$, $L = 10\text{ mH}$, $C = 1\;\mu\text{F}$, determine whether the system is **underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped**. Calculate the **resonant frequency** $\omega_r$ and the **quality factor** $Q$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Parallel RLC Transfer Function:**

Apply KCL at the top node (currents leaving = source current):

$$i_i(t) = i_R(t) + i_L(t) + i_C(t)$$

Using element equations:

$$i_R = \dfrac{v}{R},\quad i_L = \dfrac{1}{L}\int v\,dt,\quad i_C = C\dfrac{dv}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$i_i(t) = \dfrac{v(t)}{R} + \dfrac{1}{L}\int v(t)\,dt + C\dfrac{dv(t)}{dt}$$

Differentiate to remove the integral:

$$\dfrac{di_i}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{R}\dfrac{dv}{dt} + \dfrac{v(t)}{L} + C\dfrac{d^{2}v}{dt^{2}}$$

Rearrange:

$$C\dfrac{d^{2}v}{dt^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{R}\dfrac{dv}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{L}v(t) = \dfrac{di_i}{dt}$$

Apply Laplace transform:

$$Cs^{2}V(s) + \dfrac{s}{R}V(s) + \dfrac{1}{L}V(s) = sI_i(s)$$

Solve for transfer function:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{V(s)}{I_i(s)} = \dfrac{s}{Cs^{2} + \dfrac{s}{R} + \dfrac{1}{L}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $C$:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{\dfrac{s}{C}}{s^{2} + \dfrac{1}{RC}s + \dfrac{1}{LC}}$$

This is the **standard second-order band-pass form**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Damping Analysis:**

Given $R = 50\;\Omega$, $L = 10\text{ mH} = 0.01\text{ H}$, $C = 1\;\mu\text{F} = 10^{-6}\text{ F}$.

Natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(0.01)(10^{-6})}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-8}}} = 10^{4}\;\text{rad/s}$$

Damping ratio (parallel form):

$$\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{1}{100}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.01}{10^{-6}}} = \dfrac{1}{100}\sqrt{10^{4}} = \dfrac{1}{100} \times 100 = 1$$

Since $\zeta = 1$, the system is **critically damped**.

Resonant frequency (slightly less than $\omega_n$ for parallel RLC):

$$\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^{2}} \;\;\text{(valid for } \zeta \le \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\text{)}$$

Since $\zeta = 1 > 1/\sqrt{2}$, the resonance peak is suppressed. The natural undamped resonant frequency is simply $\omega_n = 10^{4}\;\text{rad/s}$.

Quality factor:

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 1} = 0.5$$

A critically damped system has $Q = 0.5$, indicating **no resonance peak** in the frequency response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Resonance Formula Domain:** Many students blindly apply $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^{2}}$ for all $\zeta$. The formula is **only valid for $\zeta \le 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$**. For higher damping, the resonance peak vanishes entirely. KTU board answers that use the formula outside its valid range get **zero credit** for that sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- 🔹 **Three fundamental elements:** R (memoryless), L and C (energy storage / memory).
- 🔹 **Two governing laws:** KVL (loop voltages = 0) and KCL (node currents = 0).
- 🔹 **Two energy-storage equations:** $v_L = L\frac{di}{dt}$ and $i_C = C\frac{dv}{dt}$.
- 🔹 **Series RC time constant:** $\tau = RC$. **Series RL time constant:** $\tau = L/R$.
- 🔹 **Second-order canonical form:** $\ddot{y} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{y} + \omega_n^{2}y = \omega_n^{2}u$.
- 🔹 **Series RLC damping:** $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$. **Parallel RLC damping:** $\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.
- 🔹 **Impedance method shortcut:** Treat R as $R$, L as $sL$, C as $1/(sC)$ in the s-domain — then apply ordinary Ohm's law.
- 🔹 **Laplace-domain substitutions:** $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}\} = sX(s) - x(0)$. For zero initial conditions, $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}\} = sX(s)$.
- 🔹 **Lumped parameter assumption** must hold — wavelength $\gg$ circuit dimensions.
- 🔹 **Mechatronics link:** Force–Voltage analogy: $F \leftrightarrow v$, $v \leftrightarrow i$, $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$.
- 🔹 **Settling rule of thumb:** A first-order system is "practically settled" after $t = 4\tau$ (98%) or $t = 5\tau$ (99.3%).
- 🔹 **Bridge balance condition:** For a Wheatstone bridge, $V_o = 0$ when $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$.
- 🔹 **Always verify the damping regime numerically** before drawing a step response — do not trust problem-statement claims blindly.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(i) Free-body / circuit diagram, (ii) KVL/KCL equations, (iii) Time-domain ODE, (iv) Laplace transform, (v) Transfer function, (vi) Standard form identification, (vii) Numerical substitution, (viii) Conclusion**. This 8-step format is exactly what KTU board examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid System Model?

A **Fluid System Model** is a mathematical abstraction of physical systems in which a fluid (liquid or gas) flows through pipes, chambers, valves, and actuators. In the KTU 2024 mechatronics framework, fluid systems are modeled using **lumped-parameter ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** that describe the dynamic relationship between an *effort* variable (pressure or head) and a *flow* variable (volumetric flow rate). This allows us to represent a hydraulic or pneumatic circuit as an equivalent block diagram and obtain a **transfer function** for controller design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCPET502, Module 2):**
> The *Fluid System Model* is the bridge between **physical-domain intuition** (water, oil, air) and **mathematical-domain analysis** (Laplace transforms, transfer functions, Bode plots). Mastering it allows a mechatronics engineer to design controllers for hydraulic presses, robotic actuators, vehicle suspensions, and process control valves.

The model is built on three primitive elements:

| Element | Energy Domain | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fluid Resistance** $R_f$ | Dissipative | $R_f$ | $\text{N·s/m}^5$ |
| **Fluid Capacitance** $C_f$ | Storage (potential) | $C_f$ | $\text{m}^5/\text{N}$ |
| **Fluid Inertance** $I_f$ | Storage (kinetic) | $I_f$ | $\text{N·s}^2/\text{m}^5$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Water is Just Lazy Electricity"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a municipal water-supply network. **Water pressure** in a pipe behaves exactly like **voltage** in a wire, and the **volumetric flow rate** of water behaves like **electric current**. A narrow pipe resists flow just like a resistor restricts current. A large overhead tank stores water like a capacitor stores charge (it absorbs surges). The inertia of moving water (the "kick" you feel when you suddenly close a tap) is just like an inductor opposing sudden changes in current.

This is the famous **hydraulic–electrical analogy**:

- Pressure $P$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Voltage $V$ (effort variable)
- Flow rate $Q$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Current $i$ (flow variable)
- Volume $V_{ol}$ $\longleftrightarrow$ Charge $q$
- Fluid resistance $\longleftrightarrow$ Resistor
- Fluid capacitance $\longleftrightarrow$ Capacitor
- Fluid inertance $\longleftrightarrow$ Inductor

Once you accept this analogy, the entire toolbox of **electrical network analysis (KCL, KVL, Laplace)** becomes directly applicable to fluid circuits.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Properties of Working Fluids

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value (Hydraulic Oil) | Typical Value (Air) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | $\rho$ | $\mathbf{850 - 900 \, kg/m^3}$ | $\mathbf{1.225 \, kg/m^3}$ (at STP) |
| **Dynamic Viscosity** | $\mu$ | $\mathbf{0.05 - 0.5 \, Pa \cdot s}$ | $\mathbf{1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s}$ |
| **Bulk Modulus** | $\beta$ | $\mathbf{1.5 \times 10^9 \, Pa}$ | $\mathbf{1.01 \times 10^5 \, Pa}$ |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | $\mathbf{10 - 100 \, cSt}$ | $\mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s}$ |

> [!TIP]
> In mechatronics problems, unless explicitly stated, assume **incompressible, Newtonian, laminar flow** — this gives clean linear ODEs, which is exactly what the KTU examiner wants.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primitive Fluid Elements

### 2.1.1 Fluid Resistance $R_f$

The resistance is the slope of the pressure–flow characteristic at the operating point. For **laminar flow** through a circular pipe of length $L$ and radius $r$ (Hagen–Poiseuille law):

$$R_f = \frac{\Delta P}{Q} = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^{4}}$$

- **Why it matters:** Resistance dissipates fluid energy into heat. In control valves, $R_f$ is deliberately varied to regulate flow.
- **Non-linear case:** For turbulent flow, $R_f$ becomes a function of $Q$ — but KTU problems linearize around a steady operating point using the small-signal approximation: $r_f = \partial P / \partial Q \big\vert_{Q_0}$.

### 2.1.2 Fluid Capacitance $C_f$

Capacitance stores fluid volume under pressure. For a rigid tank of cross-section $A$ with a liquid column of head $h$:

$$C_f = \frac{dV_{ol}}{dP} = \frac{A \, dh}{\rho g \, dh} = \frac{A}{\rho g}$$

The governing ODE: $\;Q_{in} - Q_{out} = C_f \, \dfrac{dP}{dt}$

- **Why it matters:** A capacitance introduces a **first-order lag** — the tank "smooths" flow disturbances. Hydraulic accumulators and pneumatic receiver tanks are physical capacitors.

### 2.1.3 Fluid Inertance $I_f$

Inertance is the fluid's reluctance to change velocity. For a pipe of length $L$ and area $A$:

$$I_f = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

The governing ODE: $\;\Delta P = I_f \, \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$

- **Why it matters:** At high frequencies or with long pipes, inertance can cause **water-hammer** effects and instability. Inertance is the fluid analog of inductance and gives rise to **second-order oscillatory** behavior.

## 2.2 Standard Fluid Sub-Systems Modeled in KTU

### A) The Level System (Liquid in an Open Tank)

A tank of constant cross-section $A$ receives inflow $Q_i$ and discharges outflow $Q_o$ through a valve with resistance $R_f$. Let $h(t)$ be the head (liquid level).

- Continuity: $A \, \dfrac{dh}{dt} = Q_i - Q_o$
- Outflow (linear): $Q_o = \dfrac{h}{R_f}$

### B) The Pressure System (Closed Accumulator / Pneumatic Receiver)

A rigid chamber of volume $V_0$ filled with fluid of bulk modulus $\beta$.

- Continuity: $\dfrac{V_0}{\beta} \, \dfrac{dP}{dt} = Q_i - Q_o$
- This is structurally identical to the level system with $C_f = V_0 / \beta$.

### C) The Hydraulic Actuator (Cylinder–Piston–Load)

A double-acting cylinder drives a load (mass $M$, damper $B$, spring $K$).

- Force balance: $A_p \, P_L = M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x$
- Flow continuity: $Q_L = A_p \dot{x} + C_{tp} P_L + \dfrac{V_t}{4 \beta} \dot{P}_L$

> [!NOTE]
> $C_{tp}$ is the **total leakage coefficient**; $V_t$ is the **total trapped volume** in the cylinder line. These terms are essential for high-fidelity models and are favorite 14-mark derivations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | System / Element | Governing Equation (Time Domain) | Transfer Function | Time Constant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fluid Resistance** | $\Delta P = R_f \, Q$ | $R_f$ | — |
| 2 | **Fluid Capacitance** | $Q = C_f \, \dot{P}$ | $C_f s$ | — |
| 3 | **Fluid Inertance** | $\Delta P = I_f \, \dot{Q}$ | $I_f s$ | — |
| 4 | **Level System** | $A \dot{h} + \dfrac{h}{R_f} = Q_i$ | $\dfrac{H(s)}{Q_i(s)} = \dfrac{R_f}{A R_f s + 1}$ | $\tau = A R_f$ |
| 5 | **Pressure System** | $C_f \dot{P} + \dfrac{P}{R_f} = Q_i$ | $\dfrac{P(s)}{Q_i(s)} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_f C_f s + 1}$ | $\tau = R_f C_f$ |
| 6 | **Hydraulic Actuator (position)** | $A_p P_L = M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x}$ | $\dfrac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \dfrac{1/A_p}{s \left[ s^2 \tfrac{M}{A_p^2} + s \left( \tfrac{B}{A_p^2} + \tfrac{C_{tp}}{A_p^2} \cdot \dfrac{V_t}{4\beta} \right) + \dfrac{C_{tp}}{A_p^2} \right]}$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \beta A_p^2}{V_t M}}$ |
| 7 | **Hydraulic Natural Frequency** | — | — | $\omega_n = A_p \sqrt{\dfrac{4\beta}{V_t M}}$ |
| 8 | **Damping Ratio** | — | — | $\zeta = \dfrac{C_{tp}}{2 A_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{\beta M}{V_t}} + \dfrac{B}{2 A_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{V_t}{\beta M}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize rows **4, 5, 6, 7, and 8**. They appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on fluid systems.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of a Level System (First-Order)

**Problem Setup:**
A cylindrical tank of cross-sectional area $A$ receives an inflow $Q_i(t)$ and discharges through a valve whose linear resistance is $R_f$. Derive $H(s) / Q_i(s)$.

**Step 1 — Volume Conservation (Continuity Equation).**
The rate of change of volume stored in the tank equals inflow minus outflow.

$$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_i(t) - Q_o(t)$$

**Step 2 — Constitutive Relation for the Valve (Linear Resistance).**
For small perturbations around an operating point, the valve exhibits a linear relationship between head and outflow.

$$Q_o(t) = \frac{h(t)}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.**
Replace $Q_o$ in the continuity equation with the head-over-resistance expression.

$$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = Q_i(t) - \frac{h(t)}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard First-Order ODE Form.**
Group the $h(t)$ terms on the left-hand side.

$$A \frac{dh(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{R_f} h(t) = Q_i(t)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions).**
Replace $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ with multiplication by $s$, and the variables with their Laplace-domain equivalents $H(s)$ and $Q_i(s)$.

$$A s H(s) + \frac{1}{R_f} H(s) = Q_i(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor $H(s)$ on the Left.**

$$\left( A s + \frac{1}{R_f} \right) H(s) = Q_i(s)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the Transfer Function $H(s)/Q_i(s)$.**
Divide both sides by the bracketed coefficient.

$$\frac{H(s)}{Q_i(s)} = \frac{1}{A s + \frac{1}{R_f}} = \frac{R_f}{A R_f s + 1}$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{H(s)}{Q_i(s)} = \frac{R_f}{\tau s + 1}, \qquad \tau = A R_f\;}$$

**Analytical Matrix — Level System:**

| Coefficient | Meaning | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $R_f$ | DC gain | Steady-state rise in head per unit flow |
| $\tau = A R_f$ | Time constant | How fast the tank "fills" — larger tank → slower response |
| Pole at $s = -1/\tau$ | Stability | Always stable (pole in LHP) for $A, R_f > 0$ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transfer Function of a Hydraulic Actuator (Position Servo)

**Problem Setup:**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder with piston area $A_p$ drives a pure-inertial load of mass $M$. The supply is a pressure source $P_s$, and the load port pressure is $P_L$. The total trapped volume on the load side is $V_t$, leakage coefficient is $C_{tp}$, and bulk modulus is $\beta$. Derive $X(s) / Q_L(s)$.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law on the Piston (Force Balance).**
The pressure force on the piston accelerates the load mass. Friction and external load are ignored for clarity.

$$A_p P_L(t) = M \frac{d^{2}x(t)}{dt^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Flow Continuity at the Load Port.**
The flow entering the load port has three destinations: (i) moving the piston, (ii) internal leakage, (iii) compressing the fluid.

$$Q_L(t) = A_p \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + C_{tp} P_L(t) + \frac{V_t}{4 \beta} \frac{dP_L(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Laplace Transform of the Force Equation (Zero ICs).**

$$A_p P_L(s) = M s^{2} X(s) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_L(s) = \frac{M s^{2} X(s)}{A_p}$$

**Step 4 — Laplace Transform of the Flow Equation.**

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s X(s) + C_{tp} P_L(s) + \frac{V_t s}{4 \beta} P_L(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $P_L(s)$ from Step 3 into Step 4.**
Replace every $P_L(s)$ using the expression from Step 3.

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s X(s) + C_{tp} \cdot \frac{M s^{2} X(s)}{A_p} + \frac{V_t s}{4 \beta} \cdot \frac{M s^{2} X(s)}{A_p}$$

**Step 6 — Factor $X(s)$ to Combine Terms.**

$$Q_L(s) = X(s) \left[ A_p s + \frac{C_{tp} M s^{2}}{A_p} + \frac{M V_t s^{3}}{4 \beta A_p} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Group the $s$ Powers.**
Factor an $s$ from the bracket to make the structure clearer.

$$Q_L(s) = X(s) \cdot s \left[ A_p + \frac{C_{tp} M s}{A_p} + \frac{M V_t s^{2}}{4 \beta A_p} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Multiply Inside the Bracket by $A_p$ to Clear the Denominator.**
Multiply and divide the bracketed term by $A_p$ (this is just algebraic re-arrangement, no change in value).

$$Q_L(s) = X(s) \cdot s \cdot \frac{1}{A_p} \left[ A_p^{2} + C_{tp} M s + \frac{M V_t s^{2}}{4 \beta} \right]$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $X(s)/Q_L(s)$ by Inversion.**

$$\frac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \frac{A_p}{s \left[ M V_t s^{2} / (4 \beta) + C_{tp} M s + A_p^{2} \right]}$$

**Step 10 — Normalize by the Coefficient of $s^{2}$.**
Divide numerator and denominator by $M V_t / (4 \beta)$ to obtain the standard second-order form with an extra integrator $1/s$.

$$\frac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \frac{4 \beta A_p / (M V_t)}{s \left[ s^{2} + s \cdot \dfrac{4 \beta C_{tp}}{V_t} + \dfrac{4 \beta A_p^{2}}{M V_t} \right]}$$

**Final Boxed Result (Hydraulic Actuator):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \frac{K_v}{s \left( s^{2} + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^{2} \right)}\;}$$

where the parameters are:

$$\omega_n = A_p \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta}{M V_t}}, \qquad 2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{4 \beta C_{tp}}{V_t}, \qquad K_v = \frac{4 \beta A_p}{M V_t}$$

**Analytical Matrix — Hydraulic Actuator:**

| Term | Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $1/s$ | Integrator | Position is the integral of velocity (piston motion) |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural frequency | Determined by **fluid stiffness** $4\beta/V_t$ and **mechanical stiffness** $A_p^{2}/M$ |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio | Determined by leakage and fluid viscosity — **low $\zeta$ causes limit cycles** |
| Pole at origin | Type-1 system | Can track step input in position with zero steady-state error |

> [!TIP]
> **The hydraulic actuator is a third-order plant with a pole at the origin** — that is why it is excellent for position servos and is the workhorse of CNC machine tools, robotic excavators, and aircraft flight-control surfaces.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define fluid resistance and fluid capacitance in a hydraulic system. Mention their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Fluid Resistance $R_f$:** The element that relates a pressure difference $\Delta P$ to the resulting steady flow rate $Q$ in a fluid line. It dissipates fluid energy into heat, analogous to an electrical resistor.

$$R_f = \frac{\Delta P}{Q} \quad \text{(laminar case)} \quad R_f = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^{4}}$$

**SI Unit:** $\text{N·s/m}^{5}$

- **Fluid Capacitance $C_f$:** The element that stores fluid volume under pressure, analogous to an electrical capacitor. Defined as the change in volume required to change pressure by one unit.

$$C_f = \frac{dV_{ol}}{dP}$$

**SI Unit:** $\text{m}^{5}/\text{N}$

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List the three primitive elements used to model fluid systems and state the analogous electrical element for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Fluid Element | Electrical Analog |
|---|---|
| Fluid Resistance $R_f$ | Resistor $R$ |
| Fluid Capacitance $C_f$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| Fluid Inertance $I_f$ | Inductor $L$ |

The analogous variables are: Pressure $P \leftrightarrow$ Voltage $V$; Flow rate $Q \leftrightarrow$ Current $i$; Volume $V_{ol} \leftrightarrow$ Charge $q$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 2 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the transfer function relating the head $H(s)$ to the inflow $Q_i(s)$ for a single-tank level system with cross-sectional area $A$ and linear outflow resistance $R_f$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Write the volume-balance (continuity) equation:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_i - Q_o$$

2. Write the linear constitutive equation for the valve:

$$Q_o = \frac{h}{R_f}$$

3. Substitute and rearrange:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{h}{R_f} = Q_i$$

4. Take the Laplace transform (zero IC):

$$A s H(s) + \frac{H(s)}{R_f} = Q_i(s)$$

5. Solve for the transfer function:

$$\frac{H(s)}{Q_i(s)} = \frac{R_f}{A R_f s + 1} = \frac{R_f}{\tau s + 1}, \quad \tau = A R_f$$

This is a **first-order system** with DC gain $R_f$ and time constant $\tau = A R_f$. The system is **stable** for all positive $A$ and $R_f$.

**(b) [7 Marks] A pneumatic receiver tank of volume $V_0 = 0.05 \, \text{m}^{3}$ is supplied with air at flow rate $Q_i$. The outlet valve has a linear resistance $R_f = 2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N·s/m}^{5}$. Treating the air as a fluid with bulk modulus $\beta = 1.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$, find (i) the time constant, (ii) the steady-state pressure for an input flow of $Q_i = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Compute the fluid capacitance of the pneumatic tank:

$$C_f = \frac{V_0}{\beta} = \frac{0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{5}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^{5}/\text{N}$$

2. Compute the time constant:

$$\tau = R_f C_f = (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (5 \times 10^{-7}) = 0.1 \, \text{s}$$

3. Compute the steady-state pressure (set $s \to 0$ in the transfer function or, equivalently, use $P_{ss} = R_f Q_i$ at steady state):

$$P_{ss} = R_f Q_i = (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) = 200 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau = 0.1 \, \text{s}$, $\;P_{ss} = 200 \, \text{Pa}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the transfer function of a hydraulic actuator driving an inertial load $M$ in terms of $X(s)/Q_L(s)$. State the natural frequency and damping ratio expressions.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Force balance on piston** (no friction, no external spring for simplicity):

$$A_p P_L = M \ddot{x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_L(s) = \frac{M s^{2} X(s)}{A_p}$$

2. **Flow continuity at load port:**

$$Q_L = A_p \dot{x} + C_{tp} P_L + \frac{V_t}{4 \beta} \dot{P}_L$$

3. **Laplace transform of continuity:**

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s X(s) + C_{tp} P_L(s) + \frac{V_t s}{4 \beta} P_L(s)$$

4. **Substitute $P_L(s)$ from Step 1 into Step 3:**

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s X(s) + C_{tp} \frac{M s^{2} X(s)}{A_p} + \frac{V_t s}{4 \beta} \cdot \frac{M s^{2} X(s)}{A_p}$$

5. **Factor $X(s)$ and group:**

$$Q_L(s) = X(s) \left[ A_p s + \frac{C_{tp} M s^{2}}{A_p} + \frac{M V_t s^{3}}{4 \beta A_p} \right]$$

6. **Solve for $X(s)/Q_L(s)$:**

$$\frac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \frac{A_p}{s \left( \frac{M V_t s^{2}}{4 \beta} + C_{tp} M s + A_p^{2} \right)}$$

7. **Normalize to standard form:**

$$\frac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \frac{4 \beta A_p / (M V_t)}{s \left( s^{2} + \dfrac{4 \beta C_{tp}}{V_t} s + \dfrac{4 \beta A_p^{2}}{M V_t} \right)} = \frac{K_v}{s(s^{2} + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^{2})}$$

8. **Natural frequency and damping ratio:**

$$\omega_n = A_p \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta}{M V_t}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{C_{tp}}{2 A_p} \sqrt{\frac{\beta M}{V_t}}$$

**(b) [7 Marks] A hydraulic cylinder with $A_p = 0.01 \, \text{m}^{2}$, $V_t = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}$, $M = 50 \, \text{kg}$, $\beta = 1.5 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$, and $C_{tp} = 0$ drives a pure inertial load. Compute the natural frequency $\omega_n$ in rad/s and Hz.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Substitute values into the natural frequency formula:

$$\omega_n = A_p \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta}{M V_t}} = 0.01 \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9}}{50 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

2. Compute the numerator and denominator inside the square root:

$$\frac{4 \beta}{M V_t} = \frac{6 \times 10^{9}}{0.01} = 6 \times 10^{11}$$

3. Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{6 \times 10^{11}} \approx 7.746 \times 10^{5}$$

4. Multiply by $A_p = 0.01$:

$$\omega_n = 0.01 \times 7.746 \times 10^{5} = 7746 \, \text{rad/s}$$

5. Convert to Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2 \pi} = \frac{7746}{6.283} \approx 1233 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $\omega_n \approx 7746 \, \text{rad/s}$, $\;f_n \approx 1233 \, \text{Hz}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/s$ integrator** in the hydraulic actuator transfer function. The piston position is the integral of velocity — losing the $1/s$ costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_{tp}$ (leakage) with $C_f$ (capacitance).** These look similar but have different units and different positions in the equation.
> 3. **Skipping the Laplace step.** KTU examiners award marks for explicitly writing "taking Laplace transform with zero initial conditions" before substituting.
> 4. **Sign errors in the flow continuity equation.** Always: $\text{Flow in} = \text{Piston flow} + \text{Leakage} + \text{Compression}$. The compression term is $\dfrac{V_t}{4\beta}\dot{P}_L$, not $\dfrac{V_t}{\beta}\dot{P}_L$.
> 5. **Confusing $V_t$ (trapped volume) with $V_0$ (tank volume).** In actuator derivations, $V_t$ is the total dead volume including piping.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fluid System Model (Module 2)**

- [x] **Three primitives:** $R_f$ (dissipative), $C_f$ (potential storage), $I_f$ (kinetic storage). Know the formula for each in laminar flow.
- [x] **Hydraulic–Electrical analogy:** $P \leftrightarrow V$, $Q \leftrightarrow i$, $V_{ol} \leftrightarrow q$, $R_f \leftrightarrow R$, $C_f \leftrightarrow C$, $I_f \leftrightarrow L$.
- [x] **Level system TF:** $H(s)/Q_i(s) = R_f/(A R_f s + 1)$, time constant $\tau = A R_f$, first-order stable system.
- [x] **Pressure system TF:** Identical structure with $C_f = V_0/\beta$ substituting for $A/(\rho g)$.
- [x] **Hydraulic actuator TF:** Third-order with one pole at origin — $\dfrac{X(s)}{Q_L(s)} = \dfrac{K_v}{s(s^{2} + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^{2})}$.
- [x] **Natural frequency formula:** $\omega_n = A_p \sqrt{4\beta/(M V_t)}$ — bulk modulus $\beta$ makes hydraulics **stiff**, hence high $\omega_n$.
- [x] **Damping ratio formula:** $\zeta = \dfrac{C_{tp}}{2 A_p}\sqrt{\beta M / V_t}$ — leakage is the **dominant** damping source in most practical cylinders.
- [x] **Two key ODEs to memorize:** (i) $A \dot{h} = Q_i - Q_o$, (ii) $Q_L = A_p \dot{x} + C_{tp} P_L + \dfrac{V_t}{4\beta}\dot{P}_L$.
- [x] **Steady-state shortcuts:** At $s = 0$, capacitors open and inductors short — so $P_{ss} = R_f Q_i$, $H_{ss} = R_f Q_i$.
- [x] **Units mastery:** $R_f$ in $\text{N·s/m}^5$, $C_f$ in $\text{m}^5/\text{N}$, $I_f$ in $\text{N·s}^2/\text{m}^5$ — examiners love to test dimensional consistency.
- [x] **Common values:** Hydraulic oil $\beta \approx 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa, water $\beta \approx 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa, air $\beta \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa.
- [x] **Assumption to state explicitly:** Incompressible, Newtonian, laminar flow, lumped parameters, linearization about operating point.
- [x] **KTU-favorite trick question:** "Why is a hydraulic actuator faster than a pneumatic one?" — Answer: air's low bulk modulus gives low $\omega_n$, so pneumatic systems are slow and oscillatory.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "P-Q-C-L":**
> **P**ressure (effort) — like **V**oltage
> **Q** flow (flow) — like **C**urrent (or **I**)
> **C**apacitance stores **V**olume under pressure
> **L**iquid inertia = **I**nertance = $\rho L / A$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microprocessor (μP):** A microprocessor is a single-chip, multipurpose, programmable, clock-driven, register-based, binary-compatible central processing unit (CPU) fabricated using Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI) technology on a single integrated circuit (IC) that accepts binary data as input, processes it according to instructions stored in external memory, and produces results through external peripheral devices.

In the context of the **PCPET502 – Sensors and Mechatronic Systems** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), the microprocessor (typically the **8-bit Intel 8085** or the **16-bit Intel 8086**) is introduced as the **central computational brain** that interfaces with sensors (input), actuators (output), and the memory subsystem to form a complete mechatronic embedded system.

**Architectural classification accepted by KTU examiners:**

| Classification | Description |
|----------------|-------------|
| Von Neumann Architecture | Common memory and data bus; instructions and data share the same pathway |
| Harvard Architecture | Separate program and data memory with independent buses |

The **8085** uses a **modified Von Neumann** architecture with multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7 lower byte, A8–A15 upper byte).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: "The Human Brain of a Machine"**

Imagine a fully automatic washing machine. It must:
1. **Read** the weight of clothes (sensor input),
2. **Decide** wash time and water level (logic),
3. **Control** the motor and water valve (actuator output).

A microprocessor performs exactly this — but with **electrical signals** instead of human senses. It is like a **post-office clerk**:
- The **desk (registers)** holds letters being worked on,
- The **rule book (memory)** tells the clerk what to do,
- The **in-tray (input port)** brings new work,
- The **out-tray (output port)** sends completed work,
- The **stamp tool (ALU)** actually processes (adds/subtracts/compares) the work.

The microprocessor is the **master controller** of every modern mechatronic device — from a digital thermometer to a CNC robotic arm.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants and Metrics

The following **standard engineering constants and metrics** are vital for KTU problems:

> - **Crystal Frequency ($f_{xtal}$)** of 8085: **6.144 MHz** (default) or **3 MHz**
> - **Internal Operating Frequency ($f_{int}$)** = $f_{xtal} / 2$ → typically **3.072 MHz** or **1.5 MHz**
> - **T-State Duration ($T$)** = $1 / f_{int}$
> - **Address Bus Width** = **16 bits** → directly addresses $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$ memory locations
> - **Data Bus Width** = **8 bits**
> - **Word Size** = **8 bits (1 byte)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture of the 8085 Microprocessor

The 8085 CPU is divided into **three functional units**:

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs arithmetic: **ADD, SUB, INR, DCR, ADD with carry, subtract with borrow**
- Performs logical: **AND, OR, XOR, NOT, Compare, Rotate**
- Operates on **8-bit data** held temporarily in the **Accumulator (Register A)**.
- Sets **five flag bits** in the **Flag Register (F)**:
  - **S** (Sign), **Z** (Zero), **AC** (Auxiliary Carry), **P** (Parity), **CY** (Carry)

### 2.1.2 Register Array (6 Programmable + 2 Temporary Registers)

| Register | Size | Function |
|----------|------|----------|
| A (Accumulator) | 8-bit | Primary ALU operand; result destination |
| F (Flag) | 8-bit | Holds 5 status flags + 3 unused bits |
| B, C | 8-bit | General-purpose; can pair as **BC** (16-bit) |
| D, E | 8-bit | General-purpose; can pair as **DE** (16-bit) |
| H, L | 8-bit | General-purpose; can pair as **HL** (16-bit) — used as **memory pointer** |
| PC (Program Counter) | 16-bit | Holds address of next instruction to fetch |
| SP (Stack Pointer) | 16-bit | Points to top of stack in RAM |

### 2.1.3 Timing and Control Unit
- Synchronizes all internal and external operations with the **system clock**.
- Generates **control signals**: $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $IO/\overline{M}$, $S_1$, $S_0$, $\overline{ALE}$.

## 2.2 Bus System — The Three Highway Structure

A **bus** is a set of common conducting paths carrying similar information.

| Bus Type | Width (8085) | Direction | Function |
|----------|--------------|-----------|----------|
| **Address Bus** | 16-bit (A0–A15) | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | Identifies memory/I/O location |
| **Data Bus** | 8-bit (D0–D7) | Bidirectional | Carries actual data/operand |
| **Control Bus** | Variable | Bidirectional | Synchronizes read/write, interrupts, DMA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demultiplexing Note (KTU Hot Topic):** The lower address byte (AD0–AD7) and data share the same 8 lines. The **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** signal latches the address into an external **74LS373 / 74HC573 latch** so data can later be placed on the same lines.

## 2.3 Instruction Cycle, Machine Cycle, and T-State

The execution of any instruction is hierarchically decomposed:

1. **T-State** — The smallest unit of time = one clock pulse (1 clock period).
2. **Machine Cycle (MC)** — The time to perform one memory or I/O access = 3 to 6 T-states.
3. **Instruction Cycle (IC)** — The total time to fetch, decode, and execute one instruction = 1 to 6 machine cycles.

### 2.3.1 Seven Types of Machine Cycles in 8085

| Machine Cycle | Status $IO/\overline{M}$ | Status $S_1$ | Status $S_0$ |
|---------------|--------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| Opcode Fetch (OF) | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| Memory Read (MR) | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| Memory Write (MW) | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| I/O Read (IOR) | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| I/O Write (IOW) | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| Interrupt Acknowledge (INA) | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Bus Idle (BI) | 0/1 | 0 | 0 |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Box — Keep this table on a single laminated sheet for revision.**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|-------|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Addressable memory | $M = 2^n$ where $n$ = address bus width | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes (64 KB) for 8085 |
| 2 | Internal clock frequency | $f_{int} = f_{xtal} / 2$ | **1.5 MHz** (for 3 MHz crystal) |
| 3 | T-state duration | $T = 1 / f_{int}$ | $0.667 \ \mu s$ |
| 4 | Time per machine cycle | $T_{MC} = N \times T$ where $N$ = number of T-states in MC | depends on MC type |
| 5 | Total instruction time | $T_{instr} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{MC_i}$ | $k$ = number of MCs in instruction |
| 6 | Stack Pointer movement (PUSH) | $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ | decremented by 2 bytes |
| 7 | Stack Pointer movement (POP) | $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ | incremented by 2 bytes |
| 8 | Memory address from HL | $M_{addr} = (H \times 256) + L$ | 16-bit linear address |
| 9 | Decoded address for chip select | $\overline{CS} = f(A_{15}, A_{14}, \ldots)$ | using 3-to-8 decoder (74LS138) |
| 10 | Absolute branching displacement | $TA = PC + 2 + \text{displacement}$ | signed 8-bit, $\vert d \vert \le 127$ |

> **Engineering utility:** In a CNC machine or autonomous robot (mechatronic systems), the microprocessor's real-time instruction cycle timing dictates **sampling rate**, **control loop bandwidth**, and **actuator response latency**. Choosing a microprocessor with the right $f_{int}$ is critical in selection of mechatronic controllers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Address Calculation from HL Register Pair

In the 8085 microprocessor, the **HL pair** acts as a memory pointer. The 16-bit address is formed by concatenating the contents of H (high byte) and L (low byte). Mathematically:

$$A_{mem} = (H)_{hex} \cdot 16^2 + (L)_{hex} \cdot 16^0$$

We can equivalently write this in decimal form:

$$A_{mem} = (H)_{dec} \times 256 + (L)_{dec}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** After execution of certain instructions, $H = 3A_{H}$ and $L = F5_{H}$. Find the effective memory address pointed to by HL.

**Step 1:** Convert hexadecimal to decimal for clarity.

The 8-bit hex value $3A_{H}$ converts as:

$$3A_{H} = 3 \times 16 + 10 = 48 + 10 = 58_{10}$$

Similarly, the 8-bit hex value $F5_{H}$ converts as:

$$F5_{H} = 15 \times 16 + 5 = 240 + 5 = 245_{10}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the linear address formula.

$$A_{mem} = 58 \times 256 + 245$$

Compute the first term:

$$58 \times 256 = 58 \times 250 + 58 \times 6 = 14{,}500 + 348 = 14{,}848$$

Now add the offset:

$$A_{mem} = 14{,}848 + 245 = 15{,}093_{10}$$

**Step 3:** Convert back to hexadecimal for verification.

Divide 15,093 by 16 repeatedly:

$$15{,}093 \div 16 = 943 \text{ remainder } 5$$

$$943 \div 16 = 58 \text{ remainder } 15 \; (F)$$

$$58 \div 16 = 3 \text{ remainder } 10 \; (A)$$

$$3 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 3$$

Reading remainders in reverse: $3AF5_{H}$, which matches HL concatenation. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Instruction Execution Time Calculation

A standard KTU numerical problem: *Compute the time to execute a given instruction sequence.*

**Given:**
- Crystal frequency $f_{xtal} = 3 \text{ MHz}$
- Instruction sequence contains:
  - $5$ instructions of type **MVI r, data** (Opcode Fetch = 4T, Memory Read = 3T) → total $7T$ each
  - $3$ instructions of type **ADD r** (Opcode Fetch = 4T) → total $4T$ each
  - $1$ instruction of type **STA 16-bit** (OF=4T, MR=3T, MR=3T, MW=3T) → total $13T$

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Compute the internal clock frequency.

$$f_{int} = \frac{f_{xtal}}{2} = \frac{3 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 1.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2:** Compute T-state duration.

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{int}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.667 \ \mu s$$

**Step 3:** Total T-states for the entire program.

$$T_{total} = 5 \times 7T + 3 \times 4T + 1 \times 13T$$

$$T_{total} = 35T + 12T + 13T = 60T$$

**Step 4:** Total execution time in seconds.

$$t_{exec} = 60 \times 0.667 \ \mu s = 40.0 \ \mu s$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Absolute Branch Target Address for JUMP

For conditional and unconditional jumps, the 8085 uses a **signed 8-bit relative displacement**. The target address is computed relative to the program counter.

$$TA = PC_{current} + 2 + \text{displacement}_{signed}$$

### Worked Example
**Given:** $PC = 2050_{H}$, the instruction is **JMP** with the displacement byte $= FB_{H}$ (which is signed $-5$ in two's complement).

**Step 1:** Convert signed byte to decimal.

$$FB_{H} = 1111 \, 1011_{b} = -5_{10} \text{ (two's complement of } 5)$$

**Step 2:** Apply target address formula.

$$TA = 2050_{H} + 2 + (-5)$$

$$TA = 2052_{H} - 5 = 204D_{H}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — T-States Per Machine Cycle (8085 Standard)

| Machine Cycle | T-States | Activity |
|---------------|----------|----------|
| Opcode Fetch (OF) | **4T** | $PC$ placed on address bus, opcode read from memory, PC incremented |
| Memory Read (MR) | **3T** | Address placed on bus, data loaded into register |
| Memory Write (MW) | **3T** | Address placed on bus, register contents written to memory |
| I/O Read (IOR) | **3T** | 8-bit port address placed, data input to accumulator |
| I/O Write (IOW) | **3T** | 8-bit port address placed, accumulator written to port |
| Interrupt Acknowledge (INA) | **6T** | INTA signal sent, vector address read from peripheral |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Push Operation Decomposition:**
> 1. PC is decremented: $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ (1T wait)
> 2. Machine cycle 1: $MW$ of High byte (3T)
> 3. $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$
> 4. Machine cycle 2: $MW$ of Low byte (3T)
> Total = **3T + 3T + wait states = 12T** for PUSH RP instruction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of 8085 Microprocessor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["ACCUMULATOR A 8 BIT"]
    nodeB["TEMP REGISTERS"]
    nodeC["ALU 8 BIT"]
    nodeD["FLAG REGISTER F"]
    nodeE["INSTRUCTION REGISTER"]
    nodeF["INSTRUCTION DECODER"]
    nodeG["TIMING AND CONTROL UNIT"]
    nodeH["REGISTER ARRAY B C D E H L"]
    nodeI["PROGRAM COUNTER PC 16 BIT"]
    nodeJ["STACK POINTER SP 16 BIT"]
    nodeK["ADDRESS BUFFER 8 BIT A8 A15"]
    nodeL["ADDRESS DATA BUFFER 8 BIT AD0 AD7"]
    nodeM["INCREMENTER DECREMENTER"]
    nodeN["SERIAL I/O CONTROL"]
    nodeO["INTERRUPT CONTROL"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeO
    nodeG --> nodeN
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeO
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Microprocessor in a Mechatronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["PHYSICAL SENSOR TEMPERATURE"]
    nodeS2["PHYSICAL SENSOR PRESSURE"]
    nodeS3["PHYSICAL SENSOR POSITION"]
    nodeSC["SIGNAL CONDITIONING AMPLIFIER FILTER"]
    nodeADC["ADC 0808 8 BIT"]
    nodeLATCH["74LS373 ADDRESS LATCH"]
    nodeCPU["MICROPROCESSOR 8085 CPU"]
    nodeMEMP["ROM PROGRAM MEMORY"]
    nodeMEMD["RAM DATA MEMORY"]
    nodeDEC["74LS138 ADDRESS DECODER"]
    nodeDAC["DAC 0808 OUTPUT"]
    nodeAMP["POWER AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeACT1["ACTUATOR DC MOTOR"]
    nodeACT2["ACTUATOR RELAY COIL"]
    nodeACT3["ACTUATOR SOLENOID"]
    nodeHMI["HMI DISPLAY KEYBOARD"]

    nodeS1 --> nodeSC
    nodeS2 --> nodeSC
    nodeS3 --> nodeSC
    nodeSC --> nodeADC
    nodeADC --> nodeCPU
    nodeCPU --> nodeLATCH
    nodeCPU --> nodeMEMP
    nodeCPU --> nodeMEMD
    nodeCPU --> nodeDEC
    nodeDEC --> nodeMEMP
    nodeDEC --> nodeMEMD
    nodeDEC --> nodeADC
    nodeCPU --> nodeDAC
    nodeDAC --> nodeAMP
    nodeAMP --> nodeACT1
    nodeAMP --> nodeACT2
    nodeAMP --> nodeACT3
    nodeCPU --> nodeHMI
```

> **Interpretation:** The microprocessor sits at the **central decision node**. Sensors feed analog signals through signal conditioning and an ADC into the CPU. The CPU runs the control algorithm stored in ROM and produces actuator commands via a DAC and power amplifier. The 74LS138 decoder uniquely selects memory and I/O chips based on the high-order address bits.

## 4.3 Instruction Execution Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF1["START FETCH T1 PC ADDR ON BUS"]
    nodeF2["T2 READ OPCODE FROM MEMORY"]
    nodeF3["T3 STORE IN INSTRUCTION REGISTER"]
    nodeF4["T4 DECODE OPCODE INCREMENT PC"]
    nodeD1["DECODE BY INSTRUCTION DECODER"]
    nodeE1["EXECUTE FETCH OPERANDS IF ANY"]
    nodeE2["PERFORM OPERATION IN ALU"]
    nodeE3["STORE RESULT IN DESTINATION"]
    nodeE4["UPDATE FLAGS"]
    nodeEND["END READY FOR NEXT INSTRUCTION"]

    nodeF1 --> nodeF2
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4
    nodeF4 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3
    nodeE3 --> nodeE4
    nodeE4 --> nodeEND
```

## 4.4 Pin Configuration Summary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX1["X1 X2 CRYSTAL INPUT 6MHZ"]
    nodeX2["CLK OUT CLOCK OUTPUT 3MHZ"]
    nodeX3["RESET IN PIN 36"]
    nodeX4["RESET OUT PIN 3"]
    nodeX5["ALE PIN 30 ADDRESS LATCH ENABLE"]
    nodeX6["S1 S0 PIN 29 33 STATUS SIGNALS"]
    nodeX7["RD WR PIN 32 31 READ WRITE CONTROL"]
    nodeX8["IO OVERBAR M PIN 34"]
    nodeX9["HOLD HLDA PIN 39 38 DMA CONTROL"]
    nodeX10["READY PIN 35 SLOW MEMORY"]
    nodeX11["SID SOD PIN 4 5 SERIAL I/O"]
    nodeX12["TRAP RST7 5 RST6 5 INTR PIN 6 7 8 9 10 11"]
    nodeX13["A15 A8 PIN 21 28 UPPER ADDRESS"]
    nodeX14["AD7 AD0 PIN 12 19 MULTIPLEXED ADDRESS DATA"]
    nodeX15["VCC VSS PIN 40 20 POWER GROUND"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** T-states, machine cycles, and instruction cycles — they are three different hierarchical time units.
> - **Always divide** the crystal frequency by 2 to get the internal operating frequency of the 8085.
> - **PC is incremented during opcode fetch, not during operand fetch** — KTU examiners frequently test this.
> - **Signed relative jumps:** displacement is signed; remember to add +2 to PC (size of JMP instruction in bytes).
> - **Multiplexed bus:** Lower address and data share pins AD0–AD7 — forgetting this loses 2–3 marks.
> - **Memory map vs I/O map:** Memory-mapped I/O uses the full 16-bit address; I/O-mapped I/O uses only 8-bit port address with $IO/\overline{M} = 1$.

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architecture. Which one does the 8085 microprocessor use?**

**Model Answer (Key Points for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Memory | Single shared memory for program + data | Separate program and data memory |
| Bus | Common address/data bus | Independent address/data bus |
| Speed | Slower (bus contention) | Faster (parallel access) |
| Example | 8085, 8086 | 8051, DSP, RISC processors |

**Conclusion:** The 8085 uses a **modified Von Neumann** architecture because the program and data memories are physically separate externally, but they share a common multiplexed address/data bus internally via pins AD0–AD7. **(1 mark)** — exact architectural identity must be stated.

---

### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model]**
**Explain the function of the Program Counter (PC) and Stack Pointer (SP) in the 8085 microprocessor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Program Counter (PC):** A 16-bit register that holds the memory address of the **next instruction** to be fetched. It is automatically incremented by one during each opcode fetch. It determines the **sequential flow** of program execution. **(1½ marks)**
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** A 16-bit register that always points to the **top of the stack** in RAM. It is **decremented before PUSH** and **incremented after POP**. The stack is used for subroutine calls (CALL/RET), interrupts, and temporary data storage. **(1½ marks)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Module 2 Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of the 8085 microprocessor and explain the functions of the ALU, Register Array, and Timing & Control Unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the **Opcode Fetch machine cycle** of the 8085 microprocessor, clearly indicating the role of $\overline{RD}$, $IO/\overline{M}$, $S_1$, $S_0$, and $ALE$ signals. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- The 8085 is an **8-bit microprocessor** packaged in a **40-pin DIP** (Dual Inline Package) and operates on a single **+5 V** power supply. **(1 mark)**
- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs all arithmetic (ADD, SUB, INR, DCR, ADC, SBB) and logical (AND, OR, XOR, CMP, rotate) operations on 8-bit data. Operands come from the Accumulator and a specified register/memory. Sets the **5 flag bits** (S, Z, AC, P, CY) in the Flag Register. **(2 marks)**
- **Register Array:** Contains six 8-bit general-purpose registers (B, C, D, E, H, L) that can pair as BC, DE, HL (16-bit) for indirect addressing and memory pointer operations. Includes the **Accumulator (A)** and **Flag Register (F)**, plus two critical 16-bit special registers: **Program Counter (PC)** and **Stack Pointer (SP)**. **(2 marks)**
- **Timing and Control Unit:** Decodes instructions, generates all internal/external control signals ($\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $ALE$, $IO/\overline{M}$), synchronizes data flow, and handles interrupts (TRAP, RST 7.5, 6.5, 5.5, INTR). **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The Opcode Fetch (OF) machine cycle consists of **4 T-states**:

- **T1:** Contents of PC placed on the address bus (A15–A8 on A8–A15 pins; lower byte on AD0–AD7). $\overline{ALE} = 1$ (HIGH) so external latch (74LS373) captures the lower address byte. The status signals $IO/\overline{M} = 0$, $S_1 = 1$, $S_0 = 1$ indicate a memory read operation. **(2 marks)**
- **T2:** The $\overline{RD}$ signal goes LOW. Memory places the opcode byte on the data bus. PC is incremented internally during the T1–T2 transition. **(1½ marks)**
- **T3:** Data from the data bus is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**. $\overline{RD}$ returns HIGH. **(1½ marks)**
- **T4:** The opcode in IR is **decoded** by the instruction decoder, and the CPU prepares for the next machine cycle (operand fetch, if any). **(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **memory interfacing** with the 8085. Design a memory map with **16 KB ROM** and **16 KB RAM** using 8 KB memory chips, and explain the role of the **74LS138 decoder**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an 8085 system with a **3 MHz crystal**, calculate the time required to execute the following instruction sequence: **(7 marks)**

- 10 instructions of type **MVI r, data** (4T + 3T = 7T each)
- 5 instructions of type **ADD r** (4T each)
- 1 instruction of **HLT** (5T)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Memory Interfacing:** The 8085 has a **16-bit address bus**, hence can address **64 KB** of memory. Since 8085 has no built-in memory, external ROM (for program) and RAM (for data/stack) must be connected. **(1 mark)**
- **Memory Map Design:** With 8 KB chips, each chip needs **13 address lines** ($2^{13} = 8192 = 8 \text{ KB}$). ROM occupies the lower 16 KB range and RAM the next 16 KB. The remaining address lines ($A_{13}, A_{14}, A_{15}$) feed the **74LS138 3-to-8 decoder** to generate unique $\overline{CS}$ signals for each chip. **(3 marks)**

| Address Range | Chip Selected | Memory Type |
|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| $0000_{H} - 1FFF_{H}$ | ROM1 (8 KB) | Program memory |
| $2000_{H} - 3FFF_{H}$ | ROM2 (8 KB) | Program memory |
| $4000_{H} - 5FFF_{H}$ | RAM1 (8 KB) | Data memory |
| $6000_{H} - 7FFF_{H}$ | RAM2 (8 KB) | Data memory |

- **74LS138 Decoder Role:** Produces **8 active-LOW chip select outputs** $\overline{Y_0}$ through $\overline{Y_7}$ from $A_{13}, A_{14}, A_{15}$ inputs. The $\overline{CS}$ input of each memory chip is tied to one of these outputs, ensuring **no address conflict** and clean selection. **(3 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Internal clock frequency.**

$$f_{int} = \frac{3 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 1.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: T-state duration.**

$$T = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.667 \ \mu s$$

**Step 3: Total T-states for the program.**

$$T_{total} = (10 \times 7) + (5 \times 4) + (1 \times 5)$$

$$T_{total} = 70 + 20 + 5 = 95T$$

**Step 4: Total execution time.**

$$t_{exec} = 95 \times 0.667 \ \mu s \approx 63.33 \ \mu s$$

Therefore, the program completes in approximately **63.33 microseconds**. **(7 marks awarded for complete derivation)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Microprocessor**

- A **microprocessor** is a single-chip CPU — the brain of any mechatronic system.
- The **8085** is an **8-bit, 40-pin, +5 V, NMOS** microprocessor with a **16-bit address bus** and **8-bit data bus**.
- **Address bus** is unidirectional (CPU → outside), **data bus** is bidirectional, **control bus** carries timing and synchronization signals.
- **Multiplexed bus** concept: $AD_0 - AD_7$ pins carry both lower address and data; an external **74LS373 latch** demultiplexes them using $\overline{ALE}$.
- The **internal clock** of the 8085 is **half** the external crystal frequency.
- **Instruction Cycle** = sum of all **Machine Cycles** = sum of all **T-States**.
- An **Opcode Fetch** machine cycle always takes **4 T-states**.
- **Register pair HL** is the most-used 16-bit memory pointer.
- The **PC** points to the **next** instruction; the **SP** points to the **top of stack**.
- **PUSH** decrements SP first; **POP** increments SP after reading.
- **Memory interfacing** requires the **74LS138 3-to-8 decoder** for unique chip select signals.
- The 8085 supports **5 interrupt sources**: TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and INTR (vectored via INTA machine cycle).
- In mechatronics, the microprocessor interfaces with **sensors (via ADC)** on the input side and **actuators (via DAC + power amplifier)** on the output side.
- The 8085 is now mostly replaced by **microcontrollers (8051, PIC, AVR, ARM)** in modern embedded mechatronic designs, but the architectural fundamentals remain the same.

> **Final Tip:** KTU examiners expect students to **always draw a labeled diagram** and **show the derivation of $f_{int}$** before computing instruction timing — even if the question is worth only 7 marks. Skipping the diagram or the $f_{xtal}/2$ step costs **1–2 marks** consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microcontroller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** is a single-chip, highly integrated microcomputer fabricated using Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) technology, containing a Central Processing Unit (CPU), program memory (ROM/Flash), data memory (RAM), input/output (I/O) ports, timers/counters, serial communication interfaces (UART/SPI/I²C), and interrupt handling logic — all embedded on a single Integrated Circuit (IC) substrate. Unlike a general-purpose microprocessor, which requires external peripheral chips to function, a microcontroller is a *self-contained computing engine* designed specifically for real-time, embedded control applications in mechatronic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> A microcontroller is a "computer-on-a-chip" optimized for **embedded control**, where deterministic response to physical-world stimuli (sensor inputs → actuator outputs) is the central design philosophy.

### Microcontroller vs. Microprocessor — The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Function** | Pure computation engine | Computation + control |
| **Memory** | External (RAM, ROM) | Internal (on-chip) |
| **Peripherals** | External chips required | Integrated (Timers, ADC, I/O) |
| **Cost** | Higher (multi-chip system) | Lower (single-chip system) |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low |
| **Application Domain** | PCs, laptops, servers | Embedded systems, sensors, mechatronics |
| **Example** | Intel Core i7, AMD Ryzen | Intel 8051, Atmel ATmega328, PIC16F877 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — The "Smart Pocket Watch" Analogy**

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife**. A microprocessor is like a single, very sharp, professional-grade chef's knife — outstanding at one task (pure computation) but useless without a cutting board, a stove, and pans. A microcontroller, on the other hand, is the **Swiss Army Knife** itself: it has the blade (CPU), the screwdriver (I/O pins), the bottle opener (communication ports), the magnifying glass (ADC), the toothpick (timers), and the corkscrew (interrupt logic) — all inside one compact handle. In a mechatronic system, you don't need a "chef's knife"; you need a multi-purpose, reliable, low-power tool that lives inside your washing machine, drone, or anti-lock braking system for years. That tool is the **microcontroller**.

### The Three Pillars of MCU Design

1. **Integration** — Everything on one die (single IC package).
2. **Determinism** — Hard real-time response to external events.
3. **Autonomy** — Stand-alone operation with minimal external support components (crystal, decoupling caps, power).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Clock Frequency ($f_{CLK}$)**: Typically **1 MHz to 200 MHz** (low-power MCUs); 8051 operates at **12 MHz** (1 µs machine cycle).
- **Machine Cycle**: $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{CLK}}$ for the classic 8051.
- **Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$)**: **+5V DC** (standard 8051); modern MCUs use **1.8V – 3.3V**.
- **Word Size**: 8-bit (8051, ATmega), 16-bit (MSP430), 32-bit (ARM Cortex-M).
- **Power Dissipation ($P_d$)**: Typically **10 mW – 500 mW** for low-power MCUs.

> [!NOTE]
> **CISC vs. RISC Architecture Choice:**
> The **8051** is a **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** with 255 opcodes. The **AVR (ATmega)** and **ARM Cortex** are **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** with streamlined, single-cycle instructions. KTU frequently asks comparative questions on this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Generic Microcontroller

A microcontroller is architecturally composed of **seven functional blocks** operating in synchrony via internal data, address, and control buses.

### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)
