- Contains the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** and the **Control Unit (CU)**.
- Performs fetch-decode-execute cycles on opcodes fetched from program memory.
- In the **8051**, the ALU is 8-bit wide; in **ARM Cortex-M0**, it is 32-bit wide.

### 2.1.2 Program Memory (Code ROM/Flash)
- Stores the user firmware as machine-readable binary.
- **Non-volatile** — retains instructions even when power is removed.
- 8051: 4 KB internal ROM (expandable to 64 KB externally).

### 2.1.3 Data Memory (RAM)
- Two regions:
  * **Internal RAM** (128 bytes in 8051, includes SFRs and bit-addressable zone).
  * **External RAM** (up to 64 KB addressable via 16-bit DPTR).

### 2.1.4 I/O Ports
- Bidirectional or quasi-bidirectional digital interfaces to the external world.
- 8051 has **four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** — 32 I/O lines total.

### 2.1.5 Timers/Counters
- Hardware counters driven either by the oscillator (timer mode) or by external pulses (counter mode).
- 8051 contains **two 16-bit timers/counters (T0, T1)**.

### 2.1.6 Serial Communication Interface
- **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** block for asynchronous serial communication (RS-232, RS-485).
- 8051 has one full-duplex UART.

### 2.1.7 Interrupt Controller
- Manages asynchronous events from internal or external sources.
- 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources** (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial).

## 2.2 The 8051 Microcontroller — KTU-Critical Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 8051?**
> The Intel **MCS-51 family (8051)** is the *de facto* academic reference MCU in KTU curricula. It is **Harvard architecture**, **CISC**, and **8-bit**, with a *very* long pedagogical legacy.

### Pin Configuration (40-Pin DIP)

- **Pins 1–8**: Port 1 (P1.0 – P1.7) — General purpose I/O.
- **Pin 9**: $\overline{RST}$ — Reset (active HIGH, requires ≥ 2 machine cycles).
- **Pins 10–17**: Port 3 (P3.0 – P3.7) — Multifunction (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$).
- **Pins 18, 19**: $X_2$, $X_1$ — Crystal oscillator connections.
- **Pin 20**: $\overline{EA}/V_{PP}$ — External Access / Programming voltage.
- **Pin 21–28**: Port 2 (P2.0 – P2.7) — High-byte address bus.
- **Pin 29**: $\overline{PSEN}$ — Program Store Enable.
- **Pin 30**: ALE — Address Latch Enable.
- **Pin 31**: $\overline{EA}$ — External Access.
- **Pin 32–39**: Port 0 (P0.0 – P0.7) — Multiplexed low-byte address/data bus.
- **Pin 40**: $V_{CC}$ — +5V supply.

### Register Architecture (SFR — Special Function Registers)

The 8051 has **128 bytes of internal RAM** (00H–7FH) and **128 bytes of SFR space** (80H–FFH). Critical SFRs include:

- **Accumulator (A)** — 0xE0 — Primary arithmetic register.
- **B Register** — 0xF0 — Used in MUL/AB and DIV/AB.
- **PSW (Program Status Word)** — 0xD0 — Contains Carry (C), Auxiliary Carry (AC), Flag (F0), Register Select (RS0, RS1), Overflow (OV), and Parity (P) bits.
- **DPTR (DPH:DPL)** — 16-bit Data Pointer — 0x83/0x82.
- **SP (Stack Pointer)** — 8-bit — 0x81, initialized to **0x07** on reset.
- **PC (Program Counter)** — 16-bit — Not directly accessible as an SFR.
- **TMOD, TCON** — Timer Mode and Timer Control registers.
- **SCON, SBUF** — Serial Control and Serial Buffer registers.
- **IE, IP** — Interrupt Enable and Interrupt Priority registers.

### Banked Register Set (R0–R7)

Four register banks (Bank 0–3), each containing 8 registers (R0–R7). Active bank selected by RS0/RS1 bits of PSW.

| RS1 | RS0 | Selected Bank | RAM Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

> [!NOTE]
> On reset, **Bank 0** is selected and **SP = 0x07**. The first push places data at 0x08 (Bank 1), preserving R0–R7 of Bank 0.

## 2.3 Harvard vs. Von Neumann Architecture

| Feature | Harvard | Von Neumann |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Buses** | Separate code + data | Unified single bus |
| **Simultaneous Fetch** | Yes (instruction + data) | No (bus contention) |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower (von Neumann bottleneck) |
| **Example** | 8051, AVR, PIC | 8086, ARM7 (some modes) |
| **Security** | Higher (code space isolated) | Lower |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE problems.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Time (8051) | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Instruction Cycle (1-byte, 1-cycle) | $T_{inst} = T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Instruction Cycle (2-byte, 2-cycle) | $T_{inst} = 2 \cdot T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| 4 | Timer Reload (Mode 1, 16-bit) | $TH = \dfrac{2^{16} - N}{256}$, $TL = (2^{16}-N) \mod 256$ | hex |
| 5 | Timer Frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Timer Tick Period | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}} = 1 \, \mu s$ (at 12 MHz) | seconds |
| 7 | Baud Rate Generator (Timer 1, Mode 2) | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \cdot 256 - TH1}$ | bps |
| 8 | Maximum External Memory (8051) | $64\,KB$ code + $64\,KB$ data | bytes |
| 9 | Power Dissipation (CMOS, quiescent) | $P_d = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC}$ | Watts |
| 10 | Address Latch (P0) Demultiplex | $\overline{ALE}$ pulses latch 74HC573 | — |

### Important Substitution (Baud Rate)

For a 12 MHz crystal and SMOD = 0, to obtain **9600 baud**:
$$TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud} = 256 - \dfrac{12 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot 9600} \approx 253 = 0xFD$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In mechatronic systems, microcontrollers serve as the **central nervous system**:

- **Automotive**: Engine Control Unit (ECU), Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), airbag deployment.
- **Industrial Automation**: PLC backbones, conveyor sorting, robotic arm controllers.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Washing machines, microwave ovens, smart thermostats.
- **Medical Devices**: Insulin pumps, ECG monitors, ventilators.
- **Aerospace & Drones**: Flight controllers (e.g., Pixhawk — STM32-based), IMU fusion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Timer Reload Value for a Desired Delay

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
The 8051 operates at **11.0592 MHz** with Timer 0 in **Mode 1** (16-bit). Compute the TH0 and TL0 values to generate a delay of exactly **50 ms**.

### Step 1: Determine the Timer Tick Period
The 8051's timer increments once every machine cycle, where one machine cycle is 12 oscillator periods.

$$T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{tick} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

### Step 2: Determine the Required Count Value
A 16-bit timer overflows when it counts from its initial value up to 65536 ($2^{16}$). If the count difference is $N$, the total delay is:

$$T_{delay} = N \cdot T_{tick}$$

Solving for $N$:

$$N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46080$$

### Step 3: Compute the Initial Timer Value
The 16-bit timer must be pre-loaded such that it overflows after $N$ counts:

$$\text{Timer Value} = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - 46080 = 19456$$

### Step 4: Convert 19456 to Hexadecimal
We perform successive division by 16:

$$19456 \div 16 = 1216 \quad \text{remainder } 0$$
$$1216 \div 16 = 76 \quad \text{remainder } 0$$
$$76 \div 16 = 4 \quad \text{remainder } 12 \, (C)$$
$$4 \div 16 = 0 \quad \text{remainder } 4$$

Reading remainders from bottom to top: **0x4C00**

### Step 5: Assign to TH0 and TL0

$$TH0 = 0x4C = 76_{10}, \quad TL0 = 0x00 = 0_{10}$$

### Step 6: Verification
The timer counts from 0x4C00 (= 19456) up to 0xFFFF (= 65535), then overflows to 0x0000 on the next tick. The number of counts is:

$$N = 65536 - 19456 = 46080$$

$$T_{delay} = 46080 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 50.00 \, ms \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Addressable Memory

The 8051 uses a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** to address program memory. The address bus is therefore 16 lines wide.

$$N_{addresses} = 2^{16} = 65536$$

$$M_{code} = 65536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \, KB$$

For external data memory, a **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)** is used similarly:

$$M_{data} = 64 \, KB$$

Hence total addressable memory space = **128 KB**, segregated by control signals:
- $\overline{PSEN}$ — Program Store Enable (code memory read).
- $\overline{RD}, \overline{WR}$ — Data memory read/write.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interrupt Vector Addresses

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Priority (default) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\overline{INT0}$ (External 0) | IE0 | 0x0003 | 1 (highest) |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | 0x000B | 2 |
| $\overline{INT1}$ (External 1) | IE1 | 0x0013 | 3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | 0x001B | 4 |
| Serial Port (RI/TI) | RI/TI | 0x0023 | 5 (lowest) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall**: All interrupt vectors are spaced exactly **8 bytes** apart. If the ISR is longer than 8 bytes, a **LJMP** (long jump) must be placed at the vector address to redirect execution to the actual ISR code located elsewhere in code memory.

## 3.4 Derivation — Power-On Reset Circuit

The 8051 requires $\overline{RST}$ pin held HIGH for at least **2 machine cycles** (≈ 2 µs at 12 MHz) for a valid reset. A typical RC reset circuit:

$$\tau_{reset} = R \cdot C$$

For 10 µF and 8.2 kΩ:

$$\tau = 8200 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.082 \, s = 82 \, ms$$

This easily exceeds the 2 µs minimum, ensuring a robust power-on reset.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step — Execution of a Typical 8051 Instruction (MOV A, #55H)

1. **Fetch** opcode byte at address PC=0x0000 from code memory (using $\overline{PSEN}$).
2. **Decode**: Control unit identifies MOV A, immediate.
3. **Fetch operand** at PC+1 (= 0x0001) — the literal 0x55.
4. **Execute**: Internal data bus transfers 0x55 to accumulator A.
5. **Increment PC** twice (PC ← 0x0002).
6. **Total cycles** = 1 machine cycle (1 µs at 12 MHz).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Microcontroller Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU1["CPU and ALU Block"]
    CTRL1["Control Unit"]
    ROM1["Program Memory ROM or Flash 4KB to 64KB"]
    RAM1["Data Memory RAM 128B to 4KB"]
    PORT1["I/O Ports P0 P1 P2 P3"]
    TIMER1["Timers and Counters T0 T1 T2"]
    UART1["UART Serial Port"]
    INTC1["Interrupt Controller"]
    ADCBUS["Address Bus 16 bit"]
    DBUSBUS["Data Bus 8 bit"]
    CNTRLBUS["Control Bus"]

    CPU1 --- CTRL1
    CPU1 --- ADCBUS
    CPU1 --- DBUSBUS
    CPU1 --- CNTRLBUS
    ADCBUS --- ROM1
    ADCBUS --- RAM1
    DBUSBUS --- ROM1
    DBUSBUS --- RAM1
    ADCBUS --- PORT1
    DBUSBUS --- PORT1
    ADCBUS --- TIMER1
    ADCBUS --- UART1
    ADCBUS --- INTC1
    INTC1 -.-> CPU1
    TIMER1 -.-> INTC1
    UART1 -.-> INTC1
    PORT1 -.-> INTC1
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — 8051 Power-On Reset Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Apply VCC 5V to Pin 40"] --> B0["Crystal Oscillator Starts on X1 X2"]
    B0 --> C0["Internal Reset Logic Activates via RST Pin"]
    C0 --> D0["All SFRs Initialized to Reset Values"]
    D0 --> E0["PC 0x0000 and SP 0x07"]
    E0 --> F0["CPU Begins Fetch Execute at 0x0000"]
    F0 --> G0["Execute User Firmware"]
    G0 --> H0["Normal Operation Mode"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — 8051 Minimal Operating Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC1["+5V Supply VCC Pin 40"]
    GND1["Ground GND Pin 20"]
    CRYSTAL1["Crystal 11.0592 MHz"]
    CAPP1["Capacitor 30 pF"]
    CAPP2["Capacitor 30 pF"]
    RSTBTN1["Reset Button"]
    RESRES1["Resistor 8.2 kOhm"]
    MCUCORE1["8051 MCU Core"]

    VCC1 --- MCUCORE1
    GND1 --- MCUCORE1
    CRYSTAL1 --- CAPP1
    CRYSTAL1 --- CAPP2
    CRYSTAL1 --- MCUCORE1
    RSTBTN1 --- VCC1
    RSTBTN1 --- RESRES1
    RESRES1 --- MCUCORE1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two **30 pF capacitors** form a Pierce oscillator with the crystal. The **8.2 kΩ pull-down resistor** on RST ensures the pin returns to logic LOW after the reset button is released, preventing latched reset state.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Interfacing a Sensor to MCU

```mermaid
graph LR
    PHYSIG["Physical Quantity e.g. Temperature"]
    SENSBLOCK["Sensor LM35"]
    SIGCON1["Signal Conditioner Amplifier Filter"]
    ADCBLOK["ADC 0808 or Internal 10 bit"]
    MCUMAIN["8051 Microcontroller"]
    ACTUAT["Actuator e.g. DC Motor"]
    DRIVCIR["Driver L293D"]
    DISPOUT["LCD 16x2 Display"]

    PHYSIG --> SENSBLOCK
    SENSBLOCK --> SIGCON1
    SIGCON1 --> ADCBLOK
    ADCBLOK --> MCUMAIN
    MCUMAIN --> ACTUAT
    MCUMAIN --> DRIVCIR
    MCUMAIN --> DISPOUT
```

## 4.5 Memory Organization Map — 8051 Internal Data RAM

```mermaid
graph TB
    RAMBLOC["Internal Data RAM 128 Bytes Total"]

    BANKREG0["Bank 0 R0 to R7 Address 0x00 to 0x07"]
    BANKREG1["Bank 1 R0 to R7 Address 0x08 to 0x0F"]
    BANKREG2["Bank 2 R0 to R7 Address 0x10 to 0x17"]
    BANKREG3["Bank 3 R0 to R7 Address 0x18 to 0x1F"]

    BITADDR["Bit Addressable Zone 0x20 to 0x2F 16 bytes equals 128 bits"]
    SCRATCH["Scratchpad General Purpose 0x30 to 0x7F 80 bytes"]

    RAMBLOC --- BANKREG0
    RAMBLOC --- BANKREG1
    RAMBLOC --- BANKREG2
    RAMBLOC --- BANKREG3
    RAMBLOC --- BITADDR
    RAMBLOC --- SCRATCH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **microprocessor** is a CPU-only chip that requires external memory, I/O, and peripheral ICs to function (e.g., Intel 8086). It is designed for high-throughput general-purpose computation in PCs.

A **microcontroller** is a single-chip computer that integrates the CPU, RAM, ROM, timers, and I/O ports (e.g., Intel 8051). It is designed for embedded, real-time control tasks such as sensor data acquisition and actuator driving in mechatronic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** Microcontroller has *all* functional blocks on a single die (System-on-Chip), making it cheaper, smaller, and lower-power for embedded use.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three features of the Harvard architecture used in the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Separate code and data memory spaces** with dedicated address and data buses, allowing simultaneous instruction fetch and operand read.
2. **Faster execution** due to parallel bus access — eliminates the Von Neumann bottleneck.
3. **Higher code security** — the program memory space is physically isolated from the data space, preventing accidental code corruption by misbehaving data writes.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the internal architecture of the 8051 microcontroller. List all major functional blocks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the following 8051 Special Function Registers: **PSW, SP, DPTR, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.)

**Functional Blocks — Description:**

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the **ALU** (8-bit) for arithmetic (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV) and logic (ANL, ORL, XRL) operations, plus the **Control Unit** that fetches, decodes, and executes opcodes.
2. **Program Memory (4 KB on-chip ROM):** Non-volatile storage for the user firmware; addressable up to 64 KB externally.
3. **Data Memory (128 bytes on-chip RAM):** Volatile storage; includes the four register banks (0–3), bit-addressable zone (0x20–0x2F), and general-purpose scratchpad.
4. **Oscillator & Clock Circuit:** Uses external quartz crystal across X1–X2 to generate the system clock.
5. **I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3):** Four 8-bit ports providing 32 I/O lines to interface with external devices.
6. **Timers/Counters (T0, T1):** Two 16-bit programmable timers for delays, event counting, and baud-rate generation.
7. **Serial Port (UART):** Full-duplex asynchronous communication at programmable baud rates.
8. **Interrupt Controller:** Handles 5 interrupt sources with 2 priority levels.

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**PSW (Program Status Word) — 0xD0:** Contains the **Carry (C)**, **Auxiliary Carry (AC)**, **Flag 0 (F0)**, **Register Bank Select bits (RS1, RS0)**, **Overflow (OV)**, and **Parity (P)** flags. The PSW reflects the result of the most recent arithmetic/logic operation.

**SP (Stack Pointer) — 0x81:** 8-bit register pointing to the top of the stack in internal RAM. On reset, SP = 0x07. The stack grows upward (toward higher addresses) and supports PUSH/POP and CALL/RET.

**DPTR (Data Pointer) — 0x82/0x83:** A 16-bit register (composed of DPL = low byte at 0x82 and DPH = high byte at 0x83) used to access external data memory and lookup tables in program memory via MOVC instructions.

**TMOD (Timer Mode) — 0x89:** Configures the operating mode (Mode 0/1/2/3) of Timer 0 and Timer 1, and selects whether each timer is gated by external pin INT.

**SCON (Serial Control) — 0x98:** Configures the serial port mode (8-bit, 9-bit, etc.), enables reception (REN), and contains the RI/TI interrupt flags.

**IE (Interrupt Enable) — 0xA8:** Master enable bit (EA) plus individual enable bits for the 5 interrupt sources. Setting EA=1 and a specific bit enables that interrupt.

**IP (Interrupt Priority) — 0xB8:** Assigns each interrupt a **high** or **low** priority. A high-priority interrupt can pre-empt a low-priority one. After reset, all are set to low priority.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Harvard** and **Von Neumann** architectures. Justify why the 8051 uses Harvard architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz** crystal. Calculate the values to be loaded into **TH1 and TL1** to generate a baud rate of **4800 bps** in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) with **SMOD = 0** for serial communication. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Von Neumann Architecture:**
- Uses a **single memory space** for both instructions and data.
- A single bus carries both addresses and data.
- Limitation: The CPU cannot simultaneously fetch an instruction and read/write data — this is the **Von Neumann bottleneck**.
- Example: Intel 8086 (in real mode).

**Harvard Architecture:**
- Uses **two physically separate memory spaces** — one for program code (ROM) and one for data (RAM).
- Has **two independent sets of buses** — code bus (with $\overline{PSEN}$ control) and data bus (with $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$ controls).
- Allows **simultaneous instruction fetch and data access**, doubling throughput.
- Provides **code security**: data writes cannot corrupt the program memory.

**Why 8051 uses Harvard:**
- Embedded control demands **deterministic, real-time response**. Harvard's parallel bus access guarantees that an opcode fetch never waits for a data memory access, enabling **single-cycle instruction execution** for most instructions.
- The 8051 can fetch an opcode from code memory on one bus *while* the previous instruction's data is being written to RAM on the other bus — essential for high-speed mechatronic feedback loops.
- Harvard isolation also prevents the program counter from accidentally being overwritten by errant data writes, a critical safety feature in industrial control.

---

### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:**
- $f_{OSC} = 12 \, MHz$
- Desired Baud Rate = 4800 bps
- Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload)
- SMOD = 0

**Step 1: Recall the Baud Rate Formula for Timer 1, Mode 2**

$$Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Known Values**

$$4800 = \dfrac{2^0}{32} \cdot \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

$$4800 = \dfrac{1}{32} \cdot \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3: Simplify**

$$4800 = \dfrac{10^6}{32 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$

$$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{10^6}{32 \cdot 4800} = \dfrac{10^6}{153600} = 6.5104$$

Since the timer reload value must be an integer, we round: $256 - TH1 = 6$, which gives an actual baud rate of:

$$Baud_{actual} = \dfrac{10^6}{32 \cdot 6} = 5208.33 \, bps$$

**Closest standard value:** For the standard 4800 baud, the 8051 community conventionally uses the **11.0592 MHz** crystal (which gives an exact integer). For 12 MHz, the achievable closest baud rate is **5208** bps (error ≈ 8.5%) or **2400** bps (exact). We adopt **TH1 = 250 = 0xFA** for 2400 baud as a practical choice.

**Alternative — 2400 bps with 12 MHz:**

$$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{10^6}{32 \cdot 2400} = 13.02 \approx 13$$

$$TH1 = 256 - 13 = 243 = 0xF3$$

**Step 4: Load the Value**

$$TH1 = 0xF3, \quad TL1 = 0xF3 \text{ (auto-reload)}$$

**Step 5: Verification**

$$Baud = \dfrac{1}{32} \cdot \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \cdot 13} = \dfrac{10^6}{32 \cdot 13} = 2403.85 \, bps \approx 2400 \text{ bps} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> (i) Do **not** confuse **SMOD = 0** with **SMOD = 1**. The $2^{SMOD}$ factor doubles the baud rate when SMOD = 1 (this bit is in the **PCON** register, bit 7).
> (ii) Always state the **mode of Timer 1** (Mode 2 is auto-reload — meaning TH1 is auto-copied to TL1 on overflow).
> (iii) For an 8051 to communicate with a PC at standard baud rates (4800, 9600, 19200), the **crystal MUST be 11.0592 MHz** to yield integer reload values. With 12 MHz, the actual baud rate deviates from the nominal, and serial communication may fail. Mention this caveat in your answer for bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ⭐ **Microcontroller = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on a single chip** (definition).
- ⭐ The **8051** is an **8-bit, CISC, Harvard-architecture** MCU with **4 KB on-chip ROM, 128 bytes on-chip RAM, 4 I/O ports, 2 timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts**.
- ⭐ **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** At 12 MHz, one machine cycle = **1 µs**.
- ⭐ **SP initializes to 0x07** on reset; the stack grows upward.
- ⭐ **PSW bits**: C, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P — memorize their positions.
- ⭐ **Register banks 0–3** are switched via RS0/RS1 in PSW.
- ⭐ **Interrupts**: 5 sources; vectors at **0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023**; each ISR slot is **8 bytes wide**.
- ⭐ **Harvard** = separate code + data buses (faster, more secure). **Von Neumann** = single bus (simpler, bottleneck).
- ⭐ **Timer Mode 1** = 16-bit manual reload. **Timer Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload (commonly used for baud-rate generation).
- ⭐ **Baud Rate formula** for 8051 Timer 1, Mode 2:
  $$Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$$
- ⭐ For **exact PC communication** baud rates, use an **11.0592 MHz crystal**.
- ⭐ **P0** is open-drain and requires external pull-ups; it serves as the **multiplexed low-byte address/data bus** in external memory mode.
- ⭐ **$\overline{EA}$ = 0** forces all code fetches to external ROM; **$\overline{EA}$ = 1** uses internal ROM first.
- ⭐ **ALE** latches the low-byte of address from P0 into an external 74HC573 for memory demultiplexing.
- ⭐ **$\overline{PSEN}$** activates external program memory read; **$\overline{RD}$ / $\overline{WR}$** activate external data memory read/write.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Programming of Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Programming of Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microcontroller Programming** is the process of writing, assembling/compiling, and embedding a sequence of low-level machine instructions (firmware) into the non-volatile program memory of a microcontroller unit (MCU) to perform a dedicated, real-time control, sensing, or actuation task within an embedded mechatronic system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A microcontroller is a *single-chip microcomputer* containing a CPU, RAM, ROM/Flash, I/O ports, timers, and serial communication peripherals. Programming it is fundamentally different from programming a general-purpose computer because resources (memory, clock speed, word size) are **severely constrained**, and the program must typically run **forever in an infinite loop**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Exams

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Firmware** | The final binary (`.hex`/`.bin`) image stored permanently in the MCU's Flash/ROM. |
| **Cross-Compiler** | A compiler running on a PC that generates code for a *different* target MCU architecture. |
| **IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** | Software suite (e.g., Keil µVision, MPLAB X, Arduino IDE) combining editor, compiler, debugger. |
| **Assembler** | Translator that converts human-readable **mnemonics** (e.g., `MOV A, #05H`) into machine opcodes. |
| **Linker / Locator** | Combines multiple object files and assigns physical memory addresses. |
| **Programmer / Burner** | Hardware device that writes the compiled HEX file into the MCU's Flash memory. |
| **SFR (Special Function Register)** | Memory-mapped hardware control registers inside the MCU used to control peripherals. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Microcontroller as a Strict Bilingual Office Worker"

Imagine a microcontroller as a **highly disciplined, single-purpose office clerk** sitting inside a machine:
- The clerk understands only one language: **binary (0s and 1s)**.
- We, the engineers, speak English/C/Assembly — which he does **not** understand.
- So we hire a **translator** (the *compiler* or *assembler*) that runs on our big office computer (PC) and produces a strict, step-by-step list of orders in the clerk's native language.
- These orders are written into a **permanent instruction diary** (Flash/ROM) using a special pen (*programmer*).
- The clerk then reads his diary every morning at power-on and executes each instruction **one by one, forever**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why programming microcontrollers differs from PC programming:** There is **no Operating System (OS)**, **no file system**, and **no dynamic memory allocation** in most KTU-level MCU applications. The program begins at a fixed vector (e.g., `0x0000` for 8051) and must manage *every* clock cycle and *every* bit manually.

## 1.4 Major Programming Languages Used in KTU Curriculum

1. **Assembly Language** — Direct mnemonics, 1-to-1 mapping with machine code. Used in time-critical and memory-tight mechatronic code.
2. **Embedded C** — A constrained dialect of ANSI C (e.g., Keil C51 for 8051, XC8 for PIC, CMSIS C for ARM). Most common in industry and KTU labs.
3. **Graphical / Block Languages** — e.g., Arduino Sketch, Simulink/Stateflow blocks. Used for rapid prototyping in mechatronics.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners often ask: *"Why is Embedded C preferred over Assembly in modern mechatronics?"* — Answer: Better **readability**, **portability** across MCU families, **easier maintenance**, and **rich libraries** — at a marginal cost in code size and speed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Model of a Microcontroller Program

A KTU-grade microcontroller program is best understood as a stack of three functional layers:

- **Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Direct manipulation of SFRs and pins (e.g., `P1 = 0xFF;` to drive all Port-1 pins HIGH).
- **Layer 2 — Driver Layer:** Mid-level functions such as `delay_ms()`, `uart_send()`, `adc_read()`. These call the HAL.
- **Layer 3 — Application Layer:** The `main()` function and the **super-loop**, which orchestrates all driver calls forever.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Typical Embedded C Program (8051/ARM Generic)

```c
#include <reg51.h>            // SFR definitions for the target MCU

/* ---- Layer 3: Application ---- */
void main(void)
{
    P1 = 0x00;                // Initialize Port 1 to LOW (HAL)
    TMOD = 0x01;              // Configure Timer 0 in Mode 1 (HAL)
    
    while(1)                  // Super-loop (Application)
    {
        P1 = 0xFF;            // Turn all LEDs ON
        delay_ms(500);
        P1 = 0x00;            // Turn all LEDs OFF
        delay_ms(500);
    }
}
```

The three critical structural rules visible above are:

- `#include <reg51.h>` — brings in the **memory map** of every SFR.
- The **infinite `while(1)` loop** — the program's "heartbeat".
- Direct SFR write `P1 = 0x00;` — no function call, no OS, no buffering.

## 2.3 Program Development Tool Chain (Cross-Development)

The flow from source code to execution inside the chip is:

`Editor → Preprocessor → Compiler/Assembler → Assembler → Linker/Locator → HEX file → Programmer → Flash ROM → CPU Execution`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it is called "Cross" Development:** The *host* (your laptop) uses x86 architecture with GBs of RAM running Windows/Linux. The *target* (the MCU) is a Harvard-architecture 8/32-bit chip with KBs of RAM. The two architectures are *different*, hence the term **cross-compilation**.

## 2.4 Machine Cycle & Instruction Timing — The Heartbeat of MCU Code

The most fundamental timing relationship in MCU programming:

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}}$$

where
- $T_{MC}$ = Duration of **one machine cycle** in seconds.
- $f_{OSC}$ = External crystal oscillator frequency in **Hz**.
- $f_{MC}$ = Internal machine-cycle clock frequency in **Hz**.

The reason for the **division by 12** is that the MCU divides the oscillator by 12 internally to perform an instruction fetch-decode-execute sequence per machine cycle (e.g., 8051, classic 8052).

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern ARM Cortex-M MCUs** do *not* use a 1/12 ratio. They use a bus clock divider of 1, so 1 machine cycle = 1 oscillator cycle. The 8051 formula above applies specifically to the **8051 family**, which is the KTU 2024 default syllabus MCU.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` instead of vertical bars in all table cells. LaTeX formulas are wrapped in inline `$$...$$` where necessary to avoid breaking table layout.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine cycle time | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | $f_{OSC}$: crystal frequency | seconds |
| 2 | Timer tick frequency | $f_{T} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | For 8051 family | Hz |
| 3 | Timer tick period | $T_{T} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | One timer increment | seconds |
| 4 | Required timer counts | $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{T}}$ | $T_{delay}$: desired delay | counts |
| 5 | Timer reload value (16-bit) | $THTL = 65536 - N$ | For 8051 Mode 1 | decimal |
| 6 | Baud rate (8051, T1 Mode 2) | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$ | Standard 8051 serial | bits/s |
| 7 | TH1 for desired baud | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud}$ | When SMOD = 0 | integer |
| 8 | Instruction cycles per `NOP` | 1 machine cycle | For 8051 | cycles |
| 9 | Loop delay (software) | $T_{d} = N_{loop} \cdot k \cdot T_{MC}$ | $k$ = cycles per loop body | seconds |
| 10 | Flash endurance | $\approx 10{,}000$ to $100{,}000$ write/erase cycles | Vendor dependent | cycles |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU:** Code is written in Embedded C, cross-compiled, and flashed into an ARM Cortex-M3. Even a 1 ms timing error can misfire an injector.
- **Industrial PLC / Robotic Arm:** Assembly-optimized interrupt routines ensure deterministic sub-microsecond response.
- **Medical Infusion Pump:** Programmed to 100% deterministic, fail-safe loops; programming errors are a **patient-safety** issue, not a software bug.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Converting Assembly Mnemonic to Machine Code

**Problem:** Convert the 8051 instruction `MOV A, #0F8H` (Move immediate value `0xF8` into the **Accumulator**) into its 16-bit machine code.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic **KTL Part-A** type question. The student must know the **opcode table** for the 8051 instruction set.

### Step 1 — Identify the instruction class

The `MOV A, #data` instruction belongs to the class of **immediate-to-accumulator** loads. In the 8051 ISA, this is a **2-byte, 1-cycle** instruction.

### Step 2 — Identify the opcode byte

The base opcode for `MOV A, #immediate` is fixed by the silicon design:

$$Opcode = 74H$$

### Step 3 — Append the immediate operand

The second byte of the instruction is simply the 8-bit immediate operand value, as supplied in the source line:

$$Byte\ 2 = F8H$$

### Step 4 — Form the final machine word

$$
\begin{aligned}
Machine\ Code &= 74H\ F8H \\
&= \underbrace{0111\ 0100}_{Opcode}\ \underbrace{1111\ 1000}_{Immediate\ Data}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In a Keil-generated `.LST` (list) file, this instruction appears as:
> `74 F8    MOV A,#0F8H   ;  1 byte(s)  1 cycle(s)`

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Software Delay Loop Timing Analysis

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. A software delay loop is written in Assembly as follows. Compute the actual time delay produced.

```asm
DELAY:  MOV  R0, #0FFH    ; (1) Load R0 with 255
LOOP:   DJNZ R0, LOOP     ; (2) Decrement R0; if not zero, jump to LOOP
        RET                ; (3) Return to caller
```

### Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} \\
&= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 1.085\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine cycles per instruction

| Instruction | Machine Cycles | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV R0, #0FFH` | 1 cycle | 2-byte immediate load |
| `DJNZ R0, LOOP` | 2 cycles | Decrement + conditional jump |
| `RET` | 2 cycles | Pop PC from stack |

### Step 3 — Count the total cycles

The `DJNZ` instruction executes **255 times** (the initial value of R0 = 255 decrements to 0 before the fall-through).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 1_{MOV} + (255 \times 2)_{DJNZ} + 2_{RET} \\
&= 1 + 510 + 2 \\
&= 513\ cycles
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert cycles to time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{delay} &= N_{total} \times T_{MC} \\
&= 513 \times 1.085\ \mu s \\
&= 556.6\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting that `DJNZ` takes **2 cycles**, not 1, and forgetting the **setup (`MOV`) and teardown (`RET`) cycles** outside the loop. Always use the vendor's *instruction-set timing table* — never guess.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Hardware Timer Delay Computation

**Problem:** Using Timer 0 of an 8051 in **Mode 1 (16-bit)** with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz, generate a delay of exactly **50 ms**. Compute `TH0` and `TL0`.

### Step 1 — Compute the timer tick period

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{T} &= \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} \\
&= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 1.085\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the required count

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{T}} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 46{,}080\ counts
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the timer reload value (16-bit unsigned)

$$
\begin{aligned}
THTL &= 65536 - N \\
&= 65536 - 46080 \\
&= 19{,}456 \\
&= 4C00H
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Split into TH0 and TL0

$$
\begin{aligned}
TH0 &= High\ Byte(4C00H) = 4CH \\
TL0 &= Low\ Byte(4C00H) = 00H
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
Overflow\ after\ loading\ 4C00H &= 65536 - 19456 \\
&= 46080\ ticks \\
Time &= 46080 \times 1.085\ \mu s \\
&= 49{,}996.8\ \mu s \approx 50\ ms\ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 11.0592 MHz crystal is preferred over 12 MHz:** It is a *magic frequency* chosen by Intel because it makes the UART baud rate divide down to an *exact integer* — eliminating serial communication timing errors. This is why **all serial-programmed 8051 kits use 11.0592 MHz**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Programming Constructs vs. Real-World Use

| Programming Construct | Syntax Example | Real Mechatronic Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bit manipulation | `P1_0 = 1;` | Drive a single solenoid valve | Very High |
| Bit-test branch | `if(P3_2 == 0)` | Read a push-button input | Very High |
| Lookup table | `code unsigned char seg[] = {0x3F, 0x06,...};` | 7-segment LED display decoding | High |
| Subroutine call | `delay_ms(50);` | Time-based de-bouncing | Very High |
| Interrupt service | `void ext0(void) interrupt 0` | Emergency stop button | High |
| Pointer to SFR | `volatile unsigned char *p = &P1;` | Generic port driver | Medium |
| Nested loops | `for(i=0;i<255;i++) for(j=0;j<255;j++);` | Long software delays | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The MCU Programming Tool Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code in C or Assembly] --> B[Preprocessor]
    B --> C[Cross Compiler or Assembler]
    C --> D[Object File .obj]
    D --> E[Linker and Locator]
    E --> F[HEX File .hex]
    F --> G[Hardware Programmer Burner]
    G --> H[Flash ROM inside MCU]
    H --> I[CPU Fetches and Executes Instructions]
    I --> J[Hardware Peripherals Respond]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic Embedded C Program Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainA[Power On Reset Vector 0x0000] --> mainB[Initialize Stack Pointer]
    mainB --> mainC[Initialize SFRs and Variables]
    mainC --> mainD[Hardware Abstraction Layer Calls]
    mainD --> superLoop{Super Loop while 1}
    superLoop --> task1[Task 1 Read Sensors via ADC]
    task1 --> task2[Task 2 Run Control Algorithm]
    task2 --> task3[Task 3 Drive Actuators via PWM]
    task3 --> task4[Task 4 Update Display and UART]
    task4 --> superLoop
    superLoop -.->|Async Event| isr[Interrupt Service Routine]
    isr --> superLoop
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Memory Organization Inside a Typical 8-bit MCU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CodeSpace["Program Memory Flash"]
        A0[0x0000 Reset Vector]
        A1[0x0003 External Int 0 ISR]
        A2[0x000B Timer 0 ISR]
        A3[0x0013 External Int 1 ISR]
        A4[0x001B Timer 1 ISR]
        A5[0x0023 Serial Port ISR]
        A6[0x0030 to 0xFFFF User Code and Constants]
    end
    subgraph DataSpace["Internal RAM 128 bytes"]
        B0[0x00 to 0x1F Register Banks RB0 to RB3]
        B1[0x20 to 0x2F Bit Addressable Area]
        B2[0x30 to 0x7F General Purpose RAM]
    end
    subgraph SFRs["Special Function Registers 0x80 to 0xFF"]
        C0[Port 0 P0]
        C1[Port 1 P1]
        C2[Port 2 P2]
        C3[Port 3 P3]
        C4[Accumulator A]
        C5[B Register]
        C6[Stack Pointer SP]
        C7[Data Pointer DPTR]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Cross-Development Host vs. Target

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Host["HOST PC x86 GB RAM"]
        E1[Keil uVision IDE]
        E2[Editor]
        E3[C51 Cross Compiler]
        E4[Linker]
        E5[Object to Hex Converter OH51]
    end
    subgraph Target["TARGET 8051 KB RAM"]
        F1[Flash ROM stores .hex]
        F2[8051 CPU fetches instructions]
        F3[On chip Peripherals respond]
    end
    E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5
    E5 -->|Serial or USB| F1
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Interrupt-Driven vs. Polling Programming Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Polling["POLLING Model CPU Busy Wait"]
        P1[Start] --> P2[Check Sensor Flag in Loop]
        P2 -->|No Event| P3[Do Other Tasks]
        P3 --> P2
        P2 -->|Yes Event| P4[Handle Event]
        P4 --> P2
    end
    subgraph Interrupt["INTERRUPT Model CPU Free for Main Task"]
        I1[Main Super Loop Runs Continuously]
        I1 -.->|Hardware Signal| I2[Interrupt Flag Set by Peripheral]
        I2 --> I3[CPU Saves PC to Stack]
        I3 --> I4[CPU Jumps to ISR Vector]
        I4 --> I5[ISR Handles Event in Microseconds]
        I5 --> I6[RETI Restores PC and Resumes Main]
        I6 --> I1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a microcontroller. List any four programming languages used to program microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A microcontroller is a **single-chip microcomputer** that contains a CPU, RAM, ROM/Flash, I/O ports, timers, and communication peripherals on a single integrated circuit, designed for embedded real-time control applications.

Four programming languages used to program microcontrollers are:

1. **Assembly Language** — lowest-level, 1-to-1 with machine code.
2. **Embedded C** — constrained ANSI C, most widely used in industry.
3. **Embedded C++ / Embedded Python (MicroPython)** — for high-end MCUs (e.g., ESP32).
4. **Graphical / Block languages** — LabVIEW, Simulink, Arduino graphical IDE.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if the student defines the MCU AND lists *four distinct* languages with at least one identifying feature.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: What is a Special Function Register (SFR)? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Special Function Register (SFR)** is a memory-mapped hardware register located in the upper internal RAM of the microcontroller (addresses `0x80` to `0xFF` for the 8051) that is used to directly control or monitor the on-chip peripherals such as I/O ports, timers, and the serial port.

**Two examples:**

- `P1` (Port 1 Data Register, address `0x90`) — controls the logic level on the 8 pins of Port 1.
- `TMOD` (Timer Mode Register, address `0x89`) — selects the mode (0/1/2/3) and operation (timer/counter) of the two internal timers.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. List its **four** major on-chip peripherals. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain the **cross-development tool chain** used to program a microcontroller. State *two* advantages of using Embedded C over Assembly language. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 8051 Architecture (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The 8051 microcontroller is a **Harvard-architecture**, 8-bit, single-chip microcomputer. Its major blocks are:

- **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** — contains the **Accumulator (A)**, **B register**, **Program Counter (PC)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **Data Pointer (DPTR)**, and the **Arithmetic-Logic Unit (ALU)**.
- **Program Memory (Flash/ROM)** — 4 KB on-chip, address range `0x0000`–`0x0FFF`, used to store the user firmware.
- **Internal Data Memory (RAM)** — 128 bytes (`0x00`–`0x7F`) of general-purpose RAM plus 128 bytes (`0x80`–`0xFF`) of SFR area.
- **Oscillator & Clock Circuit** — drives the CPU from an external crystal, typically **11.0592 MHz** or 12 MHz.
- **I/O Ports** — four 8-bit bidirectional ports: **P0, P1, P2, P3** (32 I/O lines total).
- **Serial Port (UART)** — full-duplex asynchronous communication.

**Four Major On-Chip Peripherals (2 marks):**

1. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) — for time-delay generation, event counting, and baud-rate generation.
2. **Full-Duplex UART (Serial Port)** — for RS-232 communication with PC or another MCU.
3. **Interrupt Controller** — supports 5 interrupt sources: External 0, External 1, Timer 0, Timer 1, and Serial Port.
4. **Four 8-bit I/O Ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3 for direct digital interfacing.

**Key Architectural Features (2 marks):**

- 8-bit ALU performing arithmetic, logical, and bit-manipulation instructions.
- Separate 16-bit Program Counter allows 64 KB program memory addressing.
- Boolean processor allows direct bit-level set/clear/test operations on the bit-addressable area `0x20`–`0x2F` and on bit-addressable SFRs.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Cross-Development Tool Chain (7 marks)

**Cross-Development Flow Diagram (4 marks):**

The cross-development of an MCU program follows the chain:

**Step 1 — Source Code Editing:** The programmer writes the firmware in `main.c` (or `.asm`) using a text editor inside the IDE (e.g., Keil µVision).

**Step 2 — Preprocessing:** The preprocessor handles directives such as `#include <reg51.h>` and `#define`, producing an expanded `.i` source file.

**Step 3 — Cross-Compilation/Assembly:** The C51 compiler (or A51 assembler) translates the source into an **8051 object file (.obj)**. Each instruction family produces its own relocatable code.

**Step 4 — Linking and Locating:** The BL51 linker combines all `.obj` files and library files, then the **LX51 locator** assigns absolute physical addresses to every symbol, producing a single absolute object file.

**Step 5 — HEX File Generation:** The **OH51 object-to-HEX converter** produces an Intel-HEX file (`.hex`) — a text file containing the binary program in ASCII-hex format.

**Step 6 — Programming the Device:** A hardware programmer (serial ISP, USB, or JTAG) writes the HEX file into the MCU's Flash memory.

**Step 7 — Execution:** On power-on, the 8051 CPU begins fetching from address `0x0000` and executes the instructions one machine cycle at a time.

**Two Advantages of Embedded C over Assembly (3 marks):**

1. **Readability and Maintainability:** `P1 = 0xFF;` is far more readable than the equivalent `MOV 90H, #0FFH`. A new engineer can understand Embedded C in minutes, but Assembly requires days of study.
2. **Portability across MCU families:** A well-written Embedded C program (using vendor HALs) can be retargeted from 8051 → PIC → ARM with **minimal changes**, whereas Assembly code must be *rewritten from scratch* for each new architecture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Q.A:**
> 1. Students often **omit the bit-addressable RAM region** when drawing the memory map; this costs 1 mark.
> 2. Students confuse **Harvard vs Von Neumann** architecture — 8051 is *strictly Harvard* (separate code and data buses). Award 0 for Von Neumann.
> 3. For Q.A(b), students forget to mention the **preprocessor** step, treating compiler and preprocessor as the same. They are *not* the same.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of an Embedded C program for the 8051 with a suitable example. List any **four preprocessor directives** used in such programs. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a worked example, derive the value of `TH0` and `TL0` required to generate a **50 ms delay** using Timer 0 in **Mode 1**, given a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. Verify the result. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Embedded C Program Structure (7 marks)

**General Structure (3 marks):**

A typical Embedded C program for the 8051 consists of the following blocks in order:

1. **Header include directive** — e.g., `#include <reg51.h>` to bring in the SFR definitions.
2. **Macro definitions** — e.g., `#define LED P1` to give meaningful names to ports/pins.
3. **Global variable declarations** — e.g., `unsigned char count;`.
4. **Function prototypes** — e.g., `void delay_ms(unsigned int);`.
5. **Function definitions** — driver functions and the main function.
6. **`main()` function** — contains initialization code and the **super-loop**.

**Example Program — Blinking LED (2 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

#define LED P1

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms);   // Function prototype

void main(void)
{
    LED = 0x00;                   // Initialize LED port LOW
    while(1)                      // Super-loop
    {
        LED = 0xFF;               // All LEDs ON
        delay_ms(500);            // Wait 500 ms
        LED = 0x00;               // All LEDs OFF
        delay_ms(500);            // Wait 500 ms
    }
}

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i=0; i<ms; i++)
        for(j=0; j<1275; j++);    // calibrated for 11.0592 MHz
}
```

**Four Preprocessor Directives (2 marks):**

| Directive | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `#include` | Inserts contents of a header file (e.g., `<reg51.h>`). |
| `#define` | Defines a macro / symbolic constant (e.g., `#define ON 1`). |
| `#ifdef / #endif` | Conditional compilation — include code only if a symbol is defined. |
| `#pragma` | Compiler-specific directive (e.g., `#pragma SRC` to embed assembly in C). |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Timer Delay Derivation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $f_{OSC} = 11.0592\ MHz$
- Mode 1 (16-bit timer) — count range 0 to 65,535
- Required delay $T_{delay} = 50\ ms$

**Step 1 — Machine cycle time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{T} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Required number of counts (1.5 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}080\ counts
$$

**Step 3 — Reload value for 16-bit timer (1.5 marks):**

$$
THTL = 65536 - 46080 = 19{,}456 = 4C00H
$$

**Step 4 — Split into TH0 and TL0 (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TH0 &= 4CH \\
TL0 &= 00H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (2 marks):**

```
TMOD = 0x01;    // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
TH0  = 0x4C;    // High byte of reload
TL0  = 0x00;    // Low byte of reload
TR0  = 1;       // Start Timer 0
while(TF0 == 0);// Wait for overflow flag
TR0  = 0;       // Stop timer
TF0  = 0;       // Clear overflow flag
```

Overflow occurs after $65536 - 19456 = 46080$ timer ticks:

$$
T = 46080 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 49{,}996.8\ \mu s \approx 50\ ms\ \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Q.B:**
> 1. **Mode selection error:** Many students use Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) and write `TH0 = 0x4C; TL0 = 0x00;` — but in Mode 2, **only TH0 holds the reload**, and TL0 counts. The calculation differs. Always state the *Mode* explicitly.
> 2. **Unit error:** Writing $T_T = 1.085\ ms$ instead of $1.085\ \mu s$ cascades a 1000× error into the final answer — *instant zero*.
> 3. **Forgetting to clear `TF0`:** The overflow flag must be manually cleared in software, or the program will hang thinking the timer has *just* overflowed.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- **Microcontroller = CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O + Timers + UART on one chip.**
- **Programming microcontrollers is *cross-development***: host PC (x86) compiles for target MCU (8051/ARM/PIC).
- **Tool chain order:** *Source → Preprocessor → Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Locator → HEX → Programmer → Flash → Execution.*
- **The super-loop (`while(1)`) is mandatory** in bare-metal Embedded C programs — there is no OS to return to.
- **SFRs are memory-mapped hardware registers** in the upper 128 bytes of internal 8051 RAM (`0x80`–`0xFF`).
- **Bit-addressable area** is `0x20`–`0x2F` plus bit-addressable SFRs — allows single-bit operations like `P1_0 = 1;`.
- **Machine cycle time:** $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$ — every timing calculation begins here.
- **Instruction cycle counts matter:** `MOV` = 1 cycle, `DJNZ` = 2 cycles, `MUL AB` = 4 cycles, `RET`/`RETI` = 2 cycles.
- **Timer reload formula (16-bit):** $THTL = 65536 - (T_{delay} \cdot f_{OSC} / 12)$.
- **Baud rate formula (8051):** $TH1 = 256 - (f_{OSC} / (384 \cdot Baud))$ for SMOD = 0.
- **11.0592 MHz crystal is standard** because it makes the UART baud-rate divisor an *exact integer*.
- **Interrupt vs Polling:** Polling consumes CPU cycles; interrupts allow the CPU to do useful work and respond *only* when an event occurs.
- **Embedded C advantages over Assembly:** readability, portability, maintainability, library support.
- **Embedded C disadvantages vs Assembly:** slightly larger code, slower execution, less deterministic timing.
- **Preprocessor directives to remember:** `#include`, `#define`, `#ifdef`, `#endif`, `#pragma`.
- **Flash endurance is finite** (~10K–100K write cycles) — never use Flash for frequently updated variables.
- **Always declare hardware registers as `volatile`** in C — e.g., `volatile unsigned char x;` to prevent the compiler from *optimizing away* repeated reads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** The most frequently asked Module-2 long question is *"Derive TH0 and TL0 for a given delay"*. Memorize the four-step derivation (Tick Period → Counts → Reload Value → Split TH0/TL0 → Verify). Practice it for **11.0592 MHz** and **12 MHz** — both appear in past papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Response of Systems: First and Second Order Systems, Transfer Function, Systems in Series and in Feedback Loop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET502 - SENSORS AND MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Response of Systems: First and Second Order Systems, Transfer Function, Systems in Series and in Feedback Loop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Dynamic Response of a System?

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Response** is the *time-domain behaviour* of a system when subjected to a changing input. It describes *how* and *how fast* a system's output reaches a new steady-state condition when the input is altered (a step, a ramp, a pulse, or any arbitrary forcing function).

In mechatronic systems, every physical element (motor, sensor, amplifier, hydraulic actuator, thermal plant) has **inertia, damping, and storage elements** (mass, capacitor, inductor, spring). Because of these, the output does *not* follow the input instantly. The path the output traces from the initial value to the final value is the **dynamic response**.

**Three characteristics fully describe any linear dynamic response:**
1. **Transient Response** – the part that decays with time (the *during* behaviour).
2. **Steady-State Response** – the value approached as $t \to \infty$ (the *after* behaviour).
3. **Stability** – bounded output for bounded input (BIBO stability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A mechatronic engineer MUST master dynamic response analysis because sensor–actuator pairs, controllers (PID), and plant dynamics are mathematically modelled as **first-order** or **second-order** systems. The design of a stable, fast, and accurate closed-loop controller is impossible without understanding the time-domain and frequency-domain behaviour covered in this module.

---

## 1.2 First-Order System — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **First-Order System** is a system whose input–output relationship is described by a **first-order ordinary differential equation (ODE)**. It contains a single energy-storage element. Mathematically, the canonical form is:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \, x(t)$$

where $x(t)$ is the input, $y(t)$ is the output, $K$ is the **static (DC) gain** (dimensionless, units = output/input), and $\tau$ is the **time constant** (in **seconds, s**).

**Physical Realisations of a First-Order System:**
- An **RC electrical circuit** (resistor–capacitor low-pass filter).
- A **thermal system** (heater + sensor with heat capacitance and thermal resistance).
- A **liquid level tank** with a single outflow orifice.
- A **thermometer** plunged into a hot bath.
- A **DC motor at constant load** (armature current dynamics with no load inertia).

---

## 1.3 Second-Order System — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Second-Order System** is governed by a **second-order ODE** and contains **two independent energy-storage elements**. The canonical time-domain form is:

$$\frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2} \, y(t) = K\omega_{n}^{2} \, x(t)$$

where $\omega_{n}$ is the **undamped natural frequency** (in **rad/s**) and $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio** (dimensionless, $\zeta \geq 0$).

**Physical Realisations of a Second-Order System:**
- **Mass–Spring–Damper (MSD)** mechanical system.
- A **DC motor driving an inertial load** (armature + rotational inertia).
- An **RLC electrical circuit** (series or parallel).
- A **servo-hydraulic position actuator**.
- A **piezoelectric cantilever beam** used as a micro-sensor.

---

## 1.4 Transfer Function — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> The **Transfer Function** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is defined as the **ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input**, assuming *all initial conditions are zero*:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} \bigg|_{\text{zero IC}}$$

where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the **complex Laplace variable** (in **rad/s**). It is a *rational polynomial function* in $s$ and completely characterises the LTI system in the frequency domain.

---

## 1.5 Systems in Series (Cascade) and in Feedback Loop — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Series (Cascade) Connection:** Two (or more) subsystems $G_{1}(s)$ and $G_{2}(s)$ are connected such that the *output of $G_{1}$ is the input of $G_{2}$*. The equivalent transfer function is the **product**:

$$G_{series}(s) = G_{1}(s) \cdot G_{2}(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Feedback (Closed-Loop) Connection:** The output is *fed back* and subtracted from (or added to) the input. For a **negative feedback** system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$:

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

If $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback), the expression reduces to $G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.

---

## 1.6 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🍲 First-Order Analogy — "Boiling Water in a Saucepan"
Imagine you switch on an electric kettle (input = heat on) and observe the water temperature rising. The temperature does **not jump** to $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ instantly — it rises **exponentially** and approaches boiling asymptotically. The faster the kettle's heating element (smaller $\tau$), the quicker it reaches the target. This exponential rise of water temperature is the **hallmark of a first-order system**. The single energy-storage element is the *thermal capacitance of water*.

### 🚗 Second-Order Analogy — "Pressing a Car's Shock Absorber"
Push down on the rear bumper of a car and release. The body bobs **up and down** a few times before settling — this is the transient response of a **second-order mass–spring–damper system** (chassis mass + suspension springs + shock-absorber damping). If the shock absorbers are worn ($\zeta$ small), the car oscillates many times (underdamped). If they are brand new and tight ($\zeta \approx 1$), the car returns to rest in one smooth motion (critically/overdamped).

### 🔁 Feedback Analogy — "Driver Steering a Car"
The driver is the *controller*; the steering wheel angle is the *input*; the car's lateral position on the road is the *output*. The driver's *eyes* form the **feedback sensor** (measuring the error = deviation from lane centre). The driver's *brain* adjusts the steering to **minimise the error**. This continuous sense–compare–correct loop is exactly the **negative feedback control loop** of classical control theory.

---

## 1.7 Important Distinctions at a Glance

| Property | First-Order System | Second-Order System |
|---|---|---|
| Order of ODE | 1 | 2 |
| Energy-storage elements | 1 | 2 |
| Poles of $G(s)$ | 1 real pole at $-1/\tau$ | 2 poles (real/complex conjugate) |
| Step response shape | Pure exponential rise/fall | Rise + possible oscillation |
| Key performance parameters | Time constant $\tau$ | $\omega_{n}$, $\zeta$, $M_{p}$, $t_{s}$ |
| BIBO stable if | $\tau > 0$ | All poles in LHP (depends on $\zeta$ and $\omega_{n}$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order System — Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis

The governing equation of a canonical first-order system is:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \, x(t) \qquad \text{...(2.1)}$$

**Operational Logic Flow:**
- Apply the Laplace transform (with zero initial conditions $y(0)=0$):
  $$\tau s Y(s) + Y(s) = K X(s)$$
- Factor out $Y(s)$:
  $$Y(s)(\tau s + 1) = K X(s)$$
- The **transfer function** emerges as:
  $$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \qquad \text{...(2.2)}$$

**Step Response Derivation (for a unit-step input $x(t) = 1$, so $X(s) = 1/s$):**

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Performing **partial-fraction expansion**:

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{\tau s + 1}$$

Solving for $A$ and $B$: multiply through by $s(\tau s + 1)$:
$$K = A(\tau s + 1) + Bs \;\Rightarrow\; A = K,\; B = -K\tau$$

Hence $Y(s) = \dfrac{K}{s} - \dfrac{K\tau}{\tau s + 1} = \dfrac{K}{s} - \dfrac{K}{s + 1/\tau}$.

Inverse Laplace transform gives the **time-domain step response**:

$$\boxed{y(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)} \qquad \text{...(2.3)}$$

**Why this matters:** At $t = \tau$, the output has reached $63.2\%$ of its final value. At $t = 4\tau$, the output has reached $98.2\%$ — engineers treat $4\tau$ as the *practical settling time* for a first-order system.

---

## 2.2 Second-Order System — Deep Theoretical Analysis

The canonical second-order transfer function (standard form) is:

$$G(s) = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}} \qquad \text{...(2.4)}$$

The **characteristic equation** is $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$, whose roots are the system poles:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2} - 1} \qquad \text{...(2.5)}$$

### 2.2.1 The Four Damping Cases

| Case | Damping Ratio | Pole Location | Step Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Undamped** | $\zeta = 0$ | Imaginary: $\pm j\omega_{n}$ | Pure sustained oscillation |
| **Underdamped** | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Complex conjugates (LHP) | Decaying oscillation with overshoot |
| **Critically damped** | $\zeta = 1$ | Real, repeated at $-\omega_{n}$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return |
| **Overdamped** | $\zeta > 1$ | Two distinct real negative poles | Slow exponential, no overshoot |

### 2.2.2 Step Response of an Underdamped Second-Order System

For a unit-step input, with $0 < \zeta < 1$, the underdamped step response is:

$$\boxed{y(t) = K\left[1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t} \sin\!\left(\omega_{d} t + \phi\right)\right]} \qquad \text{...(2.6)}$$

where the **damped natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} \qquad \text{...(2.7)}$$

and the **phase shift** is:

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right) \qquad \text{...(2.8)}$$

### 2.2.3 Key Time-Domain Performance Indices

| Performance Index | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Overshoot** $M_{p}$ | $M_{p} = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\%$ | How much output overshoots the steady-state value (in %) |
| **Peak Time** $t_{p}$ | $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | Time at which the maximum peak occurs |
| **Rise Time** $t_{r}$ (0–100\%) | $t_{r} = \dfrac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}}$ | Time for output to first reach the final value |
| **Settling Time** $t_{s}$ (2\% criterion) | $t_{s} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | Time to stay within $\pm 2\%$ of final value |
| **Settling Time** $t_{s}$ (5\% criterion) | $t_{s} \approx \dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | Time to stay within $\pm 5\%$ of final value |

---

## 2.3 Systems in Series (Cascade)

When $n$ subsystems $G_{1}(s), G_{2}(s), \dots, G_{n}(s)$ are connected in cascade:

$$\boxed{G_{series}(s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} G_{i}(s) = G_{1}(s) G_{2}(s) \cdots G_{n}(s)} \qquad \text{...(2.9)}$$

**Important Caveat:** This product is valid **only when the loading effect of one stage on the previous stage is negligible**, i.e., each stage has an *ideal* input impedance (infinite for voltage inputs, zero for current inputs). In practice, the engineer must verify non-loading conditions before applying the cascade rule.

---

## 2.4 Negative Feedback Loop (Closed-Loop Transfer Function)

The canonical negative feedback loop has:
- Forward (plant + controller) path: $G(s)$
- Feedback path: $H(s)$ (often unity, $H=1$)

The standard block-diagram reduction yields:

$$\boxed{G_{CL}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}} \qquad \text{...(2.10)}$$

For a **positive feedback** loop (used in oscillators, regenerative sensors):

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)} \qquad \text{...(2.11)}$$

### 2.4.1 Stability Criterion (Nyquist)

The closed-loop system is **BIBO stable if and only if** all roots of the characteristic polynomial $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ lie in the **open Left-Half Plane (LHP)** (i.e., real parts < 0). The Routh–Hurwitz criterion, root-locus method, and Nyquist criterion are the three standard tools used to determine this.

---

## 2.5 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM REVISION TABLE** — print or photograph this section. All formulas here are examinable.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-order ODE | $\tau \dot{y} + y = K x$ | $\tau$ in **s**, $K$ dimensionless |
| 2 | First-order transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | Pole at $s = -1/\tau$ |
| 3 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | At $t=\tau$, $y=0.632 K$ |
| 4 | Settling time (first-order) | $t_{s} \approx 4\tau$ | 2% band |
| 5 | Second-order ODE | $\ddot{y} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\dot{y} + \omega_{n}^{2} y = K\omega_{n}^{2} x$ | $\omega_{n}$ in **rad/s**, $\zeta$ dimensionless |
| 6 | Second-order TF (standard) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ | Standard form |
| 7 | System poles | $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1}$ | LHP ⇒ stable |
| 8 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Peak overshoot | $M_{p} = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\%$ | Percent |
| 10 | Peak time | $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$ | seconds |
| 11 | Rise time (approx.) | $t_{r} \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_{n}}$ | seconds |
| 12 | Settling time (2\%) | $t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | seconds |
| 13 | Settling time (5\%) | $t_{s} = \dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | seconds |
| 14 | Series combination | $G_{ser}(s) = G_{1}(s) G_{2}(s) \cdots G_{n}(s)$ | Product |
| 15 | Negative feedback (closed-loop) | $G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Unity if $H=1$ |
| 16 | Positive feedback | $G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Used in oscillators |
| 17 | BIBO stability | All poles of $G_{CL}(s)$ must lie in LHP | $\text{Re}(s_{i}) < 0$ |
| 18 | Static error constants ($K_{p}, K_{v}, K_{a}$) | $K_{p}=\lim_{s\to 0}G(s)$ etc. | Position, velocity, accel. |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Dominant Dynamic Order | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — accelerometer | Second-order | Determines sensor bandwidth & resonance |
| **DC servo motor position control** | Second-order (armature + inertia) | Sets the achievable accuracy & speed |
| **Thermocouple / RTD temperature sensor** | First-order | Time constant dictates measurement lag |
| **Pneumatic pressure regulator** | First-order | Pressure ramp rate must be controlled |
| **Robotic manipulator arm joint** | Second-order (coupled) | Critical for trajectory tracking & stability |
| **Piezoelectric stack actuator** | First-order (electrical) + second-order (mech. resonance) | High-bandwidth nanopositioning |
| **PID controller tuning** | Both orders | Ziegler–Nichols rules rely on $\tau$, $L$, $K_{u}$, $T_{u}$ |
| **MEMS gyroscope / accelerometer** | Second-order | Quality factor $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ is a sensor spec |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reader's Note:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. Read in sequence; each numbered block is one *logical transition*.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Response of a First-Order System from ODE to Time Domain

**Given:**
$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K x(t), \qquad y(0) = 0, \qquad x(t) = u(t) \text{ (unit step)}$$

**Goal:** Derive $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Laplace transform on both sides of the ODE

Using $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{y}(t)\} = sY(s) - y(0)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = X(s)$ (with $y(0)=0$):

$$\tau s Y(s) + Y(s) = K X(s)$$

### Step 2 — Factor out $Y(s)$ on the left-hand side

$$Y(s)\,(\tau s + 1) = K X(s)$$

### Step 3 — Express the transfer function and substitute the unit-step input

For a unit step, $X(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. The output is:

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

### Step 4 — Partial-fraction expansion

Assume: $\dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{\tau s + 1}$.

Multiplying both sides by $s(\tau s + 1)$:

$$K = A(\tau s + 1) + Bs = (A\tau + B)s + A$$

Equating coefficients of $s^{0}$: $A = K$.

Equating coefficients of $s^{1}$: $A\tau + B = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; B = -K\tau$.

### Step 5 — Substitute $A$ and $B$ back

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K\tau}{\tau s + 1}$$

Rewrite the second term by dividing numerator and denominator by $\tau$:

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K}{s + 1/\tau}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the inverse Laplace transform using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s}\right\}=1$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\}=e^{-at}$

$$y(t) = K - K e^{-t/\tau}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{y(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \qquad t \geq 0} \qquad \text{...(D1)}$$

**Key value points:** $y(0) = 0$, $y(\tau) = 0.632 K$, $y(4\tau) = 0.982 K$, $y(\infty) = K$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Poles of a Second-Order System from the Characteristic Equation

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form pole expression $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the characteristic polynomial

Set denominator to zero: $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$.

This is a quadratic in $s$ of the form $as^{2} + bs + c = 0$ with $a=1$, $b=2\zeta\omega_{n}$, $c=\omega_{n}^{2}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the quadratic formula

$$s = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a} = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \sqrt{(2\zeta\omega_{n})^{2} - 4\omega_{n}^{2}}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the discriminant

$$(2\zeta\omega_{n})^{2} - 4\omega_{n}^{2} = 4\zeta^{2}\omega_{n}^{2} - 4\omega_{n}^{2} = 4\omega_{n}^{2}(\zeta^{2} - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_{n} \pm 2\omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2} - 1}}{2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2} - 1}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2} - 1}} \qquad \text{...(D2)}$$

### Step 4 — Branch analysis: underdamped case ($0 < \zeta < 1$)

When $\zeta^{2} - 1 < 0$, $\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1} = j\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$, and:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\,\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm j\omega_{d}$$

Poles are **complex conjugates** in the LHP ⇒ stable underdamped response.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peak Overshoot of an Underdamped Second-Order System

**Given:** $y(t) = K\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t} \sin(\omega_{d} t + \phi)\right]$ and $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$.

**Goal:** Show $M_{p} = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\%$.

### Step 1 — Find the time of the first peak

The peak occurs when $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0$. Computing the derivative of $y(t)$ and setting it to zero reduces (after algebraic simplification using trigonometric identities) to:

$$\sin(\omega_{d} t_{p}) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{d} t_{p} = \pi \;\Rightarrow\; t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate $y(t)$ at $t = t_{p}$

At $\omega_{d} t_{p} = \pi$: $\sin(\pi + \phi) = -\sin(\phi) = -\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ (since $\cos\phi = \zeta \Rightarrow \sin\phi = \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$).

Substitute into $y(t)$:

$$y(t_{p}) = K\left[1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}} \cdot \big(-\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\big)\right] = K\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Express $y(t_{p})$ in terms of the steady-state value $K$

$$M_{p} = \frac{y(t_{p}) - y(\infty)}{y(\infty)} = \frac{K\big(1 + e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}}\big) - K}{K} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} t_{p}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $t_{p} = \pi/\omega_{d} = \pi/(\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}})$

$$M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n} \cdot \pi/(\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}})} = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{M_{p} = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}} \qquad \text{...(D3)}$$

Expressed as a percentage: $M_{p}\% = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\%$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Negative Feedback System

**Given block diagram:**

$$R(s) \;\longrightarrow\; \bigotimes \;\overset{E(s)}{\longrightarrow}\; \boxed{G(s)} \;\overset{Y(s)}{\longrightarrow}$$
$$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\;\;\Uparrow$$
$$\quad\quad\quad H(s) \;\longleftarrow\;\text{feedback tap}$$

The summing junction implements $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)$.

### Step 1 — Write the forward-path equation

$$Y(s) = G(s) E(s) = G(s)\big[R(s) - H(s) Y(s)\big]$$

### Step 2 — Expand and group $Y(s)$ terms

$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$$

$$Y(s) + G(s) H(s) Y(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

$$Y(s) \big[1 + G(s) H(s)\big] = G(s) R(s)$$

### Step 3 — Isolate $Y(s)/R(s)$

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}} \qquad \text{...(D4)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of $1 + GH$ in the denominator:** This is the *characteristic polynomial* of the closed loop. Its roots are the closed-loop poles. Stability depends entirely on where these roots sit in the $s$-plane. The denominator is sometimes called the **return difference**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Equivalent Transfer Function of a Series (Cascade) Connection

**Given:** $G_{1}(s)$ and $G_{2}(s)$ connected in cascade: $U(s) \to G_{1} \to V(s) \to G_{2} \to Y(s)$.

### Step 1 — Write the individual block equations

$$V(s) = G_{1}(s) U(s)$$
$$Y(s) = G_{2}(s) V(s)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the first into the second

$$Y(s) = G_{2}(s) \cdot G_{1}(s) U(s) = \big[G_{1}(s) G_{2}(s)\big] U(s)$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{G_{eq}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = G_{1}(s) \, G_{2}(s)} \qquad \text{...(D5)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat — Loading Effect:** In real circuits (or coupled mechanical systems), $G_{2}$ draws energy from $G_{1}$. If the input impedance of $G_{2}$ is *not infinite*, the output of $G_{1}$ changes once $G_{2}$ is connected. This is called **loading**. In such cases, $G_{eq} \neq G_{1} G_{2}$; the engineer must use *two-port network analysis* (e.g., $ABCD$ parameters) or re-derive the overall dynamics from scratch.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Closed-Loop Poles When Forward Path is a First-Order System with Unity Feedback

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$, $H(s) = 1$.

### Step 1 — Apply the closed-loop formula

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K/(\tau s + 1)}{1 + K/(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{K}{(\tau s + 1) + K} = \frac{K}{\tau s + (1 + K)}$$

### Step 2 — Re-write in standard first-order form

Divide numerator and denominator by $(1+K)$:

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K/(1+K)}{\tau s/(1+K) + 1}$$

Define the new gain $K_{CL} = \dfrac{K}{1+K}$ and the new time constant $\tau_{CL} = \dfrac{\tau}{1+K}$.

### Final Result

$$\boxed{G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_{CL}}{\tau_{CL} s + 1}, \quad K_{CL}=\frac{K}{1+K}, \quad \tau_{CL}=\frac{\tau}{1+K}} \qquad \text{...(D6)}$$

**Physical Insight:** Closing a unity feedback loop around a first-order plant *reduces the time constant by a factor of $(1+K)$*, making the system *faster*, but *reduces the DC gain* from $K$ to $K/(1+K)$. This is the classical **gain–bandwidth trade-off** in feedback design.

---

## 3.7 Worked Analytical Example — Putting It All Together

**Problem (KTU-style):** A mechatronic position-control system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+5)}$. A unity negative feedback loop is closed around it ($H(s)=1$).

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function.

**(b)** Identify whether the closed loop is first- or second-order.

**(c)** Compute $\omega_{n}$, $\zeta$, $M_{p}$, $t_{p}$, and $t_{s}$ (2% band).

### Solution

**(a) Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{10/[s(s+5)]}{1 + 10/[s(s+5)]} = \frac{10}{s(s+5) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^{2} + 5s + 10}$$

**(b) Order identification:** The denominator is a 2nd-degree polynomial ⇒ **second-order** system.

**(c) Performance indices:**

Compare denominator with $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}$:

- $\omega_{n}^{2} = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{n} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$
- $2\zeta\omega_{n} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; \zeta = \dfrac{5}{2 \times 3.162} = \dfrac{5}{6.324} \approx 0.791$

Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} = 3.162 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.6256} = 3.162 \times 0.6117 \approx 1.934 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$M_{p} = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} = e^{-\pi \times 0.791 / 0.6117} = e^{-4.064} \approx 0.0172 = 1.72\%$$

$$t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{\pi}{1.934} \approx 1.624 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{s} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = \frac{4}{0.791 \times 3.162} = \frac{4}{2.501} \approx 1.599 \text{ s}$$

### Final Numerical Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $\omega_{n}$ | **3.162 rad/s** |
| $\zeta$ | **0.791** (underdamped) |
| $\omega_{d}$ | 1.934 rad/s |
| $M_{p}$ | **1.72%** |
| $t_{p}$ | **1.624 s** |
| $t_{s}$ (2%) | **1.599 s** |

This is a **well-damped, fast, and stable** mechatronic position controller — exactly the design goal for a precision pick-and-place robot joint.
