## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Simplified DC Motor Transfer Function

This is the single most common derivation in KTU mechatronics papers. We will derive it from first principles with **every algebraic step explicit**.

**Step 1 — Electrical (armature) equation.** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature circuit (resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, applied voltage $V_a$, back-EMF $e_b$):

$$V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF relationship.** The back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity $\omega$:

$$e_b = K_b \, \omega(t)$$

where $K_b$ is the back-EMF constant in **V·s/rad**.

**Step 3 — Mechanical (rotor) equation.** Newton's second law for rotation, with $T$ = motor torque, $J$ = rotor inertia, $B$ = viscous friction coefficient:

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \, \omega = T - T_L$$

where $T_L$ is the load torque. For an unloaded motor, $T_L = 0$.

**Step 4 — Torque-current relationship.** DC motor torque is proportional to armature current:

$$T = K_t \, i_a$$

where $K_t$ is the torque constant in **N·m/A**.

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace transform of all three equations.** Using zero initial conditions:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \, I_a(s) + K_b \, \omega(s)$$

$$(J s + B) \, \omega(s) = K_t \, I_a(s)$$

**Step 6 — Solve the mechanical equation for $I_a(s)$:**

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t} \, \omega(s)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $I_a(s)$ into the electrical equation:**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t} \, \omega(s) + K_b \, \omega(s)$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $\omega(s)$:**

$$V_a(s) = \omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

**Step 9 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $K_t$:**

$$V_a(s) = \omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}{K_t} \right]$$

**Step 10 — Invert to get the transfer function $\omega(s)/V_a(s)$:**

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$

This is the **complete first-order form** (after expansion it is second order, but with one dominant pole).

**Step 11 — Apply the standard simplification** $L_a \approx 0$ (armature inductance is small at low speeds):

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{R_a(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$

**Step 12 — Rearrange into canonical first-order form** $K_m / (\tau_m s + 1)$:

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / (K_b K_t + R_a B)}{1 + \left( \frac{R_a J}{K_b K_t + R_a B} \right) s}$$

Therefore the **motor gain** and **motor time constant** are:

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{K_b K_t + R_a B} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{K_b K_t + R_a B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for the exam:** A DC motor behaves, to a very good approximation, as a **first-order system** with electrical-mechanical time constant $\tau_m$ and steady-state gain $K_m$. Embedding this in a PID loop gives closed-loop position/speed control — the heart of practically every KTU case study.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Error of a Unity-Feedback Position Control Loop

Consider a position-control problem with plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ (a motor driving an inertial load, with position as output). The controller is proportional: $G_c(s) = K_p$.

**Step 1 — Write the open-loop transfer function:**

$$G(s) = G_c(s) G_p(s) = \frac{K_p K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

This is a **Type-1 system** (one pole at the origin).

**Step 2 — For a unit-step input** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$, the steady-state error is given by the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R(s) = 1/s$:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + \frac{K_p K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator at $s \to 0$:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{K_p K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}}$$

**Step 5 — As $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{K_p K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)} \to \infty$:**

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** A Type-1 system with a proportional controller has **zero steady-state error to a step input** — this is why almost every position-control case study in KTU uses a PI or PID (Type-1 or higher), not a pure P controller.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the Six-Step UMAF to Four Canonical Case Studies

| Case Study | Step 1: Objective | Step 2: Variables | Step 3: Sensors | Step 4: Controller | Step 5: Actuator & Driver | Step 6: Control Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Pick-and-Place Robot with Conveyor** | Transfer parts from one conveyor to another with $\pm 0.5$ mm accuracy | Position, velocity of gripper; part presence | Inductive proximity (part detect); optical encoder (joint angle); limit switches | Microcontroller (ARM Cortex) or PLC | Servo / stepper motors + PWM driver / H-bridge | PID on joint angle; sequential logic for pick/place |
| **B. Autonomous Mobile Robot (Warehouse AGV)** | Navigate from A to B avoiding obstacles | Linear/angular position, distance to obstacle, heading | IR / ultrasonic / LIDAR; wheel encoders; IMU; GPS (outdoor) | Embedded Linux / STM32 with RTOS | BLDC wheel motors + VFD / H-bridge; steering servo | Cascaded (outer position loop + inner velocity loop) with obstacle-avoidance algorithm |
| **C. Automatic Door Opener** | Open the door when a person approaches; close after a delay | Presence of person, door angle | PIR motion sensor / IR proximity; limit switches at fully-open / fully-closed | 8-bit microcontroller (8051 / PIC) | DC gear motor with H-bridge; or linear actuator | ON-OFF control + timer; safety interlock |
| **D. CNC Milling Machine** | Cut a profile from a CAD file with $\pm 10 \mu$m accuracy | X, Y, Z position; spindle speed; tool wear; cutting force | Linear optical encoders; tachogenerator; force/strain gauge | Industrial PC + DSP motion controller | BLDC / servo motors on each axis + VFD spindle drive | Cascade (position–velocity–current) with feed-forward |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Closed-Loop Analysis of the Automatic Door

**Plant model (door angular dynamics):**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

With a proportional controller $G_c(s) = K_p$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{\theta_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_p K_m / \tau_m}{s^2 + \frac{1}{\tau_m} s + \frac{K_p K_m}{\tau_m}}$$

**Comparing to the standard second-order form** $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{1}{\tau_m} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{K_p K_m}{\tau_m}$$

**Solving:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_p K_m}{\tau_m}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{1}{2 \tau_m \omega_n} = \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{K_p K_m \tau_m}}$$

**Settling time** (2%):

$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = 8 \tau_m$$

**% Overshoot** for a typical $\zeta = 0.7$:

$$M_p = 100 \, e^{-0.7 \pi / \sqrt{1 - 0.49}} \approx 4.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's favourite application:** If the question gives a desired $t_s$ and $M_p$, you can *back-calculate* the required $K_p$ and the motor time constant, then verify with the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion.

### 3.5 Safety, Reliability, and Interfacing Notes (High Marks Content)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond the math, the KTU examiner often awards marks for **engineering judgement**:
> - Add **limit switches** at end-of-travel to prevent mechanical damage.
> - Add **watchdog timer** in the microcontroller to reset on software lock-up.
> - Use **optocoupler isolation** between the high-voltage motor driver and the low-voltage controller.
> - Use **flyback diodes** across inductive loads.
> - Use **emergency stop** hardware (not just software).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Sense-Decide-Act Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PHYSICAL PLANT / PROCESS"] -- measurable variable --> node2["SENSOR AND SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
    node2 -- analog or digital signal --> node3["CONTROLLER / MICROCONTROLLER / PLC"]
    node3 -- control algorithm executes --> node4["ACTUATOR DRIVER / POWER ELECTRONICS"]
    node4 -- mechanical motion or energy --> node5["ACTUATOR / MOTOR / SOLENOID"]
    node5 -- modifies plant state --> node1
    node3 -- feedback setpoint reference --> nodeRef["SETPOINT INPUT FROM USER / HMI"]
    nodeRef -- desired value --> node3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCAS1["OUTER POSITION LOOP SLOW"] -- position error --> nodeCAS2["VELOCITY SETPOINT"]
    nodeCAS2 -- velocity setpoint --> nodeCAS3["INNER VELOCITY LOOP FAST"]
    nodeCAS3 -- current setpoint --> nodeCAS4["INNER CURRENT LOOP VERY FAST"]
    nodeCAS4 -- PWM duty --> nodeCAS5["MOTOR DRIVER"]
    nodeCAS5 -- voltage --> nodeCAS6["DC OR BLDC MOTOR"]
    nodeCAS6 -- motion --> nodeCAS7["MECHANICAL LOAD"]
    nodeCAS7 -- position --> nodeCAS8["ENCODER"]
    nodeCAS7 -- velocity --> nodeCAS9["TACHOGENERATOR"]
    nodeCAS7 -- current --> nodeCAS10["CURRENT SHUNT"]
    nodeCAS8 -- position feedback --> nodeCAS1
    nodeCAS9 -- velocity feedback --> nodeCAS3
    nodeCAS10 -- current feedback --> nodeCAS4
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read this before writing the exam!):**
> - **Forgetting the feedback path** in the block diagram → KTU examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** for an "open-loop" diagram that is supposed to be closed-loop.
> - **Confusing sensor vs. transducer terminology** → a sensor is the *whole measurement chain*; the *sensing element* is only the part that contacts the physical variable.
> - **Stating the control strategy without justifying it** → "PID is used" is *not* enough; write *why* PID, e.g., "PID is used to achieve zero steady-state error to a step input while keeping overshoot below 5%."
> - **Using SI units inconsistently** → write $V$ for voltage, $m/s$ for velocity, $rad/s$ for angular velocity, $Hz$ for frequency. Mixed units lose marks.
> - **Skipping the safety / interlock discussion** → KTU 2024 OBE pattern awards 2 marks for **safety and reliability** content in any case-study answer.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
*List any **four sensors** and **four actuators** that you would use in a pick-and-place robotic arm used in a small-scale manufacturing line. Justify each choice in one line.*

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor 1** | Optical / incremental rotary encoder on each joint | Provides real-time angular position for closed-loop control |
| **Sensor 2** | Inductive proximity sensor at the pick-up station | Detects ferrous part presence without contact |
| **Sensor 3** | Force / strain gauge on the gripper | Prevents crushing fragile parts |
| **Sensor 4** | Vision camera (CMOS) at the end-effector | Enables correction of part-position offset |
| **Actuator 1** | DC servo motor on each joint | High torque, accurate velocity and position control |
| **Actuator 2** | Stepper motor on auxiliary axes | Open-loop positioning, no encoder needed |
| **Actuator 3** | Pneumatic cylinder for the gripper | Fast, simple ON/OFF, high gripping force |
| **Actuator 4** | Solenoid valve for pneumatic flow | Interface between low-power controller and air supply |

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Explain the role of the **microcontroller** in an automatic washing machine, identifying at least **three input signals** it must read and **three output signals** it must generate.*

**Model Answer:**

The microcontroller is the **central decision-making unit** of the automatic washing machine.

**Input signals it must read (sensor outputs):**
- **Water-level sensor** (pressure transducer) → tells the controller when the drum is full.
- **Temperature sensor** (NTC thermistor) → enables the controller to regulate heater ON-time.
- **Door-lid interlock switch** → a safety input that prevents spin cycle if the door is open.
- *(Optional bonus)* **Turbidity sensor** → enables fuzzy-logic detergent dosing in premium machines.

**Output signals it must generate (actuator commands):**
- **Drum motor driver (relay / triac)** → to run the drum forward, reverse, or spin.
- **Water-inlet solenoid valve** → to admit water from the tap.
- **Heater relay** (often a triac) → to heat the water to the set temperature.
- *(Optional bonus)* **Drain pump relay** and **buzzer / display** for user feedback.

> The controller runs a **state-machine algorithm**: Fill → Wash → Drain → Rinse → Spin. Timers and sensor thresholds govern the transitions.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU ESE 2024 Pattern: Each Part-B question has internal choice. Attempt **either (a) OR (b)**, full marks = 14 (typically two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

#### **QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** *With a neat **functional block diagram**, explain the operation of an **autonomous mobile robot (AMR) used in a warehouse** for goods transportation. Identify all sensors, the controller, and the actuators. Discuss the **cascaded control strategy** used for navigation. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

A warehouse Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR) is a self-guided wheeled vehicle that transports goods from a pick-up bay to a drop-off bay while avoiding dynamic obstacles.

**Functional Block Diagram (already given in Section 4.3 above).** The student should redraw and label:

- **Sense layer:**
  - **Wheel encoders** (quadrature, 1000 PPR) on each drive wheel — measure linear displacement of each wheel.
  - **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — MEMS gyroscope + accelerometer giving tilt, roll, yaw rate.
  - **LIDAR scanner** (2-D, 10 Hz, 25 m range) — gives 360° obstacle map.
  - **Ultrasonic sensors** (4-corner bumpers) for short-range redundancy.

- **Decide layer (Controller):**
  - An **ARM Cortex-M4 / STM32** microcontroller with a real-time operating system (FreeRTOS) running the **sense-decide-act loop at 100 Hz**.
  - Path planning uses the **A\*** or **D\*** algorithm on a pre-built occupancy grid.

- **Act layer:**
  - Two **BLDC hub motors** (one per wheel) with integrated gearboxes.
  - **H-bridge / VFD drivers** that take PWM and direction bits from the controller.
  - A **steering servo** if the design is car-like Ackermann steering.

**Cascaded Control Strategy (full marks require this):**

The motor control is implemented as a **three-loop cascade**:

1. **Outer loop — Position controller** (slow, ~10 Hz): Computes a desired linear velocity $v_d$ and angular velocity $\omega_d$ from the path planner.
2. **Middle loop — Velocity controller** (medium, ~100 Hz): Compares $v_d$ to the encoder-derived actual velocity $v_a$ and outputs a desired current $i_d$.
3. **Inner loop — Current (torque) controller** (fast, ~10 kHz): Compares $i_d$ to the actual phase current measured by a shunt and outputs the PWM duty cycle to the inverter.

**Cascade Justification:** Each inner loop is **an order of magnitude faster** than the outer loop, giving disturbance rejection at every level. If a wheel slips, the velocity loop corrects it *before* the position loop notices.

**Obstacle Avoidance:** The LIDAR feed is processed by a **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** algorithm that produces a steering correction $\Delta \theta$ added to $\omega_d$.

**Safety:** A hardware **e-stop** loop, bumper switches, and a watchdog timer.

**(b)** *For the BLDC motor driving the left wheel, derive the simplified **transfer function** relating motor angular velocity $\omega(s)$ to applied voltage $V_a(s)$. Given $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$, $L_a \approx 0$, $K_t = 0.05 \, N \cdot m/A$, $K_b = 0.05 \, V \cdot s/rad$, $J = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, kg \cdot m^2$, $B = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, N \cdot m \cdot s/rad$, compute the **motor gain $K_m$** and **time constant $\tau_m$**. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.1 above:

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$

Substituting $L_a = 0$:

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{R_a J s + R_a B + K_b K_t}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

- $R_a J = (1)(1 \times 10^{-4}) = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega \cdot kg \cdot m^2$
- $R_a B = (1)(1 \times 10^{-4}) = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega \cdot N \cdot m \cdot s/rad$
- $K_b K_t = (0.05)(0.05) = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, V \cdot s/rad \cdot N \cdot m/A$

**Time constant $\tau_m$** (denominator coefficient of $s$ divided by constant term):

$$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_t} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-4} + 2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{2.6 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0385 \text{ s}$$

**Motor gain $K_m$** (numerator divided by constant term of denominator):

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t} = \frac{0.05}{2.6 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 19.23 \text{ rad/(s·V)}$$

**Final transfer function:**

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{19.23}{0.0385 \, s + 1} \quad \text{(units: rad/s per V)}$$

> **Comment for full marks:** The motor is a first-order system with a settling time $t_s \approx 4 \tau_m = 0.154$ s. This rapid response is essential for the inner velocity loop of the cascade.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Internal Choice Alternative). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** *Explain the operation of an **automatic door opening system** used in shopping malls. Draw the **block diagram**, list the sensors, controller, and actuator, and discuss the **control strategy** including safety interlocks. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

An automatic sliding door in a shopping mall must:
- **Open** within 1–2 seconds when a person approaches.
- **Stay open** while the doorway is occupied.
- **Close** 2–5 seconds after the doorway is clear.
- **Reverse** immediately if a person is detected during closing.

**Sensors used:**
- **PIR motion sensor** (or microwave Doppler sensor) on both sides of the door — detects approach.
- **IR photobeam** (through-beam) across the doorway — detects presence *during* closing.
- **Limit switches** at the **fully-open** and **fully-closed** positions.

**Controller:**
- An **8-bit microcontroller** (e.g., 8051 or PIC16F877A) running a state machine with states `CLOSED`, `OPENING`, `OPEN`, `CLOSING`, `OBSTRUCTION`.

**Actuator & Driver:**
- **DC gear motor** (24 V, 60 W) coupled to the door through a rack-and-pinion or a belt.
- **H-bridge driver** (e.g., L298N or BTS7960) accepts logic-level direction and PWM from the controller.

**Block Diagram** (reproduce Section 4.4 in your answer script).

**Control strategy — state machine:**

| Current State | Trigger | Next State | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| CLOSED | PIR = HIGH | OPENING | Motor forward, PWM = 80% |
| OPENING | Limit switch OPEN = HIGH | OPEN | Motor stop, start 5 s timer |
| OPEN | Timer elapsed AND IR beam unbroken | CLOSING | Motor reverse, PWM = 60% |
| CLOSING | IR beam broken | OPENING | Motor forward again |
| CLOSING | Limit switch CLOSED = HIGH | CLOSED | Motor stop |

**Safety interlocks:**
- **Hardware e-stop** that cuts motor power directly.
- **IR photobeam** has *higher* priority than the timer.
- **Motor current sensing** detects a stalled door (person stuck) and reverses.
- **Battery backup** keeps the door functional during power outage.

**(b)** *For the door, the motor–load transfer function is $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{10}{s(0.1 s + 1)}$, where $\theta$ is the door angle in radians and $V_a$ is in volts. A proportional controller of gain $K_p$ is used in unity feedback. Find the value of $K_p$ that gives a **damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$**, and compute the resulting **settling time** and **% overshoot**. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Open-loop TF:**

$$G(s) = K_p \cdot \frac{10}{s(0.1 s + 1)} = \frac{10 K_p}{s(0.1 s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop TF** (unity feedback):

$$T(s) = \frac{10 K_p / 0.1}{s^2 + \frac{1}{0.1} s + \frac{10 K_p}{0.1}} = \frac{100 K_p}{s^2 + 10 s + 100 K_p}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standard form $s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:**

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = 10 \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_n^2 = 100 K_p$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\omega_n$ using $\zeta = 0.7$:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{10}{2 \times 0.7} = \frac{10}{1.4} \approx 7.143 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $K_p$:**

$$K_p = \frac{\omega_n^2}{100} = \frac{(7.143)^2}{100} = \frac{51.02}{100} \approx 0.51$$

**Step 6 — Settling time (2%):**

$$t_s = \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.7 \times 7.143} = \frac{4}{5.0} = 0.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7 — % Overshoot:**

$$M_p = 100 \, e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = 100 \, e^{-0.7 \pi / \sqrt{1 - 0.49}}$$

$$M_p = 100 \, e^{-0.7 \pi / 0.714} = 100 \, e^{-3.08} \approx 4.6\%$$

> **Conclusion:** With $K_p \approx 0.51$, the door reaches its open position in under 0.8 s with less than 5% overshoot — a typical design specification for an automatic door.

> **Print this checklist. Read it twice before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Definition to memorize:** Mechatronics = *integration* of mechanical + electronic + computer + control.
- [x] **UMAF six steps:** Objective → Variables → Sensors → Controller → Actuator & Driver → Control Strategy.
- [x] **Sense–Decide–Act loop** is the universal skeleton of every case study.
- [x] **PID controller equation:** $G_c(s) = K_p + K_d s + K_i / s$. **Closed-loop TF:** $T(s) = G_c G_p / (1 + G_c G_p)$.
- [x] **DC motor simplified TF:** $\omega(s)/V_a(s) = K_m / (\tau_m s + 1)$. With load inertia, becomes $K_m / [s(\tau_m s + 1)]$ for position.
- [x] **Damping ratio and natural frequency** determine $t_s$, $M_p$, and $t_p$.
- [x] **Type-1 system with P-controller gives zero steady-state error to a step input.**
- [x] **Sensors common to case studies:** encoder, IR proximity, PIR, ultrasonic, LIDAR, IMU, thermistor, strain gauge, Hall sensor, photodiode, limit switch.
- [x] **Actuators common to case studies:** DC motor, stepper, servo, BLDC, solenoid, pneumatic cylinder, piezoelectric, voice coil.
- [x] **Controllers common to case studies:** 8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex, Arduino, PLC, DSP motion controller, industrial PC.
- [x] **Drivers / Power electronics:** H-bridge (L298N, BTS7960), VFD, relay, triac, optocoupler isolation.
- [x] **Cascade control** = position (outer, slow) → velocity (middle) → current (inner, fast). Always justify *why* cascade.
- [x] **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — design with a safety factor of 5 to 10.
- [x] **Safety & reliability points to mention:** e-stop, limit switches, optocoupler isolation, flyback diode, watchdog timer, battery backup, current-limit sensing.
- [x] **Interfaces to mention:** PWM, UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, RS-485, Modbus (industrial), Ethernet/IP.
- [x] **Always draw a closed-loop block diagram** in your answer. Open-loop diagrams of closed-loop problems lose marks.
- [x] **Always end with a justification sentence** — why this sensor, why this actuator, why this control law.
- [x] **SI units everywhere:** $m$, $m/s$, $rad/s$, $V$, $A$, $Hz$, $s$, $N$, $N \cdot m$, $kg$, $kg \cdot m^2$, $W$.
